statCheck.fail: I'm just going by feel. I can't point to any one thing.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is always warmer than the air around it. You can feel the heat from the doorway.
details: In winter it's the most popular threshold in town. People linger, pretending they need to talk to someone inside.
In summer it's the opposite: everyone gives it a wide berth.
reactions: A building that makes its own heat is a blessing half the year and a curse the other half.

statCheck.pass: The heat comes from the process inside. Continuous operation means the walls never cool fully: the stone acts as a thermal battery.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand why it's always warm. Something about the work, I suppose.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} got a fresh coat of limewash last week. Brightest thing in the settlement now.
details: It almost hurts to look at in full sun. White as fresh snow.
Give it a month and it'll mellow. But for now it's blinding.
reactions: At least someone's keeping up appearances.

entries.intro: The drain near the {poi_type} backs up every time it rains hard. Smells like standing water for days.
details: The grate is always clogged with debris. I've never once seen anyone clear it.
After a heavy storm the whole area smells like a swamp until it drains on its own.
reactions: You'd think after the tenth time, someone would fix the drain. But here we are.

entries.intro: I can tell what day of the week it is by the sounds from the {poi_type}. Each day has its own pattern.
details: Heavy work early in the week, sorting and cleaning by the end. The rhythm never changes.
I mentioned it to someone who works there and they didn't even realize they followed a pattern.
reactions: Habits form without you noticing. That's the nature of routine.

statCheck.pass: The pattern follows material flow: heavy raw stock at the start of the week, finishing and packaging at the end. The process dictates the rhythm.
statCheck.fail: I haven't thought about why each day sounds different. It just does.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the one place in town where nobody cares about gossip. Just the work.
details: Walk in and the conversation is all about output, quality, what needs doing next.
It's refreshing. No politics, no grudges, just practical talk.
reactions: I wish more of the settlement ran like that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s door swells in the wet season. Takes two hands and a shoulder to get it open.
details: Every year someone says they'll shave it down, and every year it dries out before they bother.
The workers have a system: one pushes, one pulls the handle. Looks like a comedy routine.
reactions: Some repairs never happen because the problem solves itself just slowly enough.

statCheck.pass: The door absorbs moisture through the end grain at the bottom. A coat of wax on the cut edge would seal it and stop the swelling entirely.
statCheck.fail: I don't know woodwork. Doors swell, that's all I know.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} was the first building I could point to and name when I arrived here. It anchored the whole settlement in my mind.
details: Everything else took weeks to learn: streets, houses, which path goes where. The {poi_type} was instant.
I still give directions using it as a reference point.
reactions: A good landmark makes a stranger feel less lost.

entries.intro: The ground around the {poi_type} is littered with bits of {resource_type}. Small pieces, nothing usable.
details: Offcuts and fragments that get kicked around and ground into the dirt.
Over time it's changed the color of the soil there: darker, grittier.
reactions: A place takes on the character of its work. Even the ground tells you what happens inside.

entries.intro: I owe the {poi_type} a debt, honestly. The work that comes out of there keeps this place running.
details: My livelihood depends on it, even though I don't work there directly.
Most people here could say the same if they thought about it.
reactions: It's easy to overlook the foundation under your feet.

entries.intro: The windows at the {poi_type} are so grimy you can barely see in. Layers of soot and dust.
details: I don't think they've been cleaned since they were put in. The glass has that yellowish film.
The workers probably don't even notice anymore. You stop seeing dirt you live with every day.
reactions: Not my building, not my windows. But still.

statCheck.pass: The soot is from the interior fires: it deposits on the cold glass and bakes on over time. Vinegar and ash would cut through it, but nobody's bothered.
statCheck.fail: No idea what causes the buildup. It's just grimy.

entries.intro: I like how the {poi_type} changes with the seasons. Same building, but it feels different in every weather.
details: Snow softens it, rain darkens it. In full summer sun it looks hard and proud.
I've lived here long enough to see it through every possible condition.
reactions: Familiar things that still surprise you are worth keeping around.

entries.intro: Spiders have taken over the upper corners of the {poi_type}. Webs everywhere you look up.
details: Some of the webs are impressively large. You'd think someone would clear them, but the spiders keep the flies down.
I mentioned it and the workers shrugged. Said the spiders earn their keep.
reactions: I suppose every building has its unofficial tenants.

statCheck.pass: Spiders go where the flies are, and flies go where organic waste is. The spider population tells you how much food waste the {poi_type} is producing.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why there are so many. Spiders just like old buildings, I think.

entries.intro: The gravel path to the {poi_type} crunches louder than any other path in town. You can hear people coming from far off.
details: It's loose and coarse: more stone chips than proper gravel. Every step sounds like you're crushing something.
Nobody sneaks up on the {poi_type}. The path announces everyone.
reactions: Best security system in the settlement and it wasn't even intentional.

statCheck.pass: That gravel is crushed furnace slag, not quarried stone. It's harder and more angular, which is why it crunches so loudly and drains well too.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what kind of gravel it is. It's just louder than other paths.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is where people gather after bad news. I don't know why: habit, probably.
details: Someone hears something, and within an hour there's a knot of people outside the {poi_type} talking it over.
It's never planned. People just end up there.
reactions: I suppose shared worry is lighter than the kind you carry alone.

statCheck.pass: It's the location: central enough that everyone passes it, but set back enough that you can stand without blocking the road. A natural assembly point.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why people end up there. Habit, probably.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} from the {poi_type} has a good weight to it this season. Dense, solid stuff.
details: You can feel the quality just by holding it. Heft means the material is sound.
People are pleased. Good {resource_type} makes every downstream job easier.
reactions: When the basics are right, everything else follows.

statCheck.pass: Cooler nights during the processing period slow the curing, and slower curing means denser material: less air, more substance. Perfect conditions this year.
statCheck.fail: I couldn't tell you why it's heavier. It just is.

entries.intro: I keep finding bits of straw blown from the {poi_type} stuck in my fence. Every single morning.
details: Wind catches it off the work area and carries it across the road. My yard looks like a barn floor.
I've swept up enough straw this month to stuff a mattress.
reactions: Small annoyances add up. I'm not angry, just consistently mildly irritated.

entries.intro: The stonework at the {poi_type} has a nice color in the rain: dark grey with streaks of rust.
details: The iron in the stone comes out when it's wet. Gives the whole face a warm undertone.
I noticed it one rainy afternoon and now I look for it every time the weather turns.
reactions: Most people walk past without noticing. Their loss.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a slow start this season. Took them two weeks longer than usual to get up to full production.
details: People were getting antsy, asking when things would pick up. Nobody had answers.
It's running fine now, but those first weeks were tense.
reactions: A slow start isn't a disaster. Patience is just hard when you're the one waiting.

statCheck.pass: The late thaw threw everything off. You can't start certain processes until the ground temperature is right, and this year it lagged by almost a fortnight.
statCheck.fail: I'm not sure what caused the delay. Weather, maybe.

entries.intro: The bell at the {poi_type} rings at shift change. You can hear it all across town.
details: Twice a day, same time: morning start and afternoon end. It sets the pace for the whole settlement.
I've started using it as my own schedule marker. When the bell rings, I know where I should be.
reactions: A shared rhythm keeps people together. Even a simple bell does that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} could stand to have its hinges oiled. Every door in the place screams.
details: Open, close, squeal, all day long. The workers must have gone numb to it.
I spent ten minutes there and left with a headache.
reactions: A bit of grease: that's not asking much.

entries.intro: On clear nights you can see the {poi_type}'s outline against the stars. It cuts a clean shape on the ridge.
details: I've walked home late enough to see it silhouetted against the sky. Big, dark, familiar.
It's a comfort, in a way. Means you're close to home.
reactions: Knowing your landmarks in the dark is how you know you belong somewhere.

entries.intro: The people at the {poi_type} eat lunch together every day. Big group, all sharing.
details: Someone brings bread, someone brings cheese, and they spread it all out on a crate by the door.
There's always laughing. Whatever they talk about during meals, it's not work.
reactions: A crew that eats together works together. I've seen it enough times to believe it.

statCheck.pass: The crew has a rule: no work talk during meals, which is why there's always laughing. It resets the mood and they come back sharper in the afternoon.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what they talk about. Just that they seem to enjoy it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is drafty in the morning and stifling by noon. Worst of both worlds.
details: The stone holds cold overnight and traps heat once the sun's been on it for a few hours.
The workers dress in layers and peel them off as the day goes on.
reactions: Temperature swings like that wear a person out. I'd want a nap by midday.

statCheck.pass: Thermal mass: thick stone walls absorb heat slowly and release it slowly. A few ventilation gaps near the roof would equalize things without losing the winter benefit.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand building science. I just know it's uncomfortable.

entries.intro: I measure the {poi_type}'s health by the scrap pile. Big pile means busy, small pile means slow.
details: Right now the pile is middling: neither a mountain nor bare ground.
It's not scientific, but it's been reliable for years.
reactions: You pick up your own indicators when you live near a workplace long enough.

statCheck.pass: The scrap pile also tells you what they're working on: different material means different offcuts. Right now it's mostly fine shavings, which means detail work.
statCheck.fail: I just look at the size of the pile. I can't read the details.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is well-placed. Gets morning sun and afternoon shade, which keeps the workers comfortable.
details: Whoever chose the spot thought it through. The orientation makes a real difference.
I've been in workshops that face the wrong way: brutal in summer, dark in winter.
reactions: Good placement is one of those decisions you only appreciate years later.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} always has water standing on the windowsills. Condensation, probably.
details: It drips down the inside walls and pools on the sills. Everything near the windows is damp.
I've seen mold starting in the corners. Not much yet, but give it a season.
reactions: Damp is the quiet enemy. It ruins things so slowly you don't notice until it's too late.

statCheck.pass: That's a temperature differential issue: warm air inside, cold glass, moisture condenses. Better air circulation or a dehumidifying material near the windows would fix it.
statCheck.fail: I'm not sure why it happens. Just seems like everything near the windows is always wet.

entries.intro: I like to bring visitors to see the {poi_type}. It's the best example of what we do here.
details: People are always curious about how things work. The {poi_type} tells that story without needing words.
The workers don't mind an audience. I think they appreciate someone noticing.
reactions: Pride in your community starts with showing it off.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s waste water runs off into a ditch that empties into the grass. It's killed the plants closest to the outflow.
details: There's a dead patch spreading slowly each year. Brown and bare where everything else is green.
Nobody's bothered to redirect it. It's the kind of slow damage that doesn't feel urgent.
reactions: If it doesn't fix itself and nobody fixes it, it just gets worse. That's how things go.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has one of those roofs that changes color with the weather. Grey when dry, almost black when wet.
details: I can tell from my window whether it's raining over there before it reaches my side of town.
It's slate, I think. Good material: lasts forever if the nails hold.
reactions: A roof you can read the weather off is a useful thing.

statCheck.pass: That's mica-flecked slate: the mica absorbs water and darkens, then lightens as it dries. Good roofing material, nearly waterproof when laid properly.
statCheck.fail: I don't know roof stone. It just changes color.

entries.intro: I picked up some {resource_type} from the {poi_type} today. The price was fair and the quality was decent.
details: Not their best ever, but reliable. I know what I'm getting and that's worth something.
The person behind the counter didn't try to upsell me either. Just gave me what I asked for.
reactions: Straightforward transactions: my favorite kind.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a puddle of light at the entrance that spills onto the path. Warm and golden.
details: On dark evenings it's the most welcoming sight in the whole settlement.
People gravitate toward light without thinking about it. That doorway draws you in.
reactions: There's a reason every good building has a well-lit entrance.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} threw out a whole stack of spoiled {resource_type} last week. Total loss.
details: Rain got in through somewhere and ruined everything stored on the ground level. Days of work, gone.
The crew looked gutted. You could see it on their faces.
reactions: That stings. You can't un-spoil material.

statCheck.pass: They were storing it too close to the wall on the rain-facing side. Moisture wicks through stone overnight, and anything touching the wall absorbs it.
statCheck.fail: Bad luck, as far as I know. Rain got in somewhere.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is where half the settlement learned their trade. Including me.
details: You start there young, doing the simple jobs, and before you know it you're skilled at something useful.
I still remember the first task they gave me. Terrible work, but nobody laughed.
reactions: A place that teaches you something earns a permanent spot in your memory.

entries.intro: There's a hornets' nest forming under the overhang of the {poi_type}. Nobody's dealt with it yet.
details: It's about the size of a fist now. Give it a few weeks and it'll be trouble.
The workers walk wide around that side. I don't blame them.
reactions: Hornets don't negotiate. Better to handle it while it's small.

entries.intro: I sleep better knowing the {poi_type} is there. Steady work means steady life.
details: When it's producing, people have what they need. When people have what they need, nobody gets desperate.
It's the foundation under everything else: jobs, trade, food on the table.
reactions: Security isn't walls and watchtowers. It's having a functioning {poi_type}.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a loose stone at the corner that wobbles when you step on it. Trips people up.
details: I've watched three different people stumble on the same spot this week alone.
It's been reported, complained about, and ignored in roughly equal measure.
reactions: Someday someone will twist an ankle badly enough for it to get fixed.

entries.intro: I caught the scent of fresh bread drifting from the direction of the {poi_type} this morning. Doesn't usually smell like that.
details: Someone must have brought food for the workers. The whole area smelled wonderful for an hour.
It was gone by midmorning, replaced by the usual work smells.
reactions: Funny how a single good smell can improve your whole walk.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is too important to let slide, and I think people know that. They just can't agree on who should fix what.
details: Every time repairs come up, the same arguments start. Who pays, who does the labor, who decides.
Meanwhile the building doesn't care about politics. It just keeps getting older.
reactions: Buildings deteriorate on schedule. People decide on no schedule at all.

entries.intro: I watched the sunrise reflect off the {poi_type}'s windows this morning. Blinding, but beautiful.
details: For about five minutes the whole face of the building was gold. Like the windows were on fire.
Then the angle shifted and it was just a building again.
reactions: Five minutes of beauty in a whole day. Enough, if you catch it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the noisiest building in the settlement and it's not close.
details: Some days you can hear it from the other end of town. Banging, grinding, the occasional shout.
The neighbors have either made peace with it or gone a bit deaf.
reactions: Noise means work. I keep telling myself that.

statCheck.pass: The noise carries because of the valley shape: sound reflects off the ridge and channels into the settlement. A different location would halve the volume.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand acoustics. It's just loud.

entries.intro: The old bench outside the {poi_type} finally collapsed. Wood rotted right through.
details: It's been there longer than I have. The legs just gave out one afternoon: nobody was sitting on it, thankfully.
Someone stacked the pieces by the road. I don't think anyone plans to replace it.
reactions: I'm going to miss that bench more than I expected.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} gives good shade on the south side. There's always someone sitting there at midday.
details: The wall stays cool even when the sun is fierce. You lean against it and the heat doesn't follow.
I've seen five people share that strip of shade on a hot day, shoulder to shoulder.
reactions: Shared shade makes strangers friendly.

entries.intro: I've noticed the {poi_type} hums. Low and constant, from the work going on inside.
details: You don't hear it unless you're close and the wind is right. A vibration more than a sound.
Once you notice it, it's hard to un-notice. Like a pulse.
reactions: Buildings absorb the energy of what happens inside them. That hum is proof.

statCheck.pass: That hum is resonance: the heavy equipment runs at a frequency that the stone walls amplify. Change the speed and the hum changes pitch.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what causes it. Some kind of vibration from the work, maybe.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is where I go when I want to feel useful. There's always something that needs a hand.
details: Carrying, sorting, holding something steady while someone else works on it. Small tasks that add up.
Nobody asks, but nobody turns you away either.
reactions: Having somewhere to be useful beats sitting at home wondering what to do with the day.

entries.intro: The runoff from the {poi_type} has stained the stones along the path. Yellowish brown, impossible to scrub out.
details: It's been building up over years. Each season adds another layer to the discoloration.
People have stopped trying to clean it. The stain is permanent now.
reactions: Not pretty, but I suppose it's character.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks its best right after a snowfall. Clean lines, white on stone, absolutely still.
details: The snow fills in all the cracks and patches and makes the whole building look brand new.
By afternoon the workers have tramped the snow to slush and the illusion is gone.
reactions: Fresh snow forgives every flaw. If only it lasted.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has been skipping maintenance again. Little things piling up into bigger things.
details: A cracked pane here, a sagging board there. Individually they're nothing, but together they tell a story.
The people who work there are too busy producing to stop and fix the building they produce in.
reactions: Nobody maintains the thing that maintains them. Oldest problem in the world.

statCheck.pass: The maintenance budget gets redirected to production whenever output targets are tight. It's a short-term saving that costs more in the long run.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the budget situation. Things just keep not getting fixed.

entries.intro: There's a patch of warm air near the {poi_type} that hits you as you walk past. Like stepping through an invisible curtain.
details: It's the heat escaping through the walls. Concentrated in one spot where the stone is thinnest.
On cold mornings I slow down when I pass that stretch. Free warmth.
reactions: Small comforts you discover on your daily route are the best kind.

statCheck.pass: The thin spot in the wall is where they patched a former doorway. The fill is thinner than the original stone, so heat conducts through more easily.
statCheck.fail: I have no idea why that one spot is warm. Just is.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a shelf life, like anything. I wonder how many more years it has in it.
details: It's holding up fine for now. But I've seen buildings this age start to go quickly once they turn.
The bones are good, but the surfaces are tired.
reactions: Everything ages. The question is whether anyone invests in keeping it young.

statCheck.pass: Structurally it's got decades left if the roof stays sound. The roof is the key: once water gets into the timber framing, decay accelerates fast.
statCheck.fail: I'm no builder. It just looks like it's getting on.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the loudest it's ever been. I think they took on extra work this month.
details: Earlier starts, later finishes, and more bodies through the door than I've seen in a year.
The pace is impressive, but I can see people wearing thin.
reactions: Busy is good until it turns into exhausted.

statCheck.pass: They've taken on overflow work from {other_settlement}. Their {poi_type} is down for repairs, so the demand shifted here temporarily.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why the uptick. More work, I suppose.

entries.intro: I like that the {poi_type} smells like honest work. Earthy, sharp, real.
details: Walk past any time of day and you know exactly what's happening inside just from the smell.
It's not perfume, but it's genuine. Better than the nothing-smell of an empty building.
reactions: A place with a strong smell is a place where things are getting done.

entries.intro: The woodpile behind the {poi_type} is getting low. Usually it's stacked shoulder-high by now.
details: Either they're burning through it faster or the delivery is late. Nobody's said which.
A thin woodpile before winter is the kind of thing that makes people nervous.
reactions: Fuel is the one thing you don't want to run short on.

statCheck.pass: The usual supplier is backed up. Timber cutting ran behind schedule and everyone who depends on delivered wood is feeling the delay.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the supply chain. I just know the pile is small.

entries.intro: I brought {npc_name} to see the {poi_type} for the first time last week. They weren't impressed.
details: Said it was smaller than they expected. I thought it was perfectly fine.
Expectations are strange. I've been proud of this place for years and someone walks in and shrugs.
reactions: You can't make someone appreciate what they didn't grow up around.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} keeps everything running, even when nobody talks about it. Quiet importance.
details: It's not glamorous and nobody brags about it. But take it away and the settlement stops functioning.
The best things in a community are the ones nobody has to think about because they just work.
reactions: Invisible things that keep life normal: that's what really matters.

entries.intro: There are grooves worn into the threshold of the {poi_type} from wagon wheels. Deep ones.
details: Years of deliveries in the same spot, same angle, same rut. The stone is carved smooth.
I traced them with my foot once: two perfect channels from the door to the road.
reactions: That's what repetition looks like when you carve it into stone.

statCheck.pass: The grooves show the original wheel gauge: wider than what's used now. Those ruts were cut by heavier carts, probably during the building's construction.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the history. They're just old ruts.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} got noisy neighbors this year. Chickens right next to the work area.
details: The rooster starts at dawn, the hens cluck all morning, and the workers compete to be heard over them.
Nobody's sure who moved the chickens there or why. They just appeared.
reactions: Chickens and industry: not the quietest combination.

statCheck.pass: Someone moved the coop to that spot because the {poi_type}'s waste draws grubs. Free chicken feed: clever, actually, even if it's noisy.
statCheck.fail: I've no idea why the chickens ended up there. They just did.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been closed for repairs this week. First time in years.
details: You don't realize how much you rely on it until it's shut. People are wandering around looking lost.
The repairs were overdue, honestly. Better a week of inconvenience than a month of collapse.
reactions: I just want it back open. I'm not good at waiting.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has earned its place. Every mark on it is a year of serving this settlement.
details: Scratches, dents, wear patterns: it's a record of use. A building that looks too clean hasn't been useful enough.
I'd rather have a scarred {poi_type} that works than a pretty one that doesn't.
reactions: Character comes from use, not decoration.

entries.intro: There's a draft that comes off the {poi_type} at ankle level. You feel it walking past: cold air, low to the ground.
details: Must be a gap in the foundation letting interior air escape. It's ice-cold in winter.
I always speed up through that stretch without thinking about it.
reactions: It's funny how your body learns to avoid discomfort before your mind notices.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} just got a new door: heavy timber, iron banding. Looks like it means business.
details: The old one was hanging by one hinge and a prayer. This one could stop a battering ram.
It changed the whole look of the building. One new element and the place seems five years younger.
reactions: Amazing what one repair can do for morale.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is where I take my evening walk most nights. Around it, past it, back home.
details: The route never changes but the building always offers something different. Light, sound, activity.
It's meditative. Twenty minutes and my head's quieter than when I left.
reactions: Everyone needs a route. Mine just happens to loop past the busiest spot in town.

statCheck.pass: I've learned more about the settlement from my evening walks than from any conversation. You see who's working late, who's visiting who, what's changed since yesterday.
statCheck.fail: It's just a walk. I don't pay that much attention.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} was struck by lightning last storm season. Left a scorch mark that's still visible.
details: The building held up fine. Thick timbers don't catch easily, and the rain took care of the rest.
But the black streak down the wall is a reminder. Nature doesn't care about our schedules.
reactions: Good construction versus bad luck: construction won. This time.

statCheck.pass: Lightning follows the path of least resistance. The {poi_type}'s chimney is the highest point in that area, and the metal cap acts as a conductor.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it hit there specifically. Bad luck, I'd guess.

entries.intro: I noticed the {poi_type} has a new flag flying. Someone must have put it up over the weekend.
details: It's nothing official: just a colored cloth snapping in the breeze. But it gives the place a lift.
Small touches like that matter more than people think.
reactions: Somebody cares about that building. You can always tell.

entries.intro: The ground near the {poi_type} vibrates when they're working hard. You can feel it through your boots.
details: It's faint, but unmistakable if you stop and stand still. A pulse from deep in the building.
The first time I felt it I thought something was wrong. Now I just think of it as the building breathing.
reactions: A place with that much energy in it is a place worth having.

statCheck.pass: The vibration comes from the bellows or the main wheel: heavy rotating equipment transfers energy through the foundation into the bedrock.
statCheck.fail: I'm not an engineer. The ground just shakes a little.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s output has been rising steadily. Not dramatically, just a little more each month.
details: It's the kind of growth you don't notice week to week, but over a season the difference is clear.
The workers seem more confident too. A place that's improving breeds optimism.
reactions: Slow and steady wins. I've always believed that.

entries.intro: Someone dropped a barrel of {resource_type} outside the {poi_type} and it split open. Material everywhere.
details: The cleanup took half the morning. People were picking {resource_type} out of the cracks between stones for hours.
The worker who dropped it looked like they wanted the ground to swallow them.
reactions: Accidents happen. I felt bad for laughing.

statCheck.pass: The staves on that barrel were under-hooped: only two iron bands instead of three. Under load the bottom ring slipped and the whole thing burst at the seam.
statCheck.fail: I don't know cooperage. It just broke.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is surrounded by blackberry bushes now. They've crept in from the hedgerow over the years.
details: In late summer the berries are ripe and the workers eat them during breaks. Free snacks.
The thorns are a nuisance, though. Sleeves get caught if you're not careful.
reactions: Nature reclaims what it can. Might as well enjoy the berries while we have them.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a visitor from {other_settlement} last week. Came to see how we do things here.
details: They spent a whole day watching and asking questions. The workers were on their best behavior.
I think they left impressed. At least, that's what {npc_name} told me.
reactions: It's flattering when someone comes to learn from you.

entries.intro: There's a stone marker near the {poi_type} that nobody can read anymore. The letters have worn smooth.
details: It might say who built the place, or when. Nobody alive remembers.
I've traced my fingers over it trying to feel the shapes. Nothing: just smooth rock.
reactions: Some things outlive their own meaning. The stone's still there, even if the words aren't.

statCheck.pass: I ran my fingers over it when the light was right and felt what might be a date. Old numbering, the kind they used before my time.
statCheck.fail: I've tried to read it more than once. The stone isn't talking.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} makes me proud, honestly. Not everyone can say their settlement produces good {resource_type}.
details: I've seen what other places put out. Ours is better, and I don't say that lightly.
It's the workers more than the building. Good people produce good work.
reactions: Pride in where you live isn't arrogance. It's acknowledgment.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been arguing over shift schedules again. I can hear the disagreements from outside.
details: Nobody wants the early shift and everybody wants the midday slot. Same fight, different month.
They'll sort it out, they always do. Just noisily.
reactions: People who work together argue. That's just the price of shared space.

statCheck.pass: Nobody wants the early shift because the building is coldest then, and the afternoon slot is warm with lighter cleanup. It's a comfort issue, not a laziness one.
statCheck.fail: I don't work there, so I don't know the details of their arguments.

entries.intro: I leaned on the fence outside the {poi_type} and it gave way. Rotten right through.
details: I caught myself before I fell, but it was embarrassing. A piece of it is still lying in the mud.
Nobody saw, thankfully. Or if they did, they had the decency not to mention it.
reactions: I'm blaming the fence, not my weight.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} always draws a crowd after market day. People comparing what they bought, trading opinions.
details: It becomes an informal gathering spot: loud, friendly, a bit chaotic.
By evening the crowd thins and the area's littered with dropped wrappers and crumbs.
reactions: It's the closest thing we have to a town square. Unplanned but consistent.

entries.intro: The ivy on the {poi_type} is getting out of hand. It's covering the upper windows now.
details: It grows a hand's width every season. Another year and you won't be able to see the stonework at all.
Some people think it looks charming. The people who have to work in the dark behind it disagree.
reactions: Charm versus function: the eternal argument.

statCheck.pass: Ivy roots into mortar joints and eventually pries the stones apart. It looks pretty, but it's slowly disassembling the wall from the outside in.
statCheck.fail: I don't know if it's harmful or just messy. It's a plant, not my area.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a good month. I know because {npc_name} was buying everyone drinks.
details: When the output's up, the generosity follows. People share more when there's more to share.
It doesn't last, but it's nice while it does.
reactions: A rising tide lifts all moods.

entries.intro: I heard a woodpecker hammering on the {poi_type}'s timbers this morning. Persistent little thing.
details: It was going at it like the wood owed it money. Tap-tap-tap for a solid ten minutes.
The workers came out to shoo it away and it flew to the next beam over.
reactions: Woodpeckers don't pick wood at random. I hope those beams are sound.

statCheck.pass: Woodpeckers target wood with beetle larvae inside, so if the beam has insects, it means the timber is starting to soften. Worth checking before it becomes structural.
statCheck.fail: Maybe it just liked the sound. I don't know bird behavior.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} smells like linseed oil today. Someone's been treating the woodwork.
details: It's a sharp, nutty smell: not unpleasant, just noticeable. Clings to your nose.
Means they're doing maintenance, which is more than can be said for most buildings around here.
reactions: Oil on wood is time on a building's life. Smart investment.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s foundation stones are massive. You wonder how they got them there in the first place.
details: Each one must weigh more than a person. Cut square, fit tight, not a crack between them.
Whoever laid that foundation had resources and patience.
reactions: We build on top of other people's effort. Always have.

statCheck.pass: Those foundation stones are granite, quarried and transported: you can tell by the squared grain. That means this was a planned settlement from the start.
statCheck.fail: I don't know stone types. They're just massive.

entries.intro: A {self_role} like me sees the {poi_type} differently than most. It's not just a building: it's my supply line.
details: When production dips, I feel it in my work within days. Everything's connected.
I keep one eye on their output the way a farmer watches the sky.
reactions: Dependence isn't comfortable, but pretending it doesn't exist is worse.

entries.intro: There's always a thin trail of smoke coming from the {poi_type}, even at night. The embers never fully die.
details: They bank the fires low and let them smolder until morning. Saves fuel and startup time.
On a still night you can see the wisp curling up against the stars.
reactions: A fire that never goes out feels like a promise that work continues.

statCheck.pass: Banking a fire right is a real skill: too much air and it burns out by morning, too little and it suffocates. The person who closes up knows their craft.
statCheck.fail: I couldn't keep a fire going overnight. Takes practice.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the place that makes newcomers say 'oh, you have one of those.' It legitimizes us.
details: Without it, we'd be a cluster of houses with ambitions. With it, we're a proper settlement.
I've seen people's estimation of us change the moment they spot it.
reactions: Infrastructure is credibility. Simple as that.

entries.intro: A tree root has cracked the flagstone outside the {poi_type}. It's been pushing up for years.
details: The stone sits at an angle now: you stub your toe on the raised edge if you're not looking.
It's one of those problems where fixing it means killing the tree, and nobody wants to do that.
reactions: Trees win in the end. They're more patient than we are.

entries.intro: I was at the {poi_type} when the first hard rain of the season hit. You could hear it drumming on the roof like applause.
details: Everyone inside stopped working for a moment and just listened. One of those shared silences.
Then someone laughed and the work started again. But for a few seconds, the rain had everyone's attention.
reactions: Some moments are too small to matter and too perfect to forget.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a cold spot on the floor that nobody can explain. One patch that's always colder than the rest.
details: Workers avoid standing there. It's just uncomfortable: not freezing, but noticeably chill.
Someone said there might be a void underneath. Or maybe the stone is just different there.
reactions: Buildings have their quirks. You learn to live around them.

statCheck.pass: There's probably an old drainage channel or cistern under that spot. Air moving through a void below the floor pulls heat out of the stone above.
statCheck.fail: No idea. Cold is cold.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s output was down this week, but nobody's panicking. It happens.
details: Not every week can be a record. Sometimes the material doesn't cooperate, or the weather slows things down.
The experienced workers barely blink at a slow week. They've seen enough cycles to know it bounces back.
reactions: Patience is the hardest part of depending on anything.

entries.intro: The water trough near the {poi_type} is green with algae. Nobody's scrubbed it in weeks.
details: Animals drink from it, people used to wash their hands in it. Now nobody goes near it.
It's the kind of thing that stays dirty because everyone assumes someone else will clean it.
reactions: Common resources get commonly neglected. That's just human nature.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is built from local stone. Same color as the hillside: grey-brown with flecks of quartz.
details: In certain light the quartz sparkles. Makes the whole wall shimmer like it's embedded with tiny stars.
It's the same stone the rest of the settlement is built from, but the {poi_type} has the best face of it.
reactions: Building with what's under your feet just makes sense. And it looks right.

statCheck.pass: The quartzite in that stone is a sign of metamorphic rock. Means the local bedrock was once under immense pressure: hard, dense, perfect for building.
statCheck.fail: I don't know geology. It just sparkles.

entries.intro: I tripped over a stack of crates someone left outside the {poi_type}. In the dark, no warning.
details: Skinned my knee and scared myself half to death. The crates weren't there yesterday.
By morning they were gone. Like they'd never existed, except for my bruise.
reactions: I'd complain, but I don't even know who to complain to.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s chimney sends up perfect smoke rings sometimes. Just once in a while, when conditions are right.
details: Still air and the right fuel: the smoke curls into rings as it leaves the flue. Like a parlor trick.
I've pointed it out to people and they look at me like I've lost my mind.
reactions: You have to be looking at the right time. Most people aren't.

statCheck.pass: It's a function of the flue shape and fuel moisture. Slightly damp wood creates denser smoke, and a cylindrical flue can spin it into rings if the air is still enough.
statCheck.fail: I don't know how smoke works. It just does it sometimes.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} needs more storage. They're running out of space every other week.
details: Everything's stacked too tight and it takes twice as long to find what you need.
I've heard talk of building an annex, but talk is cheap and lumber isn't.
reactions: Growing pains. Could be worse problems to have.

statCheck.pass: An elevated lean-to on the windward side would add dry storage without needing a full foundation. Half the cost of an annex and usable within a week.
statCheck.fail: I don't know building design. I just know they need more room.

entries.intro: The morning light catches the {poi_type} before anything else. It's the first building to glow.
details: Something about its angle to the east: the stone picks up the first rays while everything else is still grey.
On a good morning it looks like it's lit from inside. Beautiful, honestly.
reactions: A building that catches the dawn is a building worth looking at.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s ventilation is poor. On hot days the air inside goes thick and heavy.
details: Workers come out sweating and red-faced, sucking in fresh air like they've been underwater.
In winter the trapped heat is a blessing. In summer it's punishment.
reactions: Every building has a bad season. The {poi_type}'s bad season is summer.

statCheck.pass: The original design had vent holes near the roofline but someone blocked them to keep out rain. Open them back up with angled louvers and the airflow problem solves itself.
statCheck.fail: I'm not an architect. It's just obviously stuffy in there.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is where I met my closest friend in this settlement. We were both waiting outside for the same thing.
details: Started talking out of boredom, found we had more in common than we expected.
We still meet at that same spot sometimes. Force of habit.
reactions: Places where good things happened carry a warmth that never quite fades.

entries.intro: The mortar between the stones at the {poi_type} is crumbling. You can scrape it out with a thumbnail.
details: It's powdery and dry, white as chalk. The joints are opening up.
Individual stones are fine: it's only what holds them together that's failing.
reactions: A building is only as strong as its weakest joint. And these joints are getting pretty weak.

statCheck.pass: That mortar is lime-based and it's carbonated fully: brittle and crumbling. Repointing with a hydraulic lime mix would last another fifty years.
statCheck.fail: I don't know mortar from mud. I just see it falling out.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} feels permanent. Like it's always been here and always will be.
details: I know that's not true: everything ends eventually. But some buildings feel like exceptions.
I think it's because so many people have put so much into it. That investment anchors it.
reactions: Permanence is an illusion, but a comforting one.

entries.intro: There's a puddle of lamp oil by the door of the {poi_type}. Someone spilled it and didn't clean up.
details: It's a slick patch that catches the light: rainbow sheen on stone. Pretty, but a hazard.
I nearly went down on it carrying a full basket. Would have been a disaster.
reactions: Carelessness isn't malice, but the bruise feels the same.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been attracting traders from out of town. More than the usual passing traffic.
details: Word gets around when quality is up. People come from further away for reliable goods.
The extra business is good, but the path can't handle the extra carts.
reactions: Success creates its own problems. Better problems than failure, though.

entries.intro: The floorboards in the {poi_type} creak in sequence when someone walks across. Like a wooden xylophone.
details: Each board has its own note: deep by the door, higher toward the back.
The workers have learned to step on the silent boards. Visitors set off the whole orchestra.
reactions: You can always tell a newcomer by the amount of creaking.

statCheck.pass: The boards are nailed to different joists at different spans: shorter spans make higher notes, longer spans resonate lower. Accidental acoustics.
statCheck.fail: I have no idea why they sound different. They're all made of wood.

entries.intro: I was a {self_role} before I moved here, and the first thing I checked was the {poi_type}. It told me everything I needed to know.
details: Clean work, good output, people who knew what they were doing. I knew I could make a living here.
A settlement's {poi_type} is its handshake. You can tell what kind of place it is from that alone.
reactions: I wasn't wrong. This place suits me.

entries.intro: The loading area at the {poi_type} is always a mess. Crates, barrels, loose material everywhere.
details: They clean it at the end of each week, but by Tuesday it looks like a storm hit.
I've seen people trip, curse, and then add their own delivery to the pile.
reactions: Organization is a losing battle when the work comes in faster than you can sort it.

entries.intro: The walls of the {poi_type} are thick enough to muffle everything. Step inside and the world goes quiet.
details: The noise of the settlement vanishes. It's just the building's own sounds: creaks, drips, the work itself.
Step back outside and the volume comes back like a wave. The contrast is startling.
reactions: Thick walls are a luxury most people don't appreciate.

statCheck.pass: The walls are double-thickness with a rubble fill between. Two feet of stone and aggregate: that's why sound doesn't pass through.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand construction. The walls are just thick.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a queue of people returning defective {resource_type} yesterday. Nobody was happy.
details: The batch was flawed: brittle, undersized, not up to standard. People had every right to be annoyed.
The crew took it all back without argument. Give them that much credit.
reactions: Mistakes happen. How you handle the returns matters more than the mistake itself.

statCheck.pass: The processing temperature was too high for too long, and overheating makes the material brittle. It's a timing error, not a material one, so it's easy to correct.
statCheck.fail: I don't understand the technical side. I just know the result was bad.

entries.intro: The shadow of the {poi_type} is where the first frost melts last. Shaded ground holds the cold.
details: You can see a perfect outline of the building in white on the ground when everything else has thawed.
It's a strange sight: the building's ghost, drawn in ice.
reactions: Little things like that remind you to look down once in a while.

statCheck.pass: The north-facing shadow keeps the ground frozen because the sun never reaches it directly in winter. Once the frost is in shade-locked soil, it takes days longer to thaw.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it's the last spot. Something about the angle.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the only building in town with a proper stone chimney. Everyone else makes do with clay.
details: You can tell the difference in a storm: stone holds, clay cracks. Ours has never needed repair.
It was built right the first time. That's rarer than it should be.
reactions: Spending more up front saves you in the long run. If only everyone believed that.

entries.intro: Mice have gotten into the {poi_type}'s storage. They found evidence of chewing on the sacks.
details: Not a major loss, but it means everything needs checking before use.
The workers are setting traps and grumbling about it. Extra work nobody wanted.
reactions: Mice go where the food is. Can't take it personally.

statCheck.pass: The grain sacks are stored too low: mice can reach anything below waist height. Raised platforms with smooth legs they can't climb would solve it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know pest control. Mice just get in.

entries.intro: I appreciate the {poi_type} most when I've been away. Coming home and seeing it means I'm back.
details: It's the first familiar shape on the horizon when you approach from the road.
I didn't even know I associated it with home until the relief hit me.
reactions: Home isn't a house. It's the landmarks you recognize without thinking.

entries.intro: There's a stain on the {poi_type}'s wall where someone splashed dye or paint. It's been there for months.
details: Bright blue against grey stone. Nobody's claimed responsibility.
I've heard three different explanations and none of them sound true.
reactions: It'll fade eventually. Rain fixes most cosmetic problems, given time.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is surrounded by bootprints after a rainy day. Dozens of them, all sizes, going every direction.
details: It's like reading a map of the morning rush. You can see where people waited, turned, hurried.
By afternoon the mud dries and the prints freeze in place until the next rain washes them away.
reactions: Mud keeps an honest record.

statCheck.pass: The clay content in the soil near the {poi_type} holds prints perfectly. Sand would wash clean, but clay grabs the shape and bakes it in when it dries.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why the prints stick so well there. The mud's different, I suppose.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} could do with better lighting outside. On dark evenings you're guessing where the path ends.
details: One decent lantern on a post would solve it. The cost would be nothing compared to the convenience.
I mentioned it to {npc_name} and they agreed but said nobody's in charge of outdoor lighting.
reactions: When nobody owns the problem, the problem stays.

entries.intro: The thatch on the {poi_type} is fresh this year. Golden and neat, smells like cut grass.
details: They rethatched just before the rains started. Good timing: the old layer was thin enough to see sky through.
The workers said the new roof changed the inside temperature overnight. Warmer, drier.
reactions: A good roof is worth every hour spent on it.

statCheck.pass: Good thatch is layered in courses with the butt ends facing out, each layer overlapping the one below so water runs off instead of soaking in. Done right, it outlasts shingle.
statCheck.fail: I don't know thatching. It just looks new and smells nice.

entries.intro: I heard the {poi_type} is ahead of schedule this season. First time that's happened.
details: Normally they're scrambling to keep up. Being ahead must feel like breathing room.
I don't know what changed: new method, new crew, better luck. Whatever it is, it's working.
reactions: Ahead of schedule: two words I never expected to hear about this place.

statCheck.pass: They reorganized the workflow: batching similar tasks instead of doing everything one at a time. Small change, big improvement in throughput.
statCheck.fail: Couldn't tell you the cause. Just happy it's happening.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s waste has been attracting flies. Thick clouds of them near the back.
details: On a warm day you can hear the buzzing from the path. It's unpleasant.
The cleanup schedule slipped and nature filled the gap.
reactions: Flies don't lie. If they're there, something needs cleaning.

entries.intro: I stood inside the {poi_type} during a thunderstorm once. Safest I've felt in bad weather.
details: The walls are so thick the thunder sounds distant. Rain hammered the roof but inside was dry and still.
The workers just kept going like nothing was happening. For them, nothing was.
reactions: A well-built shelter earns your trust in a storm.

entries.intro: The weeds around the {poi_type} have gotten tall. Knee-high in places, choking the paths.
details: Nobody's pulled them in weeks. The edges of the building are disappearing into green.
It makes the place look abandoned, even though it's busier than ever inside.
reactions: Weeds grow faster than motivation to pull them. Always.

statCheck.pass: The weeds are thistle and dock, both deep-rooted: surface cutting just makes them branch. You need to dig the taproot or they come back stronger.
statCheck.fail: I don't know plants. Weeds are weeds.

entries.intro: The echo inside the {poi_type} makes everything sound important. Even a whisper carries.
details: The stone walls bounce sound around in a way that's almost theatrical.
People there have learned to speak quietly if they don't want the whole room hearing.
reactions: A room that amplifies everything is no place for secrets.

statCheck.pass: The parallel stone walls and the high ceiling create a reflective chamber. Sound bounces between the surfaces and amplifies instead of being absorbed.
statCheck.fail: I'm not sure why it echoes so much. Just the shape of the room, I guess.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} just celebrated some kind of milestone. I saw garlands on the doorframe.
details: Nobody announced what it was for. Just decorations one morning, gone by evening.
The workers seemed in high spirits, though. Whatever they were marking, it mattered to them.
reactions: A place that celebrates its own milestones is a place with soul.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a drip inside that nobody can find the source of. Just a steady plop, plop, plop.
details: The sound drives the workers mad when it's quiet. You can hear it between hammer strokes.
They've looked everywhere for the leak: ceiling's dry, walls are dry. But the drip continues.
reactions: A mystery drip: every building has one.

statCheck.pass: Condensation from temperature changes: warm humid air meets a cold stone surface somewhere hidden and the water collects and drips. Not a leak, just physics.
statCheck.fail: Beats me. Water finds its way in somehow.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} gets louder every year. I don't know if they're doing more work or just doing it noisier.
details: You can hear the clatter from two streets away now. Used to be you had to be right next to it.
I've started timing my walks to avoid the peak hours. Quieter that way.
reactions: Noise is the price of a working settlement. I just wish the price were lower some mornings.

entries.intro: Walked past the {poi_type} this morning and caught the smell of fresh timber. They've replaced some beams.
details: New wood against old stone: there's something honest about that combination.
The sawdust was still on the ground. Soft underfoot, like walking on a carpet.
reactions: A building that gets maintained is a building that gets used. That's a good sign.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} was closed for half a day last week. Nobody told us why.
details: Doors shut, no workers outside, nothing. Just a quiet building for half a morning.
By afternoon it was open again like nothing happened. Probably routine maintenance.
reactions: I don't need to know everything that goes on in there. Just felt odd.

statCheck.pass: They were recalibrating some equipment and couldn't have people milling around while they worked. Delicate job, apparently.
statCheck.fail: Nobody explained and I didn't push for answers.

entries.intro: The drainage ditch near the {poi_type} overflows every heavy rain. Water pools across the whole path.
details: You have to pick your way around it or accept wet boots. Neither option is great.
Someone threw planks across the worst part. Helps, but they shift when you step on them.
reactions: It's a small annoyance, but small annoyances add up when they happen every week.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has been turning out solid {resource_type} for months now. Consistent quality, no complaints.
details: When something works well for long enough, you stop noticing. That's the best compliment.
The workers seem less frantic than they used to be. Steady pace, no panic.
reactions: Quiet competence doesn't get celebrated, but it should.

entries.intro: I can tell the season by what I smell near the {poi_type}. Right now it's wood smoke and damp earth.
details: In summer it's dust and hot stone. In autumn it's rotting leaves piled against the wall.
Every season paints the same building differently. I like noticing the change.
reactions: A place that smells different through the year feels more alive than one that doesn't.

entries.intro: There's a bird nesting under the eaves of the {poi_type}. Comes back every spring.
details: Same spot, same nest, rebuilt from scratch each year. Stubborn little thing.
The workers leave it alone. I think they'd miss it if it stopped coming.
reactions: Something about a bird choosing your building feels like an endorsement.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a draft that cuts right through you when the wind comes from the north.
details: Doesn't matter how many doors you close: the air finds a way in. Cold fingers on the back of your neck.
In winter the workers wear an extra layer just for the draft. Standard practice by now.
reactions: You learn to live with a building's flaws the way you learn to live with a neighbor's habits.

statCheck.pass: There's a gap where the north wall meets the roofline: too high to see from the ground, but the air pours through it like a funnel.
statCheck.fail: The draft just exists. Nobody's tracked down where it comes from.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks different in fog. The edges blur and it seems bigger than it is.
details: I walked toward it last foggy morning and it loomed up like something ancient. Startled me.
Familiar things become unfamiliar in the right weather. That's not a complaint.
reactions: Fog turns a settlement into a different place. I don't mind it.

entries.intro: Someone painted numbers on the crates at the {poi_type}. First time anyone's organized anything there.
details: Bold white numbers on dark wood. Clean, clear, practical.
The people working there seem confused by the system but are going along with it.
reactions: Organization is always awkward before it becomes habit.

entries.intro: I've been a {self_role} long enough to know the {poi_type} runs this settlement. Everything flows from there.
details: When they're busy, we're all busy. When they slow down, everyone feels it.
People don't always see the connection, but I do. My livelihood depends on it.
reactions: Knowing what keeps you fed makes you pay attention to things others ignore.

entries.intro: The sunsets behind the {poi_type} are the best view in the settlement. The silhouette against the colors.
details: Orange and pink behind dark stone. On a clear evening it's worth stopping for.
I've seen it a hundred times and I still pause. That says something.
reactions: Beauty you can see from your doorstep is easy to take for granted. I try not to.

entries.intro: The step at the {poi_type}'s entrance is wearing smooth. Years of boots grinding it down.
details: There's a groove where the most feet land: curved like a shallow bowl in the stone.
I like that about it. A building that shows its age honestly.
reactions: Worn stone means people showed up. That's a good thing.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} still has a hand-painted sign. Half the letters are faded but you can make them out if you squint.
details: The paint was blue once, now it's grey with blue memories. Nobody's repainted it.
Whoever lettered it had a steady hand. The curves are still graceful even faded.
reactions: Old signs have character that new ones can't match.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} puts out its lanterns earlier than anyone else. First light on the street every evening.
details: You can navigate by those lanterns when everything else is dark. Reliable as the moon.
I don't know if it's policy or just someone who's punctual. Either way, I appreciate it.
reactions: The person who lights the lamps doesn't get thanked enough.

entries.intro: There's a crack in one of the {poi_type}'s upper windows. Been there as long as I can remember.
details: Rain gets in, probably. A little each storm, enough to stain the stone below the sill.
Nobody fixes what's out of arm's reach, apparently.
reactions: If you can ignore it, it stays broken. Human nature.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} runs like clockwork now. Wasn't always that way.
details: A few seasons ago it was chaos: missed schedules, short output, constant bickering.
Someone figured out a better way of doing things, and it stuck. Just took time.
reactions: Most problems fix themselves if you leave capable people alone long enough.

statCheck.pass: They split the crew into dedicated shifts instead of everyone doing everything. Specialization cut the wasted hours in half.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the details. I just know it works better now.

entries.intro: The ground around the {poi_type} is packed hard as stone from all the foot traffic.
details: Nothing grows there anymore. The earth is compressed and grey, almost polished.
After rain it's slick as ice. People walk carefully or they don't walk upright.
reactions: Hard ground is the mark of a well-used path. Uncomfortable but honest.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the tallest thing around. You can see it from the fields.
details: On a clear day it's a dark shape above the rooflines. A landmark you don't even think about.
When I first arrived, someone pointed to it and said 'that's us.' That was my introduction.
reactions: A settlement is its landmarks. Everything else is just houses.

entries.intro: The roof tiles on the {poi_type} are mismatched. Different colors, different shapes, patched over the years.
details: Every repair used whatever was available at the time. The result is a patchwork of eras.
It looks messy from below, but at least it doesn't leak. Function over form.
reactions: A roof that works is a roof that works, pretty or not.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been running short on hands this season. Everyone's stretched thin.
details: The same people are doing more work with fewer breaks. You can see the tiredness on their faces.
Nobody's quit, but the pace can't hold forever.
reactions: People don't break all at once. They wear down slowly, and by the time you notice, it's late.

statCheck.pass: Two of the regulars have been dealing with family matters and the replacements haven't learned the routines yet. It's a temporary gap, but it lands hard.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the specifics. I just see fewer faces doing the same amount of work.

entries.intro: I like the sound the {poi_type} makes in the early morning. Quiet creaks and settling noises, like it's waking up.
details: Before the workers arrive, the building talks to itself. Wood expanding, metal contracting.
It sounds different from the daytime racket. Peaceful, almost.
reactions: Buildings have voices if you're up early enough to hear them.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s door hinges scream every time someone opens them. You can hear it from the road.
details: A drop of oil would fix it in ten seconds. It's been screaming for months.
At this point I think the workers are deaf to it. The rest of us aren't.
reactions: There's a special kind of laziness reserved for easy fixes.

entries.intro: Saw {npc_name} coming out of the {poi_type} with a grin on their face. Must have been a good day in there.
details: They don't smile like that often. Whatever happened, it was worth the visit.
I tried to ask but they just waved and kept walking. Some good moods don't need explaining.
reactions: A good day for someone is a good day for the settlement. That's how I see it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s well has hard water. Leaves a white crust on everything it touches.
details: The buckets are coated in mineral scale. Looks like frost but it's chalky to the touch.
It's drinkable, just not pleasant. Tastes like you're chewing a pebble.
reactions: Hard water isn't dangerous. Just annoying.

statCheck.pass: The bedrock here is limestone, and the groundwater dissolves calcium as it passes through. That's what you're tasting: dissolved rock.
statCheck.fail: Something in the ground, probably. I don't know water science.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has good bones. Whatever they built it from, it was meant to last.
details: The joints are tight, the walls are plumb, the foundation hasn't shifted.
You can tell when a structure was built by someone who cared versus someone in a hurry.
reactions: Craftsmanship outlives the craftsman. That's the whole point.

entries.intro: It rained all week and the {poi_type}'s path turned to soup. Ankle-deep mud, churned to nothing.
details: Every cart wheel digs deeper ruts. By the fifth day it was more ditch than path.
People were carrying their boots and walking in bare feet. Less to clean that way.
reactions: Mud is temporary. The memory of mud in your boots is forever.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} gets more sunlight than any other building in the settlement. South-facing, wide open.
details: In winter the warmth off the stone wall is noticeable. People lean against it just for the heat.
The workers don't know how lucky they are. My place is cold by noon.
reactions: A warm building makes for happier people. It's that simple.

entries.intro: The deliveries to the {poi_type} always come at dawn. Carts rattling over cobblestones before anyone's awake.
details: I've gotten used to the sound: wheels and hooves and muffled voices. My morning alarm.
On the days they don't come, the silence wakes me instead. Funny how that works.
reactions: Routine sounds become part of how you sleep. Remove them and you notice.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s gutters are clogged again. Water pours off the edge instead of running down the drain.
details: Leaves and debris from last autumn. Nobody cleaned them out and now it's a waterfall when it rains.
The splash zone at the base is getting wider. The ground there is eroding.
reactions: Maintenance skipped is maintenance doubled. Always.

entries.intro: I like the way the {poi_type} looks in autumn. The vines turn red and the stone goes golden in the low light.
details: For about two weeks it's the prettiest building in the settlement. Then the leaves drop and it's bare again.
I make a point of walking past during that window. It's worth the detour.
reactions: Short-lived beauty is still beauty. Maybe more so.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} always has a line in the morning. Everyone shows up at the same time.
details: You'd think people would stagger their arrivals, but no. All at once, every day.
The wait isn't long. But standing there with everyone else feels like a ritual at this point.
reactions: People are creatures of habit. The line proves it.

entries.intro: Someone left a handcart at the {poi_type} overnight. It's still there, rusting in the damp.
details: One wheel is crooked and the handles are splintered. Looks abandoned, not forgotten.
It's been there three days now. If nobody claims it, the rust will.
reactions: Abandoned things become everyone's problem and nobody's responsibility.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is where the children play when it rains. Plenty of cover and space to run.
details: The workers don't seem to mind as long as the kids stay out of the working areas.
You can hear the laughter from outside: echoing off stone walls, amplified.
reactions: A building that shelters more than just workers has earned its place.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a smell to it. Not bad, just distinct. You always know when you're near.
details: It's a mix of the materials they work with and the wood smoke from inside. Gets into everything.
People who work there carry the smell home. Their neighbors can tell what shift they worked.
reactions: Every workplace leaves its mark on you. Some you can wash off, some you can't.

entries.intro: The bench outside the {poi_type} is the best seat in the settlement for people-watching.
details: Everyone passes by sooner or later. You just sit and the entertainment comes to you.
I've spent whole afternoons there. Learned more about my neighbors than I intended.
reactions: If you want to know a settlement, sit still and let it walk past you.

entries.intro: The flagpole at the {poi_type} leans to one side. Has for a while now.
details: The base is loose in the ground: you can wobble it with one hand. Nobody's reset it.
On a windy day the banner pulls it further over. One good storm and it's coming down.
reactions: I'm not going to fix it. But I'll be the first to say I told you so.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s producing more {resource_type} than we can use locally. There's talk of trading the surplus.
details: It's a good problem to have, honestly. Better too much than not enough.
If the quality holds, we might have something real to offer the next trader who comes through.
reactions: Surplus is the beginning of trade. Trade is the beginning of reputation.

statCheck.pass: The surplus started when they switched to a double-processing method: takes longer per batch but the yield per unit of raw material is almost twice what it was.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why the output jumped. Just glad it did.

entries.intro: There's a wasp nest forming on the {poi_type}. Right above the doorway.
details: Small right now: grey paper, a few wasps circling. It'll be a problem in a month if nobody deals with it.
Everyone walks under it with their head down, like not looking will protect them.
reactions: Wasps don't care about your schedule. They build where they build.

statCheck.pass: That's a paper wasp nest: less aggressive than hornets unless you disturb them, but they'll defend the entrance. Best knocked down at night when they're dormant.
statCheck.fail: I stay away from anything with a stinger. That's my whole approach.

entries.intro: The well at the {poi_type} has the sweetest water in the settlement. Cold and clean.
details: People come from the other end of town just to fill a jug. It's that much better.
Must be the depth of it, or what the water passes through on the way up.
reactions: Good water is the kind of thing you don't appreciate until you've had bad water.

statCheck.pass: The well draws from a deeper aquifer than the others: past the clay layer into sand-filtered groundwater. That's why it tastes cleaner.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why it tastes better. It just does.

entries.intro: The wind whistles through the {poi_type} on gusty days. Long, low notes like a sad flute.
details: Something about the gaps in the structure catches the air just right. Changes pitch with the wind.
Some people find it eerie. I've heard it enough times that it's just weather noise.
reactions: Buildings talk when the wind blows. You learn to tune it out.

statCheck.pass: There's a vent gap in the upper wall that narrows at one point: the Venturi effect speeds the air and makes it vibrate as it passes through. Same principle as a reed instrument.
statCheck.fail: Wind does what wind does. I don't know the mechanics.

entries.intro: I always know when the {poi_type}'s finished for the day. The noise stops and the quiet rushes in.
details: That transition from working sounds to evening silence is one of the best parts of the day.
You hear the last tools being put down, the last voices, then nothing. Just birdsong.
reactions: The end of the workday is a shared exhale. Everyone feels it.

entries.intro: Someone carved initials into the doorframe of the {poi_type}. Deep cuts, not just scratches.
details: No telling how old they are. The wood has darkened around them, smoothed the edges.
I wonder if the person who carved them still lives here, or if they moved on years ago.
reactions: People want to leave marks. That impulse never changes.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s latest batch of {resource_type} wasn't up to standard. People noticed.
details: Nothing terrible, just a dip in quality. The kind of thing regulars catch immediately.
I'm sure they'll correct it. One bad batch doesn't erase a good track record.
reactions: Consistency is what people actually pay for. The product is secondary.

statCheck.pass: The raw material they sourced this time was different: lower grade, harder to work with. Not their fault, but the result shows.
statCheck.fail: Could be anything. I don't know the process well enough to say.

entries.intro: The moss on the north side of the {poi_type} is thick as a carpet. Bright green, always damp.
details: It's been growing there longer than I've been here. The stone underneath is probably soft by now.
In the morning light it almost glows. Pretty, in a slow-decay kind of way.
reactions: Nature doesn't care who owns the wall. It grows where it's comfortable.

statCheck.pass: Moss holds moisture against the stone, and over decades that moisture breaks down the surface through freeze-thaw cycles. The wall is likely pitted underneath.
statCheck.fail: It's just moss. I don't know if it's harmful or not.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is where everyone gathers when there's news. Habit more than logic.
details: Good news, bad news, rumors: people drift toward the same spot like it's gravity.
I've found myself walking there before I even knew what I'd heard. Just instinct.
reactions: Every settlement has a natural gathering point. Ours is the {poi_type}.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s shutters are painted a deep red. Brightest color in the whole settlement.
details: Stands out against the grey stone like a signal fire. You can spot them from the road.
Whoever chose that color had opinions. Strong ones.
reactions: I like it. One bold choice can lift a whole building.

entries.intro: Dust blows off the {poi_type} in dry weather. Fine particles that coat everything downwind.
details: My laundry catches it if I hang clothes on the wrong day. Gritty film on everything.
The workers are used to it. The rest of us just learn which way the wind blows.
reactions: You live near a working building, you accept the consequences.

entries.intro: As a {self_role}, I spend more time near the {poi_type} than most. You notice things when you're there every day.
details: The way the shadows move, who comes and goes, which days are busier. It becomes a rhythm you learn.
I could draw the place from memory with my eyes closed. Every crack, every stain.
reactions: Familiarity isn't the same as boredom. I know this place, and I'm fine with that.

entries.intro: The chimney of the {poi_type} is listing slightly to one side. Not enough to fall, but enough to notice.
details: Once you see it, you can't unsee it. The whole top section leans about a hand's width east.
It's probably been that way for years. The mortar is set, the lean is stable.
reactions: Crooked doesn't mean failing. Some things settle into their tilt and stay there.

statCheck.pass: The foundation on one side sits on clay that shrinks in dry seasons. The chimney shifts when the clay contracts and never quite shifts back.
statCheck.fail: I'm no builder. It leans, that's all I know.

entries.intro: I heard the {poi_type}'s workers singing this morning. Not well, but loudly. Good spirits.
details: It was some work song with a repeating chorus. You could tell they'd been singing it for years.
The rhythm matched the work pace. Hammers and voices in time.
reactions: People who sing at work are people who don't dread the day. That matters.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} lost a shutter in the last windstorm. It's leaning against the wall now, unhung.
details: The hinges tore right out of the frame. You can see the splintered holes where they used to be.
Someone propped it up neatly, at least. But neatly propped is still not fixed.
reactions: Wind tests everything eventually. Not everything passes.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} always smells faintly of ash. Even when the fires aren't burning.
details: It's soaked into the stone and the timber over the years. Part of the building's character now.
First time I went inside I sneezed for an hour. Now I barely notice.
reactions: You get used to a smell faster than you'd think. After a week, it's invisible.

entries.intro: The wildflowers growing along the base of the {poi_type} are in bloom. Purple and yellow, thick as a border garden.
details: Nobody planted them. They just show up every spring, loyal as anything.
The bees love them. You can hear the buzzing from six paces away.
reactions: Free flowers are the best kind. No effort, all beauty.

entries.intro: The lock on the {poi_type} is more decorative than functional. A stiff kick would open that door.
details: It's a big iron thing that looks impressive but the hasp is loose and the wood around it is soft.
I suppose the lock's purpose is to say 'this is closed' more than to actually prevent entry.
reactions: Most security is symbolic. People respect the symbol more than the mechanism.

statCheck.pass: The screws holding the hasp plate are driven into punky wood: you could pull them out by hand. If you're going to lock something, lock it into sound timber.
statCheck.fail: I don't know locks. It just looks flimsy to me.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been here longer than anyone in the settlement. Nobody remembers it being built.
details: It's just always been there: part of the landscape, like a hill or a stream.
I asked around once and the oldest resident shrugged. Said it was old when they arrived.
reactions: Some things predate memory. You use them and don't question the origin.

entries.intro: I saw a cat sleeping on the warm stones outside the {poi_type}. Belly up, not a care in the world.
details: The stones hold the afternoon heat and the cat knows exactly when they're warmest.
Workers step around it. Nobody disturbs a sleeping cat: that's an unwritten law.
reactions: A cat that sleeps in the open trusts the place. That's worth something.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s waste pile is getting tall. They need to haul it out before it becomes a hill.
details: It's mostly scraps and offcuts: nothing harmful, just unsightly.
The smell isn't great either, especially in the heat. Draws flies and complaints in equal measure.
reactions: A clean workspace reflects a clean operation. This one's slipping.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a weather vane that actually works. You can tell the wind direction from across the settlement.
details: It spins freely and catches even a light breeze. Whoever made it knew what they were doing.
I check it every morning out of habit. Points east, I bring a cloak. Points south, I leave it home.
reactions: A reliable weather vane is more useful than most people realize.

entries.intro: The path to the {poi_type} forks around a boulder that nobody's ever moved. Everyone just walks around it.
details: It's been there so long the paths on either side are worn smooth. The boulder won.
Children climb on it. Adults curse it. The boulder doesn't care about either.
