reactions: Sometimes the obstacle becomes the landmark. That rock is half the directions people give.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s roof leaks in one spot. They've put a bucket under it and called it solved.
details: The plinking sound of drips hitting tin is constant during rain. Rhythmic, almost musical.
The bucket overflows if nobody empties it. Which means someone's job is to watch a bucket.
reactions: A bucket under a leak isn't a repair. It's surrender with good posture.

statCheck.pass: The leak is where the flashing meets the chimney: a seal that's failed. A strip of pitch cloth pressed into the joint would stop it permanently.
statCheck.fail: I don't know roofing. The bucket seems to work well enough.

entries.intro: I like that the {poi_type} doesn't try to be impressive. It's plain, solid, and it works.
details: No carvings, no fancy stonework, no flourishes. Just good proportions and honest materials.
The beauty is in the simplicity. Or maybe I'm just easy to please.
reactions: Plain done well is better than fancy done poorly. I'll argue that with anyone.

entries.intro: There's a puddle by the {poi_type} that never fully dries. Even in summer there's a damp spot.
details: The ground there must sit lower than everything around it. Water finds its way in from all directions.
In winter it freezes into a slick patch that catches at least one person a week.
reactions: Permanent puddles are permanent hazards. But we've all learned to step around it.

statCheck.pass: There's likely a buried spring or seep feeding that spot from below. No amount of drainage will fix it because the water is coming up, not down.
statCheck.fail: The ground is just wet there. I couldn't tell you why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s newest worker is still learning the ropes. I see them asking questions every time I pass.
details: They're eager, which counts for a lot. The experienced workers are patient with them.
Give it a season and they'll be the one answering questions. That's how it always goes.
reactions: Watching someone learn is watching the future getting built. I don't mind it.

entries.intro: The stone around the {poi_type}'s fireplace is blackened and cracked from years of heat. Still holding.
details: The cracks are fine, webbed across the surface like veins. Heat stress, written in stone.
It gives the place a weathered look. Not damaged: just experienced.
reactions: Things that endure heat earn their scars. Buildings and people both.

statCheck.pass: Repeated heating and cooling causes thermal expansion cycles that crack the surface layer. The core of the stone is still sound: it's only the face that's damaged.
statCheck.fail: I see cracks. Whether that's a problem or just cosmetic, I couldn't say.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} was quiet yesterday. Barely anyone around. Unusual for a working day.
details: I don't know if people were sick or just taking an unannounced break.
It felt strange walking past without hearing the usual noise. Like a missing tooth in a smile.
reactions: You don't appreciate the rhythm of a place until it skips a beat.

statCheck.pass: They were doing a deep clean and equipment check: happens once a season. Everything gets pulled out, scrubbed, and put back. Production stops for a day.
statCheck.fail: Could be anything. Slow days happen.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a sundial scratched into the south wall. Rough but functional.
details: Someone drove a nail into the mortar joint and the shadow tells the time. Simple and clever.
It's only accurate for half the year, but it's better than guessing.
reactions: The best solutions are the simplest ones. A nail and a wall and you've got a clock.

entries.intro: Rats got into the {poi_type}'s storage again. Third time this season.
details: They chew through everything: sacking, wood, even thin metal if it's soft enough.
The losses aren't huge but the cleanup is miserable. Nobody volunteers for rat duty.
reactions: Rats are a fact of life near stored goods. Doesn't make them less irritating.

statCheck.pass: The floor has a gap where the wall meets the foundation: just wide enough for a rat. Metal flashing pressed into the joint would seal it off.
statCheck.fail: They get in somehow. That's all I know about rats.

entries.intro: The light inside the {poi_type} is beautiful in the late afternoon. Comes through the west windows at a low angle.
details: Everything turns amber: the wood, the stone, the dust in the air. Like being inside a lantern.
The workers barely notice anymore, but visitors always stop and look.
reactions: Some buildings are accidentally beautiful at certain hours. This is one of them.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s doorstep is cracked right down the middle. Someone heavy or something dropped.
details: The halves have shifted apart about a finger's width. You can see the dirt underneath.
People step over it without thinking now. The crack is part of the entrance.
reactions: Broken things that still work don't get fixed. That's the rule.

entries.intro: The area around the {poi_type} is the warmest part of the settlement in winter. Heat leaks from the building.
details: Snow melts faster there than anywhere else. The ground is always a shade greener.
People gather outside on cold days just to stand in the warmth. Unofficial heating.
reactions: Wasted heat, maybe. But nobody standing in it calls it waste.

statCheck.pass: The building's thermal mass absorbs heat during the day and radiates it outward at night. The walls act like a slow-release furnace.
statCheck.fail: It's just warm near buildings. I don't know the science.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} gets a fresh coat of whitewash every few years. Right now it's due.
details: The current coat is grey and streaked: rain-washed, sun-faded, peeling at the edges.
When they do finally paint it, the brightness is almost startling. Like it's new again.
reactions: Whitewash is the cheapest facelift there is. I wish they'd do it more often.

entries.intro: {npc_name} says the {poi_type} used to be smaller. They expanded it before I arrived.
details: You can see where the old wall ends and the new one begins if you look closely. Different stone color.
The addition doubled the workspace. Apparently it was desperately needed.
reactions: Growth is messy, but a building that gets bigger is a building that's needed.

entries.intro: The pigeons at the {poi_type} are out of control. Dozens of them, cooing and leaving messes everywhere.
details: The ledges are caked in droppings. It's unsanitary and it looks terrible.
They've tried everything: blocking perches, making noise, nothing works. Pigeons don't negotiate.
reactions: You don't beat pigeons. You learn to tolerate pigeons.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the first place I show visitors. It makes a good impression.
details: People see it and think 'this settlement has its act together.' Which is mostly true.
I'm proud of it in a way that surprises me. I didn't build it, but I live near it.
reactions: Reflected pride is still pride. I'll take it.

entries.intro: The ground near the {poi_type} is stained where they dump wash water. Permanent discoloration.
details: The soil there is a different color from everything around it: darker, slightly oily-looking.
Grass won't grow in the stained patch. The ground just stays bare and hard.
reactions: Every workplace leaves a footprint. This one's just more visible than most.

statCheck.pass: The tannins and minerals in the wash water have changed the soil chemistry. Nothing will grow there until the contaminated layer is dug out and replaced.
statCheck.fail: Something in the water killed the ground, I guess. Not my area.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a rhythm to its workday you can set your schedule by.
details: Fires lit at dawn, peak output midmorning, break at midday, second push in the afternoon, done by dusk.
I time my errands to the quiet periods. The path is clearer when they're on break.
reactions: Living near a predictable place makes your own life more predictable. I like that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} caught a hard frost last night. The whole exterior was white this morning.
details: The stone was so cold it hurt to touch. The metal fittings had ice crystals growing on them.
By midday the sun cleared most of it, but the shaded side stayed frosted until late afternoon.
reactions: A frosted building is a beautiful thing. As long as you're looking at it from inside.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been producing steadily all month. Nothing flashy, just reliable output.
details: The kind of month nobody talks about because there's nothing to complain about.
I prefer boring months. Boring months mean everything's working.
reactions: Reliability doesn't make for good stories. But it makes for a good life.

entries.intro: The smoke from the {poi_type} stains the downwind buildings. The stone's darker on that side.
details: You can trace the prevailing wind direction by following the soot marks across the settlement.
Nobody likes it, but nobody wants to move the chimney either. Too much work.
reactions: Smoke goes where wind takes it. You can complain, but the wind won't listen.

entries.intro: I brought some {resource_type} from the {poi_type} home to examine. Good quality this season.
details: Solid weight, clean finish, no defects I could find. They're doing something right.
I'm not an expert, but I know enough to tell the difference between care and carelessness.
reactions: Quality you can hold in your hand is the most convincing kind.

entries.intro: The stairs at the {poi_type} are worn unevenly. The left side is a full step lower than the right.
details: Everyone favors the same side going up, apparently. Decades of that and the stone remembers.
I always take the right side. Less traffic, less wear, better footing.
reactions: Stone remembers every foot that crosses it. We just don't notice until the damage is done.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} draws visitors from {other_settlement} sometimes. They come to compare notes.
details: Word of good work travels further than you'd think. People want to see how it's done.
Our crew acts professional when outsiders are watching. The rest of the time, less so.
reactions: Being worth visiting means we're doing something right.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is too dark inside. Not enough windows, and the ones it has are small.
details: People work by lamplight even at midday. It's a waste of oil when the sun is right there.
I mentioned it once and was told the original builder valued thick walls over daylight.
reactions: Choices made by people long gone still shape our daily lives. That's the thing about buildings.

statCheck.pass: Thick walls with small windows were deliberate: defense over comfort. The builder expected trouble. The walls are nearly three feet thick: that's a fortress standard.
statCheck.fail: Maybe they just liked thick walls. I wouldn't know.

entries.intro: The wooden beams in the {poi_type} are carved with notch marks. Dozens of them, tallying something.
details: Seasons, shipments, workers: nobody's sure what the notches counted.
New ones haven't been added in a long time. Whatever the tradition was, it ended.
reactions: Records scratched into wood outlast the people who made them. There's a lesson in that.

statCheck.pass: The spacing matches batch counts: each notch is one completed run. I counted them once and the total matches the production records from a few years back.
statCheck.fail: Could be anything. Tally marks without a label are just scratches.

entries.intro: A heavy snowfall made the {poi_type}'s roof creak last winter. Everyone held their breath.
details: The timber groaned under the weight. You could hear it shifting, adjusting to the load.
By morning the crew had cleared the worst of it. But those sounds stay with you.
reactions: You trust a building until the building makes a noise it shouldn't. Then you trust your legs.

statCheck.pass: Snow loads on a pitched roof concentrate at the eaves and valleys. The creaking was probably the ridge beam flexing: concerning but not critical unless the span is too long.
statCheck.fail: Creaking is creaking. I got out and let the builders worry about it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the kind of place that looks better from a distance. Up close, the flaws show.
details: Patched walls, uneven mortar, stains from years of use. From across the settlement it's handsome.
I don't hold it against the place. Everything looks better with a bit of distance.
reactions: Imperfections are what make a building lived-in instead of just built.

entries.intro: The iron fittings on the {poi_type} have rusted to a deep orange. Looks almost intentional.
details: The rust streaks run down the stone beneath them: long orange teardrops on grey.
Structurally they're still sound. Rust takes decades to weaken iron this thick.
reactions: Rust is just iron returning to the earth, slowly. Not everything that's decaying is failing.

statCheck.pass: Surface rust actually protects the deeper metal by forming a barrier: it's called a patina layer. As long as it's not flaking in sheets, the iron is fine.
statCheck.fail: I see rust. Whether that's bad or just ugly, I couldn't tell you.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is where I go when I need to think. Something about the noise helps me focus.
details: Constant background sound: tools, voices, work. It fills the silence without demanding attention.
I sit near enough to hear it but far enough not to be in the way. Perfect distance.
reactions: Some people need quiet to think. I need a little chaos.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s last inspection found nothing wrong. Clean bill of health.
details: The crew looked relieved. I think they'd been worried about the older timbers.
A building that passes inspection is a building that's been cared for. Not all of them do.
reactions: Good news doesn't travel as fast as bad news. But it should.

entries.intro: There's a loose stone in the {poi_type}'s wall that wobbles when you push it. Feels like it might come out.
details: It's at shoulder height, easy to reach. I've seen people absentmindedly wiggle it as they pass.
One day someone's going to push too hard and a stone will end up on the ground.
reactions: A wall is only as strong as its weakest stone. And everyone keeps testing this one.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} hums when it's running at full capacity. A low vibration you feel in your chest.
details: It's not unpleasant: more like a heartbeat than a noise. Steady, rhythmic, purposeful.
When it stops, the absence is louder than the hum ever was.
reactions: A working building has a pulse. You only notice it when it stops.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s door is always open in summer. You can see straight through from the road.
details: The cross-breeze keeps the interior bearable. Close the door and it's an oven within the hour.
Passersby peek in: some wave, some keep walking. An open door is an invitation to look.
reactions: Open doors make a place feel welcoming. Closed ones make you wonder.

entries.intro: I don't love the {poi_type}. It's functional, and that's all it needs to be.
details: Some people get sentimental about buildings. I just need it to work and stay standing.
It does both, so I've got no complaints. Doesn't mean I have to admire it.
reactions: Not everything needs to inspire affection. Sometimes 'adequate' is enough.

entries.intro: The stonework on the {poi_type} is better than anything else in the settlement. Tight joints, clean cuts.
details: Whoever built it had skills that most masons would envy. Precision work.
The rest of the settlement was built with less care. The {poi_type} is the exception.
reactions: One well-built thing raises the standard for everything around it.

entries.intro: The signpost near the {poi_type} points in three directions. One of the signs has fallen off.
details: The remaining two point to places I've never been. The missing one might have been the most useful.
Nobody's replaced it. The post leans slightly, like it's tired of standing.
reactions: Incomplete directions are worse than no directions. At least with none you know to ask.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} closes early on market days. The workers want to get out and spend their wages.
details: By midafternoon the place is locked up and the crew is scattered through the stalls.
It's the one day the schedule bends. Nobody objects: everyone needs a short day sometimes.
reactions: A workplace that knows when to close earns loyalty. People work harder when they're not trapped.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a cat that earns its keep. Thin, fast, absolutely relentless with the mice.
details: It shows up in the morning, patrols the storage areas, and vanishes by evening.
The workers leave scraps out for it. A well-fed mouser is still a mouser.
reactions: Some workers don't need a wage. Just respect and a few scraps.

entries.intro: Rain pools on the {poi_type}'s flat roof and takes hours to drain. The weight worries me sometimes.
details: After a heavy storm you can hear the water sloshing when the wind blows. Not reassuring.
The drainage channel is too narrow for the roof area. It backs up before it clears.
reactions: Water is patient and heavy. Buildings that forget that pay the price.

statCheck.pass: A wider scupper on the low end would double the drainage rate. The current one is sized for light rain, not the downpours we actually get.
statCheck.fail: I know water's heavy. That's about the extent of my roofing knowledge.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} smells like sawdust and copper today. Different from the usual.
details: Whatever they're working on in there, the smell carries. Sharp and metallic with something sweet underneath.
I don't mind it. It's a working smell: means something's getting made.
reactions: You can tell what a place is doing by its smell. Today's a copper day.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a near miss yesterday. A loaded cart nearly rolled into the wall.
details: The brake failed on the slope and it drifted. Stopped about a foot short of the stonework.
Everyone stood frozen for a second, then someone grabbed it. No harm done, but close.
reactions: Close calls teach more than actual damage. At least, I hope they do.

entries.intro: The view from the {poi_type}'s upper level is the best in the settlement. I've seen it once.
details: You can see the fields, the tree line, the road winding in from the distance. All of it.
The workers have that view every day and probably don't even notice anymore.
reactions: A good view is wasted on people who see it every day. Or maybe they're the luckiest ones.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s waste chute is blocked. Everything's backing up.
details: The smell alone tells you something's wrong. You don't even need to see it.
Someone's going to have to clear it by hand. Nobody's volunteering.
reactions: The worst jobs are the ones nobody claims. Eventually someone breaks first.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks its best after rain. The stone darkens and the colors come alive.
details: Wet stone has a richness that dry stone lacks. Like the building wakes up when it's washed.
The lichens and moss go vivid green. For an hour the whole place looks brand new.
reactions: Rain does for a building what a good night's sleep does for a face.

entries.intro: The workers at the {poi_type} take their midday break outside when the weather's good. Cards and conversation.
details: They sit on overturned crates and eat whatever they've brought. Nobody's in a hurry to go back in.
The break lasts exactly as long as it should and not a second longer. Self-policing.
reactions: People who pace themselves last longer than people who sprint. The crew knows that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s timber frame is exposed on one side where the plaster fell off. Been like that all season.
details: You can see the construction underneath: oak beams with wattle and daub between them.
It's interesting to look at, but exposed timber rots faster than covered timber.
reactions: Every building is honest when its skin comes off. This one has good bones, at least.

statCheck.pass: The wattle pattern is hazel rods woven through oak staves: traditional but sound. If they replaster before the next wet season, no harm done.
statCheck.fail: I see bare wood. Whether that's urgent or not, I couldn't say.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a hand bell they ring at shift changes. Clear as a chapel bell, carries across the whole settlement.
details: Two rings at dawn, one at midday, two at dusk. Everyone's tuned to it whether they work there or not.
I've started using it to track my own day. Better than counting shadows.
reactions: Shared timekeeping binds a settlement together. Everyone hears the same bell.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s newer section doesn't match the old one. Different stone, different style, obviously added later.
details: The join is visible from the road: old grey stone meets new yellow stone in a straight line.
Functionally it works fine. Aesthetically, well, it is what it is.
reactions: Matching old work is expensive. Functional mismatch is cheap. Cheap usually wins.

entries.intro: I found a coin in the dirt near the {poi_type}. Old one, worn smooth.
details: The face was barely visible: just a curve that might have been a crown. The rest was gone.
I pocketed it. Not worth anything, but it felt like a small piece of the place's history.
reactions: Old coins in old dirt. The ground remembers transactions we've forgotten.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been turning out {resource_type} faster than the traders can move it. Stockpiles growing.
details: The storage is nearly full and the carts haven't come this week.
If the pace keeps up without buyers, they'll have to slow production. Nobody wants that.
reactions: Making things is only half the work. Getting them where they need to go is the other half.

statCheck.pass: The trader route shifted because of road conditions further out. Once the roads dry, the carts will come and the backlog will clear in days.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why the traders are late. They'll show up when they show up.

entries.intro: There's a vine growing up the side of the {poi_type}. It's reached the second-story window.
details: Thick stem at the base, thin tendrils at the top reaching for anything to grip.
It's green year-round. Somehow it thrives on that particular wall.
reactions: A building that grows things accidentally is a building in a good spot.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the noisiest building in the settlement. Also the most important. Funny how that works.
details: You hear it before you see it: metal on metal, voices, the thump of heavy things being moved.
It's the sound of employment. Hard to complain about that.
reactions: A quiet settlement is either peaceful or dead. Ours is loud and alive.

entries.intro: The well rope at the {poi_type} is fraying. I pointed it out and got a shrug in return.
details: It's worn thin at the pulley point: the part that takes the most stress. Obvious failure point.
Replacing a rope costs almost nothing. Fishing a bucket out of a well costs an afternoon.
reactions: Cheap prevention versus expensive cure. People always choose the cure.

entries.intro: I watched the {poi_type}'s crew repair a wall section last week. Fast, efficient, no wasted motion.
details: They had the damaged stones out and new ones fitted in half a day. Practiced hands.
It looked easy, which means it's hard and they've done it enough times to make it look simple.
reactions: Competence is invisible when it's good. You only notice when it's missing.

entries.intro: There's a scorch mark on the {poi_type}'s wall near the chimney. Old, faded, but still visible.
details: A spark caught something at some point. The stone is blackened in a fan-shaped pattern.
Whatever burned, it was small and brief. The building survived without drama.
reactions: Every building has a scar story. This one's is just more visible.

statCheck.pass: The soot pattern spreads upward and to the right: that tells you the wind was from the west when it happened. Embers carried from the chimney, probably.
statCheck.fail: Something burned there once. That's all I can read from it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s lintel stone is a single piece that must weigh as much as a horse. Massive.
details: I've stood under it and wondered how they got it up there. No crane marks, no obvious method.
It sits level and true, not a crack in it. Whoever placed it didn't get a second try.
reactions: Some construction achievements are invisible because they look effortless. This is one.

statCheck.pass: A stone that size would need a ramp and lever system: build up earth on both sides, slide the stone across, then dig the earth away. Simple in theory, brutal in practice.
statCheck.fail: Strong backs and stubborn people, I assume. I wasn't there.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is where I go to find {npc_name} when I need them. They're always there.
details: Rain or shine, morning or afternoon: same spot, same routine. Predictable as the sun.
I don't know if it's dedication or habit, but either way, I know where to look.
reactions: Reliable people at reliable places: the foundation of a settlement.

entries.intro: The cobblestones outside the {poi_type} are loose. Half of them rock when you step on them.
details: The sand bed underneath has washed out from years of runoff. The stones just sit on air.
Twist an ankle, drop a load: it's only a matter of time before someone gets hurt.
reactions: Loose footing is a slow hazard. Nobody fixes it until someone falls.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has new windows. Glass, not shutters. First real glass I've seen in a working building here.
details: The light inside is completely different now: brighter, more even, less gloomy.
Must have cost a fortune, but the difference is worth it.
reactions: Glass windows change a room the way a new season changes a landscape. Completely.

statCheck.pass: Glass comes from {other_settlement}: sand-blown, not cast. You can tell by the slight waviness. Expensive but they'll last decades with care.
statCheck.fail: I know glass is pricey. Beyond that, I don't know the sourcing.

entries.intro: The mud-brick wall behind the {poi_type} is dissolving. Every rain takes a little more.
details: The surface is pitted and crumbling: you can scrape it off with your fingers.
It was never meant to last this long. Mud brick is a temporary solution that became permanent.
reactions: Temporary fixes become permanent faster than permanent ones get started.

statCheck.pass: Mud brick needs a lime render to survive rain. Without it, each storm erodes a layer. They'd need to coat it or rebuild it in fired brick within a year.
statCheck.fail: Mud and rain don't mix. That's about all I know.

entries.intro: I passed the {poi_type} at midnight once. Just the glow of banked coals through the windows. Quiet as a church.
details: No workers, no noise: just the faint orange light and the building breathing in the dark.
It felt like seeing something private. A building at rest.
reactions: Everything needs to sleep. Even buildings.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} employs more people than any other building here. Half the settlement, it feels like.
details: When the shifts change, the paths are crowded with people coming and going. Like a tide.
The settlement's mood tracks with the {poi_type}'s output. Good week there, good week everywhere.
reactions: One building holding that much weight is either a strength or a vulnerability. Depends on the day.

statCheck.pass: If the {poi_type} closed for a month, a third of the settlement would have no income. That's not a guess: I've counted the families that depend on it.
statCheck.fail: A lot of people work there. How many exactly, I couldn't say.

entries.intro: The ladder at the {poi_type} has a rung missing. Third one from the top.
details: Everyone who uses it knows to skip it. Newcomers get a warning, usually in time.
Replacing a rung is a morning's work. The ladder's been rung-less for six months.
reactions: People adapt to broken things faster than they fix them. The ladder proves it.

entries.intro: The spring runoff carved a new channel past the {poi_type} this year. Water goes where water wants.
details: It cut a shallow trench through the packed earth: maybe a foot deep, enough to redirect foot traffic.
By summer it'll dry out and people will walk over it like it was never there.
reactions: Water reshapes a settlement one season at a time. We just walk around the changes.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a garden plot beside it now. Small, practical, nothing fancy.
details: Herbs, mostly. The kind of things you want on hand when you're working with your hands all day.
Someone's clearly tending it. Weeded, watered, cared for. A nice touch.
reactions: A bit of green next to a working building softens the whole area.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew had a disagreement out front this morning. Voices raised, arms waving.
details: It blew over in ten minutes. Handshakes and everyone back inside.
That's the nice thing about work arguments: they don't last. There's always something else to do.
reactions: People who argue and resolve it are healthier than people who simmer in silence.

entries.intro: The awning on the {poi_type} is torn. Flapping in the wind like a broken wing.
details: It was fine last week. One good gust and the fabric gave at the seam.
Now it snaps and cracks with every breeze. Annoying to hear, worse to look at.
reactions: Fabric and wind are natural enemies. Fabric always loses eventually.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s {resource_type} has a reputation beyond the settlement now. People ask for it by name.
details: Traders carry it specifically because buyers request it. That's a compliment you can't fake.
The workers know it too. There's a quiet confidence in the way they work.
reactions: Reputation built on consistent quality is the hardest to earn and the hardest to lose.

statCheck.pass: The secret is patience in the processing: they don't rush the final stage like everyone else does. The extra time shows in the finish.
statCheck.fail: They do something right. What exactly, I'm not the person to ask.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s chimney cap blew off in a storm. Smoke goes straight up now with nothing to direct it.
details: Rain gets in too. The fire hisses and spits whenever the sky opens up.
They've been working without the cap for two weeks. Nobody's climbed up to fix it.
reactions: A chimney without a cap is a chimney with an open mouth. Everything gets in.

entries.intro: I remember when the {poi_type} wasn't here. Just an empty lot and a foundation marker.
details: Watching it go up was watching the settlement decide to be more than it was.
It didn't happen fast. Months of work, setbacks, bad weather. But it happened.
reactions: I've seen this place grow. That's a privilege most people don't appreciate.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s floor is uneven. One corner sits lower than the rest.
details: Things roll toward it: tools, loose items, anything round. It's the building's collection point.
The workers keep a crate in the low corner to catch whatever drifts that way. Practical solution.
reactions: Perfection is for plans. Reality has low corners.

statCheck.pass: The foundation settled unevenly: the soil on that corner is softer, probably from an old filled-in pit. The weight above compressed it further over time.
statCheck.fail: Ground settled, I'd guess. Not really my problem to diagnose.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} gets the best breeze in the settlement. Something about where it sits.
details: On a hot day the air moves through like it's got somewhere to be. Natural ventilation.
People find excuses to visit on warm afternoons. The building is cooler than their homes.
reactions: Good placement is half the value of a building. This one lucked out.

statCheck.pass: It's positioned in a gap between two ridgelines that funnels wind. The builders might have known that, or they might have gotten lucky.
statCheck.fail: The wind's just stronger there. Don't ask me about terrain effects.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s storage bins are overflowing with {resource_type}. Stacked to the ceiling.
details: Good output, bad logistics. They're producing faster than they can ship.
It's a nice problem on paper. In practice, the workers are tripping over inventory.
reactions: Abundance without a plan is just clutter.

entries.intro: There's a worn patch on the {poi_type}'s doorframe where everyone grabs when they enter. The wood is polished.
details: Hundreds of hands over hundreds of days: the grain is smooth as glass in that one spot.
You reach for it without thinking. Your hand goes where everyone else's went.
reactions: Shared habits leave shared marks. That doorframe knows more hands than any of us do.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s output has been uneven this month. Good days and bad days, no pattern I can see.
details: One day the quality's perfect, next day it's off. The crew seems frustrated.
Inconsistency is harder to fix than a steady problem. At least a steady problem holds still.
reactions: Unpredictability wears people down faster than bad-but-steady ever could.

statCheck.pass: It's a material supply issue: the raw stock is arriving in mixed quality batches and they can't sort fast enough to keep the good separate from the poor.
statCheck.fail: I notice the ups and downs. The cause is above my pay grade.

entries.intro: The dew on the {poi_type}'s roof catches the first light. Thousands of tiny sparks every clear morning.
details: It lasts about twenty minutes before the sun dries it. Blink and the show's over.
I've made it part of my routine: step outside, look up, watch the roof glitter.
reactions: Free beauty on a schedule. All you have to do is wake up early enough.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s water barrel has algae growing in it. Green film on the surface, murky underneath.
details: Nobody's scrubbed it out because nobody wants to empty it first. Classic standoff.
The water's still used for cleaning, not drinking. But even so, it's not appetizing.
reactions: Shared resources rot when nobody feels personally responsible. Same as always.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s old and the {poi_type}'s ours. That's enough for me.
details: It doesn't need to be the best in the region. It needs to work and it does.
I've seen fancier buildings in other places that produced half as much. Style isn't substance.
reactions: Honest work in an honest building. What else do you need?

entries.intro: There's a specific spot near the {poi_type} where you can hear two conversations at once. Something about the acoustics.
details: Sound bounces off the wall at an angle and carries from around the corner. Stand in the right place and you hear everything.
I've learned more from that spot than I ever meant to. Not all of it pleasant.
reactions: Eavesdropping by architecture. Not my fault the building funnels sound.

statCheck.pass: The curved wall acts like a parabolic reflector: sound waves from one side converge at a focal point about ten paces away. Stand there and whispers become clear speech.
statCheck.fail: Something about the wall shape carries sound. I don't understand the physics.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} keeps its tools oiled and hung in order. Say what you will, they're organized in there.
details: Every tool has a peg and every peg is labeled. You can find what you need in the dark.
That kind of discipline doesn't happen by accident. Someone insists on it.
reactions: Order is a choice someone makes every day. I respect the commitment.

statCheck.pass: The senior worker started that system years ago after a tool went missing and cost them a full day of production. One bad day built a lifetime of organization.
statCheck.fail: They're just tidy people. I don't know the story behind it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s walls sweat in humid weather. Beads of moisture on the stone like the building's been running.
details: The air inside goes damp and heavy. Everything feels clammy to the touch.
Materials stored against the walls get moisture damage if they're not wrapped properly.
reactions: Stone and humidity have a complicated relationship. In this building, humidity wins.

statCheck.pass: It's condensation: the stone's thermal mass stays cool while the air warms up, and the moisture in the air condenses on the cold surface. Ventilation would fix it.
statCheck.fail: The walls get wet when it's humid. I don't know why stone does that.

entries.intro: I measured my steps from my door to the {poi_type} once. Exactly two hundred and twelve.
details: Don't ask me why I counted. I was bored and the walk felt longer than it should.
Now I can't walk there without counting. I've created a habit I didn't want.
reactions: The brain does strange things with repetition. Two hundred and twelve. Every time.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is showing its age. Nothing dramatic, just the slow accumulation of years.
details: Corners worn round, surfaces dulled, metal tarnished. The building is softening at the edges.
It still works perfectly. It just looks like something that's worked for a long time.
reactions: Age isn't decline. It's evidence.

entries.intro: The creek near the {poi_type} is high this season. Almost touching the bank.
details: The water moves fast and brown: carrying soil from uphill. Full of energy.
In dry years the same creek is barely a trickle. This year it has opinions.
reactions: High water means the rains have been generous. That's good news for everyone.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s latest hire is working out well, from what I hear. Picked things up fast.
details: The crew's been short-staffed for a while, so any help is good help.
Give it a month and they'll be pulling their own weight. Maybe more.
reactions: New blood in an old operation keeps it from going stale.

entries.intro: The soot stains above the {poi_type}'s windows tell you exactly where the airflow goes. Dark streaks pointing upward.
details: Years of smoke have painted a permanent record of the wind patterns on the stone.
It's not pretty, but it's informative. The building wears its history.
reactions: Soot doesn't lie. It goes where the air goes and stays where it lands.

statCheck.pass: The staining pattern shows the convective plume from each window vent: hot air rises and carries particles that deposit on the cooler stone above. You can read the ventilation layout from the stains.
statCheck.fail: Smoke leaves marks. That's about the depth of my understanding.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had its busiest day in months yesterday. Carts lined up, people everywhere.
details: I don't know what triggered the rush, but the crew handled it. Barely.
By evening they looked wrung out. Good wrung out, though. The kind that comes from earning your supper.
reactions: A busy day beats a quiet one. Ask anyone who's worked through both.

entries.intro: The old mortar at the {poi_type} is a different color from the patches. Grey original, white repairs.
details: You can map every repair by following the white lines. The building is striped with its own maintenance history.
Some walls are more patch than original now. At what point does the ship become the repairs?
reactions: A building that gets repaired is a building worth repairing. The patches are proof of value.

entries.intro: I tried the {resource_type} from the {poi_type}'s newest batch. It's different: harder, cleaner, better finished.
details: Something in the process changed and the result shows. Noticeable improvement.
If this is the new standard, we'll have something to brag about to the next trader.
reactions: Improvement is the best kind of surprise. Quiet, tangible, and hard to argue with.

statCheck.pass: They started pre-sorting the raw material before processing: pulling out the impurities that used to weaken the final product. Simple step, dramatic result.
statCheck.fail: It's better. Why, I couldn't explain.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is quieter on rainy days. The water muffles everything: the noise, the traffic, the urgency.
details: The crew works at a slower pace when it rains. Not laziness: just the weather's rhythm taking over.
I prefer it that way. The settlement feels gentler when the rain's in charge.
reactions: Rain slows everything down and nobody minds. We needed the excuse.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had smoke coming out of it before dawn today. Someone was in there early.
details: I don't know who starts that early, but the chimney was already going when I stepped outside.
The glow from the windows was the only light besides the stars. Looked cozy from a distance.
reactions: Early risers keep the world turning. I just wish I were one of them.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks better since they cleaned the stonework. You forget how light the original color was.
details: Years of grime just blending in until you don't notice it anymore. Then someone scrubs and it's like a new building.
The difference is most obvious around the windows where the soot was thickest.
reactions: Amazing what soap and effort can do. The place looks ten years younger.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s shutters are hanging crooked again. One good wind and they'll come off entirely.
details: The hinges have rusted through on the top one. It swings out and bangs against the wall.
I've heard it slam in the night three times this week. Wakes me up every time.
reactions: A nail and a new hinge pin: that's all it would take. But here we are.

statCheck.pass: The pins were iron, not brass, and the salt air ate through them. Whoever specced the hardware skimped on material.
statCheck.fail: Hinges break. That's about all I can tell you.

entries.intro: I like the way the {poi_type} sounds in the morning. Everything starting up at once, layered and purposeful.
details: Doors opening, tools being sorted, voices greeting each other. A place coming to life.
It builds gradually: one sound on top of another until the whole thing hums.
reactions: There's comfort in a place that starts the same way every day.

entries.intro: The drainage around the {poi_type} is a mess. Water pools there for days after any rain.
details: The puddles attract bugs and the mud gets tracked everywhere. It's an eyesore.
People have been laying planks across the worst spots, which helps but looks ridiculous.
reactions: A proper drain would solve it. Instead we have a boardwalk made of scraps.

statCheck.pass: The grade around the foundation is actually sloping inward: water runs toward the building instead of away. Regrading the last three paces out would fix it permanently.
statCheck.fail: Water goes where it wants. I don't know how you'd fix it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a new sign out front. Hand-painted, looks like someone actually cared.
details: The letters are clean and the colors are bright. First thing you notice walking up.
The old sign was so faded you could barely read it. This one you can see from the road.
reactions: Small improvements. They add up if people keep making them.

entries.intro: I passed the {poi_type} at an odd hour and caught the crew on break. They were laughing about something.
details: Sitting in a circle, eating, passing a water jug around. Just people being people.
I didn't interrupt. There's something private about a group's quiet time.
reactions: Good to see the folks there get along. Makes the work lighter.

entries.intro: The woodpile behind the {poi_type} is getting low. They're burning through it faster than usual.
details: A month ago it was stacked higher than the window. Now I can see over it without trying.
Somebody will need to make a supply run soon or they'll be cold by next week.
reactions: Wood disappears faster than you expect when the nights get cold.

statCheck.pass: They've been running double burns to keep the workspace at temperature. The process needs consistent heat or the quality drops.
statCheck.fail: I just noticed the pile shrinking. Could be anything.

entries.intro: The light inside the {poi_type} at night spills out the windows and makes patterns on the ground. Little squares of gold.
details: You can tell which rooms are occupied by where the light falls.
On dark nights those windows are the brightest thing on the street.
reactions: I like seeing it lit. Means someone's still working, still keeping the place alive.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a delivery of {resource_type} this morning. Took four people to haul it in.
details: Heavy loads coming through the door all morning. The workers barely had time to sort one before the next arrived.
By noon the yard was empty again and everything was inside. Efficient, I'll give them that.
reactions: Busy is better than idle. At least that's what I tell myself on long days.

entries.intro: Cats have taken over the area around the {poi_type}. There are at least four of them now.
details: They lounge in the sun on the warm stones and scatter when anyone gets close.
The workers leave scraps out for them. I think they enjoy the company.
reactions: Worse pests to have around. At least cats earn their keep chasing mice.

statCheck.pass: The cats showed up after the rat problem started. The workers encouraged them to stay because they keep the vermin out of the stores.
statCheck.fail: Cats go where they please. I don't know why they picked that spot.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s output has been steady all season. Nothing spectacular, nothing worrying.
details: People have stopped talking about it, which is probably the best compliment.
When something just works, it fades into the background.
reactions: Consistency is underrated: drama gets the attention, but reliability does the work.

statCheck.pass: The lead there quietly changed the maintenance schedule to preventative instead of reactive. Nobody noticed because nothing broke, which is exactly the point.
statCheck.fail: It's been steady. I don't know what they're doing differently.

entries.intro: The frost on the {poi_type}'s roof this morning was thick enough to see from the road. White as chalk.
details: The whole building looked like someone had dusted it with flour. Pretty, in a shivering sort of way.
By midmorning it was dripping off the eaves in a steady patter.
reactions: Winter doesn't ask permission. It just shows up and redecorates.

entries.intro: There's a puddle near the {poi_type} that never fully dries. It's been there since last autumn.
details: Even in the heat of summer it holds a thin film of water. Something underground must feed it.
Kids drop sticks in it and watch them float. Adults step over it and grumble.
reactions: It's become a landmark at this point. Turn left at the permanent puddle.

statCheck.pass: There's a natural spring feeding that low spot. The water table is close to the surface there: you'd have to drain it properly or it'll just keep coming back.
statCheck.fail: Water collects there and stays. Don't ask me why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been turning out decent {resource_type}. Nothing to brag about, but solid.
details: Reliable quality, no surprises. Exactly what you want from a working operation.
I've stopped checking the batches as closely because they've been consistent.
reactions: When things just work, the smart move is to leave them alone.

entries.intro: Someone planted herbs along the path to the {poi_type}. Lavender and sage, by the smell.
details: They're thriving in the gritty soil by the roadside. Tougher plants than they look.
The whole approach smells better than it has any right to. Pleasant surprise.
reactions: A bit of effort in a small spot. You notice it every time you walk past.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s doorframe has split. There's a crack running top to bottom through the lintel.
details: You can see daylight through it from inside. The gap widens when it's hot and shrinks when it's cold.
They've wedged something in to hold it together for now. Temporary fix that's lasted six months.
reactions: Nothing is more permanent than a temporary solution.

statCheck.pass: The lintel is bearing more load than it was meant for: the wall above has settled unevenly. Replacing the timber would buy time, but the real fix is repointing the stonework above it.
statCheck.fail: Something cracked and they stuffed it. That's the extent of my structural knowledge.

entries.intro: I watched the sunrise from near the {poi_type} this morning. The light hit the walls and turned them gold.
details: For about ten minutes everything had that warm, clean look. Even the mud puddles were beautiful.
The workers coming in didn't notice. They had their heads down and their minds on the day.
reactions: Beautiful moments don't wait for you to be ready. You catch them or you don't.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} was nearly empty yesterday. Just one person inside, working alone.
details: Usually there's a full crew. Seeing it so quiet felt wrong, like a market without stalls.
I asked around and apparently most of the crew had the same stomach trouble. Bad timing.
reactions: One person held the whole place together for a day. That's worth remembering.

statCheck.pass: They all ate from the same pot at lunch the day before. Bad batch of something: took out the whole crew except the one who skipped the meal.
statCheck.fail: People got sick. That's all I know.

entries.intro: The bird nests under the eaves of the {poi_type} are back again this year. Same spot as always.
details: You can hear the chicks peeping from the road when it's quiet. Busy little family up there.
The workers have learned to duck under that section. Nobody wants a surprise from above.
reactions: They picked a good spot: warm, sheltered, close to food. Smarter than some people I know.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} stinks after a hot day. Something about the heat and the materials and the sweat just combines wrong.
details: You can smell it downwind from fifty paces. The kind of smell that gets into your hair.
In winter it's fine. But summer afternoons are rough.
reactions: The people who work there don't seem to notice anymore. I envy their noses.

statCheck.pass: The waste water from processing sits in a catch basin out back. In the heat it stagnates and the anaerobic breakdown produces that smell.
statCheck.fail: It stinks in summer. I don't know the chemistry.

entries.intro: They replaced the door at the {poi_type}. The new one is thicker, heavier: actually latches properly now.
details: The old door had been shimmed and shaved so many times it barely fit the frame.
This one closes with a solid thud instead of a rattle. Small improvement, big satisfaction.
reactions: A door that works properly: you'd be surprised how much that matters.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been getting more foot traffic from travelers lately. Word is spreading, I suppose.
details: I keep seeing faces I don't recognize asking directions to it. Outsiders, passing through.
Good for trade, but the locals have to wait longer for service.
reactions: Growth is a nice problem to have, even if it doesn't always feel like it.

statCheck.pass: A trader mentioned our {poi_type} in {other_settlement} and now people are making the trip. Free advertising, I suppose.
statCheck.fail: More people showing up. Don't ask me why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s floor creaks in the same spots it always has. Right inside the door and by the back wall.
details: Everyone who goes there regularly knows the creaky boards and steps around them without thinking.
New visitors always hit both spots. You can tell who's been there before by their footwork.
reactions: Those floorboards are the world's laziest alarm system.

statCheck.pass: The joists underneath are sound: the creak is just the nails loosening in the subfloor. A few new fasteners would silence it, but nobody's bothered.
statCheck.fail: Floors creak when they're old. That's the depth of my carpentry knowledge.

entries.intro: I've been a {self_role} long enough to appreciate what the {poi_type} does for this place. We'd be lost without it.
details: Half my work depends on what comes out of there. When they slow down, I slow down.
I don't think people realize how connected everything is until one piece stops working.
reactions: You take the supply chain for granted until a link goes missing.

entries.intro: The weeds around the {poi_type} are winning. Knee-high in places, growing right up the walls.
details: Nobody's pulled them in months. The place looks abandoned even though it's not.
There's ivy creeping up one side now. Another season and it'll cover the windows.
reactions: Nature doesn't care about property lines. It moves in the second you look away.

statCheck.pass: That ivy will get into the mortar joints and pry the stonework apart over time. Better to pull it now while the roots are shallow.
statCheck.fail: Weeds grow. That's about all I know about plants.

entries.intro: I picked up some {resource_type} from the {poi_type} this morning. Quality's been slipping a bit.
details: Not terrible, just not what it was a few months ago. A noticeable step down.
I didn't say anything. Nobody likes hearing their work isn't up to standard.
reactions: It's probably a phase. I'll give it another month before I start worrying.

statCheck.pass: I think they're pushing quantity over quality to build up reserves. Understandable, but the tradeoff shows in the finish.
statCheck.fail: I'm no expert. It just doesn't feel the same in my hands.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had its chimney swept last week. Runs cleaner now: thin white smoke instead of that thick grey.
details: You could tell the difference immediately. The air around the place is noticeably fresher.
Should have been done months ago, but at least someone finally got around to it.
reactions: Maintenance isn't exciting, but the results speak for themselves.

entries.intro: The steps at the {poi_type} are worn smooth from years of feet. You can see the dip in the middle of each one.
details: Thousands of people walking the same path, wearing the stone down one footfall at a time.
In wet weather those smooth spots get slick. I've seen more than one person catch themselves.
reactions: The stone remembers every step even if we don't.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks good after the rain. Everything washed clean, the stones darker, the colors deeper.
details: Even the dust is gone for once. The whole place has that scrubbed look.
Give it three dry days and it'll be back to normal. But for now it shines.
reactions: Rain is the cheapest cleaning crew you'll ever hire.

entries.intro: Someone stacked a pile of broken tools outside the {poi_type}. Been there for a week now.
details: Bent handles, cracked heads, snapped blades. The leftovers of hard use and no replacements.
I think they're waiting for someone to haul it to the scrap pile, but nobody's volunteered.
reactions: Broken tools tell you a lot about how hard people are working.

statCheck.pass: The tool quality has been dropping for a while: softer metal, greener wood handles. The supplier changed and nobody adjusted their technique.
statCheck.fail: Tools break. That's all I see.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a good batch of {resource_type} come through yesterday. Best I've seen this season.
details: You could tell by how carefully the crew was handling it. When the material is good, people treat it like it matters.
Word got around fast. Half the settlement stopped by before end of day.
reactions: A good batch lifts everyone's mood. Even people who don't use {resource_type} directly seemed happier.

entries.intro: The wall behind the {poi_type} has been tagged with charcoal drawings. Kids, probably.
details: Little stick figures and animals and something that might be a dragon, or possibly a horse.
The rain will wash it off eventually, but for now the {poi_type} has a mural.
reactions: I think it's charming. Not everyone agrees.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s window is propped open with a stick. Same stick, same window, every single day.
details: It's become such a fixture that I'd notice if it were missing. Part of the building's personality now.
The latch must be broken. Or maybe someone just prefers the low-tech solution.
reactions: If it works, it works. Nobody needs a fancy solution for a simple problem.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} runs shorter hours now that the days are getting shorter. Makes sense, but it's an adjustment.
details: I showed up last week after they'd already closed. Stood there like a fool staring at a locked door.
People are rearranging their errands around the new schedule. Takes a few weeks to settle.
reactions: Seasons change, hours change, routines change. We catch up eventually.

statCheck.pass: The lamp oil costs more than the extra output is worth. Shorter hours in winter is just economics.
statCheck.fail: Less daylight, less work. Seems straightforward enough.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s roof is growing moss. A thick green strip right along the ridge.
details: It started small last year and now it's a proper carpet. Holds water and weighs the timbers down.
From a distance it almost looks intentional. Like someone planned a green roof.
reactions: Moss doesn't care about your plans. It grows where it's happy.

statCheck.pass: Moss traps moisture against the roofing material and accelerates rot. If they scrape it off and treat the surface with lye, it won't come back for a season or two.
statCheck.fail: It's moss: it grows. That's about the extent of my expertise.

entries.intro: I sat outside the {poi_type} yesterday just watching people come and go. Better than anything I had planned.
details: Everyone has their own pace. Some rush in, some linger, some stop to chat at the door.
You learn a lot about a place by watching who visits it and how.
reactions: Idle observation is underrated. Sometimes doing nothing is the most interesting thing available.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} got new lanterns outside. Brighter than the old ones by far.
details: The whole area feels safer at night now. You can actually see where you're walking.
Some people are complaining that the light leaks into their windows. Can't please everyone.
reactions: I'll take too bright over too dark. My shins have had enough.

entries.intro: The stone on the {poi_type}'s south side is a different color than the north. Warmer, almost golden.
details: Sun bleaching over years: the south face gets all the light and the north stays in shadow.
It looks like two different buildings depending on which direction you approach from.
reactions: The sun rewrites a building as surely as any stonemason. Just takes longer.

statCheck.pass: UV light breaks down the iron compounds in the stone surface: the south side oxidizes to that warm tone while the north side keeps its original grey.
statCheck.fail: One side's lighter. Sun does that, I think.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is running behind schedule this week. People are grumbling, but quietly.
details: Orders are backed up and the crew looks frazzled. More snapping at each other than usual.
I've been waiting on something from there for three days. Not complaining: just noting.
reactions: Bad weeks happen. The measure of a place is how it handles the next one.

statCheck.pass: They lost a key tool to breakage and the replacement hasn't come in yet. The whole workflow bottlenecks without it.
statCheck.fail: I don't know the cause. Just the effect.

entries.intro: The view of the {poi_type} from the road is nice this time of year. Trees frame it just right.
details: When the leaves are full the building sits in a little pocket of green. Like it was planned that way.
In winter when the branches are bare it's just a building on a hill. Not the same.
reactions: Context changes everything. The same place can be ordinary or lovely depending on the season.

entries.intro: Someone at the {poi_type} has been burning something foul. The smoke is acrid and thick.
details: I don't know what it is, but my eyes water when the wind blows this way.
The neighbors have noticed too. There's been talk about saying something.
reactions: Burning the wrong thing is everyone's problem when the wind shifts.

statCheck.pass: That's treated wood: they're probably burning scrap that still has pitch or sealant on it. Bad for the lungs and the chimney both.
statCheck.fail: I don't know what makes smoke smell like that. Just that it's unpleasant.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s water barrel finally got replaced. The old one was held together by habit alone.
details: One of the staves had split and they were losing half their water to the ground.
The new one is tight and full. Amazing what a difference sound equipment makes.
reactions: You forget how things are supposed to work until something actually works properly again.

entries.intro: The mud near the {poi_type} has a particular quality. Thick and sticky: the kind that pulls your boot off.
details: I lost a shoe there once and had to fish it out by hand. Not my finest moment.
The workers lay boards down in the worst spots, but the mud eats those too eventually.
reactions: Mud is patient. It waits for you to stop paying attention and then it strikes.

statCheck.pass: It's the clay content in that soil: mixed with runoff from the building it becomes a paste that grips anything. Gravel would help but you'd need a cartload.
statCheck.fail: Sticky mud. That's about as deep as my analysis goes.

entries.intro: I like the rhythm the {poi_type} gives to the day. Open in the morning, busy by noon, quiet by evening.
details: You can set your life around it like a clock. The settlement breathes in time with that place.
When the routine breaks, the whole day feels off. Like a song with a wrong note.
reactions: Routine gets a bad name, but it's really just structure wearing comfortable clothes.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} echoes. You can hear conversations from across the building bouncing off the stone.
details: People who work there have learned to keep their voices down. The walls share everything.
I said something once from the doorway and heard it repeated back to me three times.
reactions: Not the best place for a private conversation.

statCheck.pass: The high ceiling and hard surfaces create a reverb chamber. A few hangings or curtains would break up the sound, but the crew seems to have adapted instead.
statCheck.fail: It's echoey in there. That's about all I can tell you.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had its best month in a long time, from what I hear. Everyone's in good spirits.
details: The crew's been working hard and it finally paid off. You can see it in how they carry themselves.
Even the people who don't work there directly feel the lift. Good results spread.
reactions: Success has a way of loosening the tension in a place. Everyone stands a little straighter.

entries.intro: The gutter on the {poi_type} drains right onto the footpath. A curtain of water every time it rains.
details: You either sprint through it or walk all the way around. No dignified option.
I've seen people stand there waiting for a gap in the flow like it's going to stop.
reactions: A downspout extension: that's all it needs. But who am I to say.

statCheck.pass: The original drain ran underground to a soakaway, but the pipe cracked years ago. Now the water has nowhere to go but the path.
statCheck.fail: Water falls where the gutter points. That's about as much as I understand.

entries.intro: {npc_name} mentioned the {poi_type} is doing well. Said it casually, but I could tell they were proud.
details: I think they feel responsible for the place even if it's not entirely theirs to claim.
Pride in local things is good. Means people care about where they live.
reactions: When someone brags about their town working, that's the kind of pride I can get behind.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} was loud today. Louder than usual: heavy hammering, clattering, raised voices.
details: I think they were moving something big inside. The whole building seemed to vibrate.
By late afternoon it settled back to normal. Whatever it was, they got it done.
reactions: A loud day usually means a productive one. I've made my peace with the noise.

entries.intro: The chicken that wanders near the {poi_type} was on the roof again this morning. Nobody knows how it gets up there.
details: It just sits there looking down at people like it owns the place. Completely unbothered.
The workers have named it. I won't repeat the name, but it suits the bird.
reactions: Every workplace needs a mascot. Even an uninvited one.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} sits in a low spot that collects cold air on still nights. You can feel the temperature drop walking toward it.
details: The frost lingers there longer than anywhere else in the settlement. Still white at noon sometimes.
In winter the difference is dramatic: ten degrees colder in that pocket than on the ridge.
reactions: Whoever picked that site didn't account for the cold sink. Or maybe they didn't care.

statCheck.pass: Cold air is heavier than warm air and flows downhill like water, pooling in that hollow. You'd need a windbreak to redirect the flow.
statCheck.fail: Some spots are just colder. I don't know the science.

entries.intro: The crowd at the {poi_type} was thinner than usual today. I could actually get in without waiting.
details: I don't know if people are busy elsewhere or just not bothering today.
I took advantage and got my business done in half the usual time.
reactions: I'm not going to question a short line. I'm just going to enjoy it.

statCheck.pass: Market day drew most people to the other end of town. The {poi_type} always clears out when there's commerce to attend to.
statCheck.fail: Fewer people than usual. I didn't think about why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} uses more water than any other building in the settlement. You can see the runoff.
details: There's always a wet trail running from the back of the building downhill. Constant.
I don't know what process uses that much water, but the ground downstream stays green year-round.
reactions: At least something benefits. The grass near the {poi_type} is the lushest in town.

statCheck.pass: The water is used for cooling during processing. It comes out warm and mineral-rich, which is why the vegetation thrives on the runoff.
statCheck.fail: They use a lot of water. That's all I know.

entries.intro: I helped repair the fence around the {poi_type} last weekend. Took most of the day.
details: The posts were solid but the rails had rotted through. We pulled them out in pieces.
A few of us pitched in without being asked. That's the kind of place this is.
reactions: Honest labor for a shared space. Felt good to do something useful with my hands.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s waste pile is getting out of hand. It's spilling past where it should be.
details: Scraps, shavings, broken bits: all just heaped up and spreading. Nobody's hauled it away.
The smell isn't great either, especially when the wind comes from that direction.
reactions: Waste management isn't glamorous, but ignoring it doesn't make it go away.

statCheck.pass: The carter who used to haul waste moved on and nobody's hired a replacement. The job pays badly and smells worse.
statCheck.fail: Waste piles up when nobody moves it. Mystery solved.

entries.intro: The stonework on the {poi_type} is really fine if you take the time to look. Clean joints, even courses.
details: Somebody who knew their trade built that wall. The craftsmanship is obvious up close.
You don't appreciate good masonry until you've seen bad masonry. And I've seen plenty of bad.
reactions: Skill like that outlasts the person who did the work. The building carries their reputation.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been quieter lately: not dead, just subdued.
details: Fewer people coming and going. The ones who do seem to move with less purpose.
I'm not sure if something happened or if it's just a slow spell.
reactions: Every place has its off seasons. I'm not reading into it yet.

statCheck.pass: They're between projects: the last big order was filled and the next one hasn't come in yet.
statCheck.fail: It's quiet and I don't know why. That's about it.

entries.intro: I can smell the {poi_type} before I can see it. The scent carries on the wind.
details: It's not unpleasant: earthy, warm, with a trace of whatever they're working with today.
I've started using the smell as a navigation aid. Useful when the fog rolls in.
reactions: Noses are underrated as wayfinding tools.

entries.intro: The door to the {poi_type} squeaks every time it opens. You can hear it from the street.
details: It's become a kind of announcement system: squeak means someone arrived, squeak means someone left.
I've offered grease for the hinges twice and been told they like it that way.
reactions: I suppose a squeaky door is cheaper than a doorbell.

entries.intro: The {resource_type} output from the {poi_type} has been strong all month. Real strong.
details: The crew is in a groove. You can feel the confidence in how they work.
People are less anxious about supplies when the {poi_type} is pulling like this.
reactions: Abundance makes everything easier: decisions, moods, conversations. All of it.

entries.intro: The scaffolding around the {poi_type} has been up for weeks. I've stopped noticing it.
details: Whatever they're fixing, it's taking longer than anyone expected. The scaffolding has become part of the view.
Kids climb on it when nobody's watching. It's a playground now, for better or worse.
reactions: At some point the scaffolding becomes more permanent than the repair.

statCheck.pass: The materials for the actual repair are backordered. The scaffolding went up on schedule but the work can't start until the stone arrives.
statCheck.fail: It's taking a while. I don't know the details.

entries.intro: The flag on the {poi_type} is new. Bright colors, not yet faded by sun and wind.
details: The old one had been a rag for months. Barely recognizable as a flag at all.
Give this one a year and it'll look the same. The weather here doesn't spare anything.
reactions: Everything starts bright. What matters is whether someone replaces it when it fades.

entries.intro: {npc_name} was complaining about the {poi_type} again. Something about the schedule changing.
details: I only caught half the conversation, but it sounded like the new hours don't suit anyone.
When even {npc_name} is bothered, you know it's a real inconvenience.
reactions: Change is hard when you've built your day around the old way.

statCheck.pass: The schedule shift was to accommodate a supply run that comes in later now. The whole day pushes back an hour and everyone downstream feels it.
statCheck.fail: Something about hours. I didn't catch the details.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is warm inside even on cold days. They keep a fire going all the time.
details: People linger there longer than they need to in winter. Hard to blame them.
There's an unspoken rule: you can sit by the fire as long as you're not in the way.
reactions: A warm place to stand is worth more than people realize until it's cold outside.

entries.intro: There are boot prints all over the ground near the {poi_type}. Hundreds of them, overlapping, going every direction.
details: In soft ground you can read the traffic like a map. Where people pause, where they turn, where they hurry.
After a busy day it looks like an army marched through. Just ordinary workers, but still.
reactions: All those footprints and everyone ends up at the same place. Says something about routine.

statCheck.pass: The deepest prints are always in the same three spots: the door, the loading area, and the shortcut from the east. That's where the ground compacts first each season.
statCheck.fail: Lots of boot prints. I notice them but that's about it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} smells like fresh-cut wood today. Must be doing repairs or building something new.
details: That sawdust smell carries halfway across the settlement when they're cutting lumber.
Sawdust piles up outside the door in little golden drifts. Almost pretty.
reactions: I like the smell of cut wood. Reminds me the place is being looked after.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been noisy at odd hours. Not the usual daytime bustle: more like nighttime tinkering.
details: Clanking after dark, a lamp in the window past midnight. Someone's putting in extra hours.
It's not bothering me exactly. Just unusual enough to notice.
reactions: Whoever's burning the midnight oil in there must have a reason. I hope it's a good one.

statCheck.pass: They're behind on a time-sensitive order: the material has to be processed within a window or it degrades. Night shifts are the only way to stay on schedule.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why they're working late. Not my business.

entries.intro: A {self_role} like me ends up at the {poi_type} more often than most. It's practically a second home.
details: I know every corner, every sound, every quirk of the place. Comes with the territory.
The crew there greets me by name. We've spent enough hours in the same building to be family.
reactions: You can't spend that much time somewhere without feeling attached to it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s getting crowded. More people using it than it was built for.
details: You have to wait your turn for everything now. Space is tight and tempers are short.
It's a good problem in theory: demand outstripping supply. In practice, it's just frustrating.
reactions: Growth is uncomfortable before it's good.

statCheck.pass: The building was sized for maybe two-thirds of the current demand. An annex would solve it but nobody wants to fund the construction.
statCheck.fail: Too many people, not enough space. That much is obvious.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been getting compliments from traders passing through. Apparently we're doing something right.
details: A merchant told me last week that the {resource_type} from here is better than what they see elsewhere.
It's nice to hear from an outsider. We're too close to it to see our own quality.
reactions: Outside perspective is valuable. We forget what we have when we see it every day.

entries.intro: The rain barrel at the {poi_type} overflowed last night. Made a mess of the entrance.
details: Water everywhere, mud tracking inside. The first hour of the morning was just cleanup.
Someone forgot to drain it before the storm. Simple mistake, big consequence.
reactions: Most problems start with one small thing nobody thought to check.

statCheck.pass: The overflow pipe was clogged with debris. The barrel had no way to shed excess water, so it spilled over the rim instead.
statCheck.fail: It overflowed. I don't know the plumbing details.

entries.intro: The sunset light through the {poi_type}'s windows throws colored shapes on the ground outside.
details: The glass is old and uneven, so the light bends and splits. Little rainbows on the dirt.
It only happens for about twenty minutes in the evening, then the angle changes.
reactions: Imperfection in the glass making something beautiful. There's probably a lesson in that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s inventory is low right now. Shelves looking sparse.
details: Not empty, but you can see gaps where things should be. The usual fullness is missing.
Nobody's panicking. It's happened before and it sorted itself out.
reactions: Lean times come and go. Worrying about them doesn't fill the shelves.

statCheck.pass: The seasonal supply routes slow down this time of year. Deliveries pick back up in a few weeks when the roads improve.
statCheck.fail: Stock is low. Could be any number of reasons.

entries.intro: I walked past the {poi_type} in the early morning before anyone was there. Peaceful in a way it never is during the day.
details: Just the building and the birds and the light coming up. No noise, no bustle, no purpose.
For a few minutes it was just a shape in the landscape, not a workplace.
reactions: Places have personalities when they're empty. Different from when they're full.

entries.intro: The path to the {poi_type} has been graded and smoothed. About time someone got to it.
details: No more dodging ruts or hopping over tree roots. You can walk there like a civilized person.
It must have taken days of work. Quiet work, too: I didn't notice until it was done.
reactions: Infrastructure work: nobody celebrates it, everyone benefits from it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s foundation stones are sinking on one side. You can see the lean if you step back.
details: It's subtle, but once you notice it you can't unsee it. The roofline tilts.
The building still functions fine. It's just slowly becoming a parallelogram.
reactions: Gravity doesn't negotiate. It just works, year after year, until the math wins.

statCheck.pass: The soil on the low side is softer clay that compresses under load. Underpinning the foundation now would stabilize it, but it's not cheap work.
statCheck.fail: One side is lower than the other. I can see that much, but I don't know the cause.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew brought in a new worktable. Carried it in through the big door themselves.
details: Took six of them to manage it. Heavy oak, well-made, built to last.
The old one had a wobble that drove everyone mad. This one sits flat.
reactions: A solid worktable under your hands changes how you feel about the work.

entries.intro: I noticed the {poi_type}'s chimney is leaning. Just slightly, but it wasn't like that last year.
details: The mortar between the upper courses has crumbled and the stack is drifting away from the roofline.
Nobody else seems concerned. Maybe I'm the only one looking up.
reactions: Chimneys don't lean back on their own. That's only going one direction.

statCheck.pass: The flashing where the chimney meets the roof has separated, letting water in. Freeze-thaw cycles are pushing the mortar apart and the whole stack is tilting as a result.
statCheck.fail: It leans. I don't know much about chimneys beyond that.

entries.intro: The air around the {poi_type} feels different. Warmer, drier, carrying the heat of whatever's going on inside.
details: You can feel the temperature shift as you walk past. Like stepping near an oven.
In winter it's actually pleasant. In summer it's one more reason to cross the street.
reactions: The building leaks heat the way gossip leaks through walls: steadily and in all directions.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s yard has been cleared and swept. Looks like they actually care about the place.
details: Tools racked, debris gone, the ground even raked. Orderly for the first time in months.
I don't know what prompted it, but the whole area looks more respectable.
reactions: When a place is tidy, people treat it differently. Higher standard of behavior.

entries.intro: I brought {npc_name} past the {poi_type} yesterday. They'd never actually looked at it up close.
details: They were surprised by the size of it. You forget when you see something every day.
Funny how a familiar place can seem new through someone else's eyes.
reactions: We all need a reminder to actually look at where we live once in a while.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been producing more waste than usual. You can see it piling up out back.
details: More output means more leftovers. The math is simple even if the mess isn't.
They're supposed to haul it away weekly, but it's been longer than that.
reactions: Productivity has a shadow side. Someone has to deal with what's left over.
