statCheck.pass: The waste ratio went up because they switched to a faster process that's less material-efficient. More throughput, more scrap.
statCheck.fail: More waste. Couldn't tell you why.

entries.intro: The smoke from the {poi_type} drifts straight up on still mornings. A perfect column against the sky.
details: When there's no wind it just rises like a line drawn with chalk. Steady and unbroken.
You can watch it climb until it fades into nothing. It's oddly hypnotic.
reactions: There's something calming about watching smoke on a windless morning. Proof that the day is still.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks different since they whitewashed the entrance. Brighter, more inviting.
details: The contrast between the bright doorway and the weathered walls is striking. Draws the eye.
I think they're trying to make the place feel more welcoming. It's working.
reactions: First impressions matter, even for buildings. A clean entrance says 'we care about this.'

entries.intro: The ground near the {poi_type} vibrates when they're working at full tilt. You can feel it through your feet.
details: It's a low hum, barely perceptible, but once you notice it you can't ignore it.
The houses nearby have learned to live with it. Cups rattle on shelves sometimes.
reactions: A working town has a heartbeat. That rumble is ours.

statCheck.pass: The heavy equipment transfers vibration through the foundation into the ground. It's worse in wet weather when the soil conducts better.
statCheck.fail: The ground shakes a bit. I couldn't tell you why.

entries.intro: The last {resource_type} I got from the {poi_type} was above average. Pleased with it.
details: Heavier than expected, well-finished, no obvious flaws. The kind of quality you remember.
I don't say it often because standards fluctuate, but this batch earned the compliment.
reactions: Good work deserves notice. Too easy to only speak up when things go wrong.

entries.intro: The lock on the {poi_type} has been sticking. Takes three tries and a shoulder shove to open.
details: The mechanism is old and the keyhole has worn oval. The key barely catches anymore.
The first person to arrive each morning fights with it for five minutes. You can hear the cursing.
reactions: A lock that doesn't lock: philosophy or negligence, depending on your perspective.

statCheck.pass: The keyway is brass and the key is iron: different metals, different wear rates. The harder key has slowly carved the softer lock into uselessness.
statCheck.fail: It's old and doesn't work properly. That's as technical as I get.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a full crew for the first time in a while. All hands, nobody missing.
details: You could hear the difference: more voices, more clanging, more energy. A busy, healthy sound.
The output was noticeably higher. Funny what happens when you have enough people.
reactions: Full staffing shouldn't feel like a holiday, but around here it does.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s walls are warm to the touch in the afternoon. Stored heat from the sun.
details: I leaned against the building while waiting and could feel it radiating through my shirt.
The stone holds heat for hours after sunset. The building stays warm even when the air doesn't.
reactions: Stone has a good memory for warmth. Better than most people I know.

entries.intro: There's a loose stone above the {poi_type}'s door that wobbles every time someone slams it.
details: I've watched it shimmy and settle back a hundred times. One day it won't settle back.
I mentioned it to the crew and got shrugged at. They've got bigger worries, apparently.
reactions: It'll become a priority the day it falls on someone. That's usually how it works.

statCheck.pass: The mortar was mixed with too much sand: it's crumbly instead of bonded. A repoint with proper lime mortar would lock that stone in permanently.
statCheck.fail: It wobbles. That's all I can see from the ground.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} got a delivery of fresh timber. The whole yard smells like pine.
details: Stacked neatly, bark still on, still oozing sap. Whoever supplied it cut recently.
The workers were inspecting each piece, picking out the best ones.
reactions: New materials mean new projects. The place is looking forward instead of back.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has water damage on the ceiling. You can see the stain spreading from inside.
details: It's that brownish-yellow ring that means moisture is getting in from above.
The plaster underneath is soft and bulging. It'll come down eventually.
reactions: Water finds every weakness in a building. Patient and relentless.

statCheck.pass: There's a gap in the roofing where two sections overlap: water runs along the rafters until it pools at the lowest point. A patch from above would stop it.
statCheck.fail: The ceiling's wet. I don't know where the water comes from.

entries.intro: Somebody hung wind chimes near the {poi_type}. They ring in the breeze like tiny bells.
details: Metal ones, not wood: a bright, clear sound that carries surprisingly far.
Some people think they're charming, others want them taken down. The argument is ongoing.
reactions: I like them. But then I don't live next door.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the tallest building you can see from the main road. Stands above everything else.
details: Travelers use it as a landmark. You can see the peak from well outside the settlement.
On clear days the shadow it casts stretches across half the street.
reactions: It anchors the skyline. Hard to imagine the view without it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew was celebrating something yesterday. Cheering and clapping from inside.
details: I couldn't tell what the occasion was, but the mood was infectious. Even passersby were smiling.
They were still in good spirits when they came out at the end of the day.
reactions: Good cheer deserves to spread. It's rare enough that you should enjoy it when it shows up.

entries.intro: The cobbles near the {poi_type} are uneven. Three of them have sunk and the rest are lifting at the edges.
details: Cart wheels catch on the high spots and your ankle turns on the low ones.
I've seen at least four people stumble there this week. Same spot every time.
reactions: The cobbles aren't going to fix themselves, but hope springs eternal around here.

statCheck.pass: Tree roots from the large elm are growing under the cobbles and heaving them up. You'd need to cut the roots or lose the pavement.
statCheck.fail: They're uneven. I don't know what's pushing them around.

entries.intro: I sat on the wall near the {poi_type} and just watched the clouds go by. Best half hour of my week.
details: The vantage there is good: you can see the sky from horizon to horizon.
Nobody asked what I was doing. I think they assumed I was thinking deep thoughts.
reactions: Sometimes doing nothing in the right spot is exactly enough.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s drainage ditch has clogged again. Water backing up against the foundation.
details: Leaves and sediment block it every season. Someone clears it, the rain fills it, repeat.
It's a twice-yearly chore that nobody enjoys and everybody depends on.
reactions: Maintenance is a conversation with entropy. Entropy always gets the last word.

statCheck.pass: A simple grate over the inlet would catch the debris before it clogs the channel. Costs next to nothing and saves hours of digging each year.
statCheck.fail: It fills up and has to be cleared. That's all I know about drainage.

entries.intro: The early light hits the {poi_type} at just the right angle to silhouette the whole building against the dawn.
details: For a few minutes it's just an outline: sharp, black, perfectly still against the color.
Then the sun clears the ridge and it's just a building again. But that moment stays.
reactions: I'm not usually awake for it. But when I am, it's the best part of the day.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been getting complaints about noise. Not from me, but from the people who live nearby.
details: It's a working building: working buildings make noise, and that's how it goes.
I understand both sides, but there's not much to be done without shutting the place down.
reactions: Living near a workplace means living with the sounds of work. That's the trade.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is dark inside even on bright days. Something about the window placement.
details: The windows are small and set deep in the walls. The light barely makes it halfway across the room.
They burn lamps all day just to see what they're doing. Seems like a waste.
reactions: Whoever designed the building wasn't thinking about daylight. Or maybe they had their reasons.

statCheck.pass: The small windows were deliberate: better insulation and wind protection. The builders traded natural light for thermal stability, which makes sense for the climate.
statCheck.fail: It's dark in there. I don't know why they built it that way.

entries.intro: I heard the {poi_type} is ahead of schedule for the season. First time in years, apparently.
details: The crew's been efficient. Fewer delays, fewer problems, just steady output day after day.
I think the new workflow is paying off. Whatever they changed, it's working.
reactions: Success is just repetition without mistakes. Sounds simple, isn't.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s back wall is peeling. Paint flaking off in big curled strips.
details: Nobody sees the back of the building, so nobody fixes the back of the building.
I only noticed because I was cutting through the alley. It looks rough.
reactions: The front face gets all the attention. The back gets all the weather.

statCheck.pass: The paint on that side gets direct sun and rain in alternation. It bakes, soaks, bakes again, and the expansion cycles peel the layers apart.
statCheck.fail: Paint peels. I don't know why that side is worse.

entries.intro: The wind through the {poi_type}'s open windows carries the sound of work out into the street.
details: Conversations, tool sounds, the occasional laugh. A cross-section of someone's day floating past.
You can chart the mood of the place just by walking by with your ears open.
reactions: Open windows are honest. They don't let a building keep secrets.

entries.intro: {npc_name} dropped off a big delivery at the {poi_type} this morning. Looked heavy.
details: They were in and out quick. Business-like, no chatting.
The crew at the {poi_type} seemed happy to see the supplies. Must have been running low.
reactions: When the supply chain works, everyone breathes easier.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s well pump has been groaning. Makes this low, painful sound every time someone uses it.
details: The handle moves harder than it should. Takes real effort to draw water now.
I've heard people say it'll seize up completely soon if nobody greases the mechanism.
reactions: A pump that groans is a pump that's asking for help. Loudly.

statCheck.pass: The leather washer inside is dried and cracked, so you're fighting air pressure with every stroke. A new washer would have it working smooth again.
statCheck.fail: Pumps make noise when they're unhappy. That's the full extent of my knowledge.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s best season was three years ago. People still talk about it.
details: Record output, high quality, everyone proud of the work. A peak you don't forget.
They haven't matched it since, but they're not far off. Close enough to keep hoping.
reactions: A good year sets the standard. Everything after is measured against it.

statCheck.pass: That season they had an experienced lead who reorganized the workflow. When they left, the efficiency dropped back and nobody's recovered it.
statCheck.fail: Three years ago was good. I don't know what made it special.

entries.intro: There's a marking scratched into the {poi_type}'s doorframe. Looks old: deeper than a casual notch.
details: I don't know what it means. Might be a builder's mark, might be someone counting something.
The wood around it has darkened with age. Whatever it is, it's been there a long time.
reactions: Old marks make you wonder. But the answers left with the people who made them.

statCheck.pass: That's a carpenter's mark: they scored the frame during construction to track the joints. Common practice a generation ago.
statCheck.fail: It's a scratch. Could mean anything.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had to shut early because of the storm. Everyone was sent home before dark.
details: The wind was battering the shutters and the rain was coming sideways. Nobody argued.
The crew looked relieved, honestly. Sometimes the weather makes the decision for you.
reactions: A lost afternoon is better than a lost roof. The work will be there tomorrow.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been getting fresh paint on the trim. One window at a time, slow and careful.
details: Somebody is doing it on their own time, between shifts. A labor of love, not assignment.
Already three windows done. The contrast between the painted and unpainted ones is stark.
reactions: When someone cares enough to improve something without being asked, that says everything.

entries.intro: The gravel around the {poi_type} crunches loudly underfoot. You can hear everyone approaching.
details: It's impossible to sneak up on the place. Every step announces itself.
The workers use the sound as a heads-up. Crunch means visitor.
reactions: Nature's alarm system: a path made of noise.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is running so well lately that nobody has anything to complain about. Makes for boring conversation.
details: When I brought it up at dinner, everyone just shrugged. Nothing to say.
We've gotten so used to having something to gripe about that smooth operation leaves a gap.
reactions: A problem-free {poi_type} is a gift you don't appreciate until problems come back.

entries.intro: The lichen on the north side of the {poi_type} has spread over the years. A green-grey patchwork on the stone.
details: It grows so slowly you don't notice it season to season. But compare it to five years ago and it's doubled.
The stone underneath is fine. Lichen sits on surfaces without eating into them.
reactions: It gives the building age. Not all signs of time are damage.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} got a load of bad {resource_type} last week. Had to sort through the whole shipment.
details: Half of it was substandard: wrong size, wrong finish, not what was ordered.
The crew spent a full day just separating good from bad. A waste of everyone's time.
reactions: Bad materials upstream mean wasted time downstream. The math is always simple.

statCheck.pass: The supplier switched sources without telling anyone: cheaper but inconsistent. They'll need to either push back or find someone else.
statCheck.fail: The {resource_type} was bad. I don't know the supplier's situation.

entries.intro: The morning dew on the {poi_type}'s stone makes the whole building glisten. Like it's covered in tiny jewels.
details: It only lasts until the sun warms the stone. Half an hour at most.
The workers arriving early walk through it, leaving dark footprints on the wet steps.
reactions: Beauty in small things. You just have to be there at the right time.

entries.intro: The bench outside the {poi_type} collapsed last week. Just gave way when someone sat down.
details: The legs were rotten through and nobody had checked. Went all at once.
The person who fell was fine, just embarrassed. The bench, less so.
reactions: Another thing added to the repair list. The list grows faster than the repairs.

statCheck.pass: The bench legs were set directly on the ground without stone footings. Constant moisture wicked up the grain and rotted them from the inside out.
statCheck.fail: It broke. Wood does that eventually.

entries.intro: I can tell the season by what I smell walking past the {poi_type}. Each month has its own scent.
details: Spring is earthy and damp, summer is dust and hot stone, autumn is sharp and woody.
It's like the building tracks the calendar better than I do.
reactions: Your nose knows the year before your mind catches up.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s new worker seems keen. Always asking questions, always watching the experienced hands.
details: The crew's been patient with them. Showing, not just telling.
It's nice to see someone eager. A lot of people start with dread.
reactions: Enthusiasm doesn't last forever, but it matters where you start.

entries.intro: The window glass at the {poi_type} is old and warped. Everything inside looks bent when you peer through.
details: The distortion makes the interior seem like it's underwater. Wavy, shifting shapes.
It's original glass, probably. Worth keeping for that alone.
reactions: Old glass has character that new glass can't replicate. There's a difference.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s well ran dry for two days. Everyone had to haul water from the stream.
details: The crew looked haggard from the extra work. Carrying water eats up time you don't have.
It came back on its own. The water table dips sometimes and then recovers.
reactions: You don't appreciate water until you have to carry it.

statCheck.pass: The aquifer level fluctuates with recent rainfall: two weeks dry and it drops below the well depth. A deeper bore would prevent it, but that's expensive work.
statCheck.fail: The well ran dry and then it didn't. I don't know what goes on underground.

entries.intro: The people at the {poi_type} have their own jokes. You hear them laughing at things no outsider would understand.
details: Every workplace develops its own language. Theirs is all shorthand and inside references.
I caught a punchline once and it made no sense. They thought that was the funniest part.
reactions: Shared humor means shared experience. It's how people bond over tedious work.

statCheck.pass: Most of their jokes are about a batch they ruined badly last year. They've turned the disaster into a legend and it keeps morale up.
statCheck.fail: Workplace humor. I'm on the outside of it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} glows warm at twilight. Something about the stone and the low light.
details: The walls catch the last of the sun and hold the color for a few minutes after everything else has gone grey.
It's the kind of light that makes you stop and look even though you've seen it a hundred times.
reactions: Some things don't get old. Good light on good stone is one of them.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s roof tiles are mismatched. You can see where old red ones meet new grey ones in a patchwork.
details: Every repair used whatever was available at the time. The roof is a history lesson in materials.
From the ground it looks deliberate, almost artistic. Up close it's just decades of making do.
reactions: A roof that keeps the rain out is a good roof. Matching tiles are vanity.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s water supply runs clear and cold all year. One of the best wells in the settlement.
details: Even in summer the water comes up cold enough to sting your teeth. Clean, no mineral taste.
People from other parts of town come there to fill their jugs. Can't blame them.
reactions: Good water is the foundation of everything. We're lucky to have it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} creaks in the wind. The whole frame shifts and groans when a gust hits.
details: You can hear the timbers working against each other. The building flexes like a living thing.
It's done this for years and hasn't fallen, so I suppose it's working as intended.
reactions: A building that bends doesn't break. At least that's what I tell myself during storms.

statCheck.pass: The timber frame was designed with some flex to handle wind loads: rigid structures crack under stress, but this one sways and absorbs it. Good building practice, unsettling to listen to.
statCheck.fail: It groans in the wind. Whether that's fine or dangerous, I couldn't say.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is where I go when I need to feel like things are normal. The routine there is grounding.
details: Same faces, same sounds, same work being done the same way. Reliable in a way most things aren't.
I don't even need anything from there most days. I just like knowing it's ticking along.
reactions: Stability is a kind of comfort. Especially when everything else feels uncertain.

entries.intro: The rats near the {poi_type} have gotten bold. I saw one in broad daylight, not even running.
details: Where there are scraps, there are rats. And a working building always produces scraps.
The crew puts out traps but the rats just go around them.
reactions: Rats are smarter than we give them credit for. Which is exactly the problem.

statCheck.pass: There's a gap in the foundation on the back side where they come and go. Seal that gap and you cut off their route to the food stores.
statCheck.fail: Rats show up and won't leave. I don't know how to fix it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s production has been uneven this month. Good weeks and bad weeks, no pattern.
details: One week the output is above average, the next it dips below. Keeps everyone guessing.
I think the inconsistency bothers people more than steady decline would.
reactions: People can handle bad news. What they can't handle is unpredictability.

statCheck.pass: The raw materials are coming in wildly different quality batches: good input means good output, but bad input means a whole day wasted sorting. They need a more reliable supplier.
statCheck.fail: It's up and down. I don't know the mechanics of why.

entries.intro: The shadow of the {poi_type} falls across my path every evening. Like clockwork.
details: I've started using it to judge how late it is. When the shadow reaches the third stone, it's time to head home.
It's been my accidental sundial for months now.
reactions: You find uses for everything around you if you pay enough attention.

statCheck.pass: The shadow moves about a hand's width every quarter hour at this latitude. Once you calibrate it to a few landmarks, you're accurate to within ten minutes.
statCheck.fail: Shadows move. I use them to guess the time but that's as far as it goes.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has been open longer hours this week. They're keeping the lights burning past dark.
details: I don't know if that means things are going well or if they're scrambling to catch up.
Either way, the crew's putting in the time. You have to respect the effort.
reactions: Long hours tell you something's happening. Whether it's good or bad depends on the faces.

statCheck.pass: They got a larger order than expected and the deadline is tight. Extra hours now mean they can ease off next week.
statCheck.fail: They're working late. Could be any reason.

entries.intro: The creek that runs past the {poi_type} is low this year. You can see the riverbed stones.
details: Last year at this time it was ankle-deep and moving fast. Now it's barely a trickle.
The workers who use creek water for cooling have had to slow down their process.
reactions: A dry year affects everything downstream. Literally.

statCheck.pass: The snowmelt uphill was thin this year: less snowpack means less slow-release water through the warm months. The creek won't recover until next spring.
statCheck.fail: Less water. Dry weather does that, I suppose.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has good acoustics, apparently. You can hear a whisper from one end to the other.
details: The curved ceiling catches and carries sound in unexpected ways.
The crew uses it to communicate across the workspace without shouting.
reactions: An accident of architecture that turned into a feature. I like that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s iron fittings have gone orange with rust. Hinges, latches, the works.
details: The salt in the air doesn't help. Everything metal fights a losing battle here.
They oil the mechanisms regularly but the surface rust comes back within a week.
reactions: Rust is just metal remembering it wants to be ore again.

statCheck.pass: Bronze fittings would resist the corrosion entirely: more expensive upfront but you'd never replace them. Iron was the cheap choice and now everyone pays for it weekly.
statCheck.fail: Metal rusts near the water. I don't know a solution.

entries.intro: The morning crowd at the {poi_type} has its own pecking order. You can see it in who goes through the door first.
details: The regulars know their place in the queue without being told. Newcomers learn quickly.
There's no hostility about it. Just an unspoken order that everyone respects.
reactions: Every gathering develops its own rules. The unwritten ones are the strongest.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} managed a clean week: no delays, no waste, no complaints. That's rare.
details: The crew was synchronized, the materials cooperated, the weather held. Everything aligned.
I'm told it was the first clean week in four months.
reactions: A clean week deserves a quiet nod. If you celebrate too loudly, you jinx the next one.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the first building travelers see coming from the {direction}. First impression of the whole settlement.
details: I hope they keep that in mind. The condition of that building is what people judge us by.
Right now it looks decent. Not impressive, but presentable.
reactions: First and last buildings matter more than the ones in the middle. That's just how memory works.

entries.intro: I left my jacket at the {poi_type} last week and it was still there when I went back. Untouched.
details: Hung on the same peg, just where I left it. In a bigger place that wouldn't happen.
The crew knew it was mine and just left it for me. Small town benefit.
reactions: Trust like that doesn't come from rules. It comes from everyone knowing everyone.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} uses a bell to signal shift changes. You can hear it from most of the settlement.
details: Three rings for morning start, one for midday break, two for end of day. Simple code.
I've arranged my own schedule around those rings without meaning to.
reactions: The {poi_type}'s bell sets the rhythm for more people than just the ones who work there.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looked peaceful covered in snow this morning. White on every ledge and sill.
details: The sharp angles softened by the snow. Even the rough patches looked clean.
By noon the crew had shoveled the entrance clear and the spell was broken.
reactions: Snow forgives every flaw in a building. Briefly.

entries.intro: The tool rack outside the {poi_type} is organized for once. Everything hung properly, labeled, sorted by size.
details: Usually it's a jumble of handles sticking out at angles. Today it's neat as a shop display.
I give it three days before it's back to chaos.
reactions: Order is temporary and disorder is the natural state, but the attempt matters.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a visitor from {other_settlement} looking it over. Seemed impressed.
details: They were asking questions about the operation, how things were run. Taking mental notes.
Our crew was polite but a little guarded. Nobody likes being evaluated.
reactions: If someone from elsewhere is studying what you do, you're doing something worth studying.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s gutter is sagging in the middle. Hangs down like a tired arm.
details: The bracket's pulled loose from the fascia board. One more heavy rain and it's coming off.
Water already misses the gutter at the low point and dumps onto the walkway.
reactions: Gutters: unglamorous, essential, always broken.

statCheck.pass: The bracket was nailed into softwood fascia that's since rotted. Lag bolts into the rafter tails would hold properly, but someone would need to get up on a ladder.
statCheck.fail: It's sagging. I don't do heights, so I haven't looked closely.

entries.intro: Being a {self_role} means I know when the {poi_type} is running right just by the sound of it.
details: A good day has an even tempo: steady clangs, consistent rhythm, no sudden stops.
A bad day is all stutters and long silences. You can hear the frustration.
reactions: Years of listening teach your ears things your eyes never notice.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s trough was frozen solid this morning. Solid block of ice with a water jug stuck in it.
details: The crew spent the first hour of the day thawing it over the fire. Not the most productive start.
Someone left it full overnight instead of draining it. A mistake nobody will make twice.
reactions: Winter teaches the same lessons every year, and every year someone needs the reminder.

statCheck.pass: Stone troughs crack when water freezes and expands inside them. This one's been lucky so far, but repeated freezes weaken it every time.
statCheck.fail: Water froze. Winter does that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the most reliable thing in this settlement. Rain or shine, it's there and it's working.
details: People get sick, weather turns, plans change. The {poi_type} just keeps going.
I think the crew takes pride in that consistency. They should.
reactions: Reliability is the highest compliment a place can earn.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s smoke changes direction about ten times a day. North, south, swirling, straight up.
details: We're in a valley with tricky air currents. The wind never knows what it wants to do here.
The laundry hanging near the {poi_type} smells like smoke half the week because of it.
reactions: You can predict the wind here about as well as you can predict people. Which is to say, not at all.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s new batch of {resource_type} is the talk of the settlement. People are actually excited.
details: I don't remember the last time {resource_type} quality generated actual conversation.
The crew is being modest about it, which only makes people talk more.
reactions: When the product speaks for itself, the workers don't have to.

entries.intro: The ant trails near the {poi_type} are impressive this year. Thick columns going in and out of the foundation.
details: Thousands of them, moving in perfect lines. They've built a whole highway system along the wall.
The workers have given up trying to stop them. The ants won the territory.
reactions: Sometimes you just have to share the space. The ants were probably there first.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been burning through supplies faster than usual. The storage looks thin.
details: Higher output means higher consumption. The math doesn't favor you when demand spikes.
They'll need a resupply soon or the pace drops. Simple as that.
reactions: Running hot is fine until the fuel runs out.

statCheck.pass: They're trying to build a stockpile before the season changes, when supply routes get slower. The urgency is calculated, not panicked.
statCheck.fail: They're using a lot. Whether that's planned or reactive, I wouldn't know.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} smells different every season. Right now it's all warm dust and hot stone.
details: In the cold months it's damp wood and iron. I don't prefer one over the other.
The scent carries about halfway down the road before the wind picks it apart.
reactions: A place that smells like work is a place that's earning its keep.

statCheck.pass: The dust smell means they're processing a drier batch than usual. Dry stock is faster to work but harder on the lungs.
statCheck.fail: It smells like the {poi_type}. That's about all I can say.

entries.intro: I like the sound the {poi_type} makes on a busy morning. Hammers, voices, doors slamming.
details: It blends into this low hum that just means things are moving along.
The silence on rest days feels wrong by comparison. Too quiet.
reactions: Noise you're used to stops being noise and starts being comfort.

entries.intro: The path to the {poi_type} gets muddy after every rain. A proper mess that takes days to dry.
details: People track it everywhere. You can map the {poi_type}'s foot traffic by the mud prints across the settlement.
Someone threw gravel down once but it sank in by the end of the week.
reactions: Mud is nature's way of reminding us who's really in charge of infrastructure.

statCheck.pass: The path sits in a low spot with clay soil underneath: water pools because it can't drain. A raised stone path would fix it, but nobody wants to fund it.
statCheck.fail: It gets muddy. That's about all I can tell you.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s chimney has been smoking clean lately. Thin white wisps, nothing acrid.
details: When the fuel is good, the smoke shows it. You don't cough walking past.
Last month it was thick and grey and made your eyes water. Big difference.
reactions: Clean smoke is one of those small improvements that makes the whole street more pleasant.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s door squeaks every time it opens. You can hear it from three houses away.
details: It's been like that for months. At this point I think people are used to it.
Someone will oil it eventually, or it'll just become part of the background.
reactions: A squeaky door is a low-priority problem that will outlast us all.

statCheck.pass: The hinge pin is worn oval from use: oiling quiets it for a week, but a new pin is the real fix. Takes ten minutes if you have the right stock.
statCheck.fail: It squeaks. Doors do that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s output this season has been above average. Steady, reliable work all the way through.
details: No big surprises, just day after day of solid results adding up.
The crew hasn't had to work late or scramble. That's how you know it's going well.
reactions: Quiet competence rarely gets celebrated, but it's the thing that actually keeps us going.

entries.intro: Weeds are coming up through the cobblestones around the {poi_type}. Little green cracks everywhere.
details: Nobody's pulled them in weeks and they're getting established. Stubborn little things.
Give it another month and the stones will be half-hidden.
reactions: Weeds don't care about your plans. They just grow where they want.

statCheck.pass: The sand between the cobbles washed out last season, so seeds settle in and root. Resanding the joints would choke them out.
statCheck.fail: Weeds grow in cracks. That's what weeds do.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been losing workers to other jobs. The crew is thinner than it should be.
details: Two left this month alone. Not enough people to cover the shifts comfortably.
The ones who stayed are picking up the slack, but you can see the strain.
reactions: A short crew means longer days for everyone. That wears people down fast.

statCheck.pass: The pay hasn't kept up with what other places offer. People follow the coin, and right now the coin leads elsewhere.
statCheck.fail: People left. I don't know the reasons.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s well-kept garden box out front is blooming. Bright colors against the stone.
details: Somebody on the crew planted those and tends them between tasks. A small act of care.
The flowers attract bees, which the crew grumbles about, but nobody pulls the plants.
reactions: A few flowers can change how a place feels. Worth the bees.

entries.intro: The dust from the {poi_type} has been settling on everything downwind. My windows are grimy every morning.
details: I wipe them clean and by next day there's a fresh layer. It's relentless.
The wind comes from the same direction most days, so the same houses always catch it.
reactions: Living downwind of anything means living with whatever it puts in the air.

statCheck.pass: They're working with a drier batch of materials than usual: once the moisture is back in the supply, the dust settles down. Literally.
statCheck.fail: It's dusty. I don't know the cause.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has been quiet this week. Fewer people coming and going than normal.
details: I'm not sure if that means a slow period or if people are just busy elsewhere.
The crew seemed relaxed about it. Sometimes a slow week is just a slow week.
reactions: Not every quiet spell means something's wrong. Sometimes things just pause.

statCheck.pass: They're between orders on purpose: using the gap to do maintenance they've been putting off. The quiet is planned, not accidental.
statCheck.fail: It's quiet. Could mean anything.

entries.intro: The stonework on the {poi_type} is some of the best in the settlement. Clean joints, level courses.
details: Whoever built it knew what they were doing. Every stone fits tight to the next.
The building's old enough that the craftsmanship is from a different era.
reactions: Good stonework outlasts the people who laid it. That's the whole point.

statCheck.pass: The original masons used a dry-fit technique with no mortar in the load-bearing courses: friction alone holds the weight. Nobody builds like that anymore.
statCheck.fail: It's well-built. That's about as far as my knowledge goes.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been drawing a {wildlife_type} to its eaves. Comes by every morning, same time.
details: I think it's nesting somewhere nearby and the {poi_type} is on its route.
The crew leaves it alone. It's not hurting anything.
reactions: Sharing the space with the locals: the ones with feathers and the ones without.

entries.intro: There's a puddle near the {poi_type} that never fully dries. Even in summer it's there, shallow and still.
details: The ground sinks a bit right there, enough to hold water permanently.
Kids splash through it, workers step around it. It's part of the landscape now.
reactions: Some problems are so small they become features instead.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s output has been slipping. Not dramatically, but enough to notice over the weeks.
details: It's the kind of slow decline that's easy to ignore until you compare the numbers month to month.
The crew seems tired. Could just be the season wearing on them.
reactions: A gradual slide is harder to fix than a sudden drop. You can't point at one thing.

statCheck.pass: The equipment is wearing out. They're working with tools that should have been replaced last season, and the effort to compensate eats into the output.
statCheck.fail: Things are slower than they should be. I don't know the specifics.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew celebrated a milestone this week. Quiet gathering, nothing flashy.
details: Five years since the last major repair, apparently. A point of pride for them.
They shared a drink after work and that was that. Back to normal the next morning.
reactions: Milestones like that deserve recognition, even if it's just a round among friends.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s sign is faded to the point where you can barely read it. Just a ghost of lettering.
details: Sun and rain have bleached the paint away year by year.
Everyone here knows what the building is, so nobody bothers replacing it. It's only confusing for travelers.
reactions: A sign nobody reads is a sign that's done its job so long it worked itself out of one.

statCheck.pass: They actually ordered a new sign two months ago, but the painter got behind on commissions. It's sitting in a workshop somewhere, finished and uncollected.
statCheck.fail: The sign is old. Nobody's gotten around to replacing it.

entries.intro: I saw {npc_name} heading toward the {poi_type} early this morning, before anyone else was up.
details: They go early when they want to get things done without an audience.
Whatever business they had, they were back before the crew arrived.
reactions: Early risers get things done that nobody else knows about. That's half the point.

statCheck.pass: {npc_name} has been checking the supply stock before the crew arrives to make sure the day starts clean. Doing it quietly so nobody feels managed.
statCheck.fail: I saw them go in. What they were doing, I couldn't say.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been turning out good {resource_type} lately. Consistent quality, no rejects.
details: The crew's found their rhythm. When the process is smooth, the product shows it.
People have been placing bigger orders because they trust what's coming out.
reactions: Good reputation takes years to build. A steady season like this one helps.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s steps are worn smooth in the middle from decades of foot traffic. Deep grooves in the stone.
details: The edges are still sharp but the center is polished almost flat.
You have to watch your step in the rain. Smooth stone and wet feet aren't friends.
reactions: Worn steps are proof a place matters. Nobody wears down the stairs to somewhere forgettable.

statCheck.pass: The softer stone they used is local sandstone: cheap and easy to cut, but it wears ten times faster than granite. A trade-off that shows over decades.
statCheck.fail: The steps are worn. Heavy use does that.

entries.intro: The heat coming off the {poi_type} on a summer afternoon is brutal. You feel it walking past.
details: The stone absorbs the sun all day and radiates it back. Like standing next to an oven.
The crew inside must be cooking. I don't know how they manage full shifts.
reactions: Some buildings are comfortable half the year and miserable the other half. This one leans miserable in summer.

statCheck.pass: The stone walls trap heat because there's no airflow designed into the building. A few vents would make a real difference, but cutting into finished stone is expensive.
statCheck.fail: It gets hot. That's all I know about it.

entries.intro: The pile of scrap material behind the {poi_type} is getting tall. They haven't hauled it away in a while.
details: Offcuts, broken pieces, things that didn't pass quality. Just piling up out back.
The crew says they'll deal with it when they have a spare day. That day keeps not arriving.
reactions: There's always a pile somewhere that represents the things nobody has time for.

statCheck.pass: The scrap has value if someone sorts it: the metal can be reforged and the wood makes decent kindling. The crew just hasn't had time to process it.
statCheck.fail: It's a pile of junk. I don't know what else to say about it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} sits at the highest point in the settlement. You can see it from just about anywhere.
details: When I'm heading home from the fields I use it as a landmark. It's always there on the skyline.
At night when they have lanterns lit, it's the brightest point around.
reactions: A building you can see from anywhere becomes part of how you think about home.

statCheck.pass: The hilltop position wasn't chosen for views: it was the only spot with bedrock close enough to the surface to anchor a foundation. Everything else in the settlement sits on clay.
statCheck.fail: It's on the high ground. Always has been.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew has good morale right now. You can hear it in how they talk to each other.
details: There's joking, there's banter, nobody's snapping at anyone. A healthy workplace sound.
It wasn't like that a few months back. Whatever changed, it stuck.
reactions: Happy workers do better work. Not a complicated idea, but a true one.

statCheck.pass: They shuffled the shift schedule so people work with the partners they actually get along with. Small change, big effect on the daily atmosphere.
statCheck.fail: They seem happier. I don't know what's different.

entries.intro: The drainage around the {poi_type} backed up again. Water pooling where it shouldn't.
details: The channel is clogged with leaves and grit. Happens every autumn like clockwork.
The crew cleared it out but it'll plug again in a week.
reactions: Some maintenance jobs never end. You finish them just in time to start them again.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s weathervane hasn't moved in days. Stuck pointing east.
details: Either there's truly no wind, or the pivot has seized up.
I've been watching it on and off, waiting for it to twitch. Nothing.
reactions: A broken weathervane is just a metal rooster. Decorative, not functional.

statCheck.pass: The bearing is corroded: salt and moisture fused the metal. A few drops of oil and some force would free it, but nobody wants to climb up there for a weathervane.
statCheck.fail: It's stuck. Could be rust, could be anything.

entries.intro: The morning shift at the {poi_type} starts so early the crew beats the sunrise.
details: You see lanterns moving through the dark, hear doors opening while stars are still out.
By the time most people are awake, the first batch of work is already done.
reactions: The settlement runs on the labor of people who get up before it's convenient.

statCheck.pass: The early start is because the first hours are the most productive: cooler air, fewer interruptions, and the light coming up means better visibility by mid-task.
statCheck.fail: They start early. I don't know their reasoning.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} got a fresh coat of whitewash on the front. Looks almost new.
details: The contrast with the unwashed sides is stark. One wall gleaming, three walls grey.
Still, the front is what people see. And right now it looks sharp.
reactions: Whitewash is the cheapest way to make something look better. And it works.

entries.intro: A cart full of {resource_type} tipped over near the {poi_type} this morning. What a mess.
details: Took the better part of an hour to reload everything. Half the crew came out to help.
The driver looked mortified. The road is uneven right there and the wheel caught a rut.
reactions: An hour lost and a bruised ego. Could have been worse.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been a landmark for as long as I can remember. Can't imagine the settlement without it.
details: Every direction I give to travelers involves the {poi_type}. It's the reference point for everything.
I forget it's there most days, the way you forget about your own breathing.
reactions: Some things are so constant they become invisible. Until you try to picture life without them.

statCheck.pass: The building has been repaired so many times that almost nothing original remains. It's the same shape, the same location, but the stones and timbers have all been swapped out over generations.
statCheck.fail: It's been here forever. That's all I know.

entries.intro: The insects around the {poi_type} have been terrible this season. Flies thick as smoke.
details: You can't walk past without swatting at something. The crew works with them buzzing around constantly.
I think the waste bin out back is drawing them. Warm weather and scraps are a bad combination.
reactions: Flies don't care about your schedule. They show up regardless.

statCheck.pass: The waste bins are supposed to have weighted lids, but someone stopped replacing the weights and the flies found the gap. A simple fix nobody's made.
statCheck.fail: Flies show up in warm weather. Not much to do about it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks different in fog. Softer, larger somehow, like it's not quite real.
details: The sharp lines blur and the building seems to float above the ground.
People moving in and out look like ghosts. The fog eats the color right out of everything.
reactions: Fog makes familiar things strange. I always forget that until it happens.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s latest {resource_type} batch was disappointing. Below what people expected.
details: Not unusable, just mediocre. The kind of result that makes people shrug instead of smile.
The crew knows it. They're not happy about it either.
reactions: A bad batch is a bad batch. You learn from it and move on.

statCheck.pass: The raw material they got was lower grade than what was promised. You can't produce first-rate goods from second-rate stock, no matter the skill.
statCheck.fail: It wasn't great. I don't know the details.

entries.intro: The cat that lives near the {poi_type} has gotten fat. The crew must be feeding it scraps.
details: It sits on the wall and watches the workers like it owns the place. Nobody shoos it.
I've never seen a more content animal. It's living its best life at the {poi_type}'s expense.
reactions: Every workplace needs a resident creature. Gives people something to talk about besides work.

statCheck.pass: The cat showed up as a stray during a cold snap and the crew fed it once. That was enough to seal the arrangement permanently.
statCheck.fail: It's a fat cat. I don't know its origin story.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s fence posts are leaning badly. The whole line tilts to one side.
details: The ground frost heaved them up last winter and nobody straightened them after the thaw.
Functional, technically. But it looks like the fence had a long night.
reactions: A leaning fence is tomorrow's fallen fence. But tomorrow never comes for jobs like that.

statCheck.pass: The posts only go down about a foot: proper fence posts need three feet to resist frost heave. It was a quick job done cheaply.
statCheck.fail: The fence is leaning. I'm not a builder.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} hosted a small gathering after hours last week. People from around the settlement showed up.
details: Nothing formal: just the crew inviting neighbors to see what they've been working on.
There was food, some talk, a bit of showing off. The kind of evening you remember.
reactions: When a workplace opens its doors to the community, it stops feeling like a separate world.

entries.intro: There's a vine growing up the side of the {poi_type}. It's climbed all the way to the roofline.
details: Started at the base years ago and nobody cut it back. Now it's part of the building.
In summer it goes leafy and green and softens the whole facade.
reactions: Some people see a problem. I see a building that's growing its own decoration.

statCheck.pass: The vine's roots are working into the mortar joints between the stones: it looks nice, but it's slowly pulling the wall apart. In a few years it'll be a real problem.
statCheck.fail: It's a vine. Looks nice enough to me.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} runs like it runs. No drama, no fuss, just day in and day out.
details: That kind of steadiness is easy to overlook. Nobody praises a building for being reliable.
But when something breaks you realize how much you counted on it working.
reactions: The things you don't notice are the things holding everything together.

statCheck.pass: The consistency comes from the lead worker, who's been there longer than anyone and keeps standards high without making a fuss. If they ever leave, the place would feel the gap immediately.
statCheck.fail: It runs well. I don't know the inner workings.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s awning is ripped. Half of it flaps loose in the wind.
details: A storm caught it wrong and tore the canvas right along the seam.
The other half still provides shade, but it looks like the building is missing a tooth.
reactions: A torn awning is the kind of thing that stays torn longer than it should.

entries.intro: I remember when the {poi_type} was quieter: less traffic, less urgency.
details: The settlement has grown and the {poi_type} grew with it. More demand, more pace.
I don't miss the slow days, exactly. But I notice the difference.
reactions: Growth is good until you catch yourself being nostalgic for when things were smaller.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s newest hand is a quick learner. Already keeping pace with the experienced workers.
details: They watch closely, ask the right questions, and don't repeat the same mistake twice.
The rest of the crew has taken notice. You can tell they're impressed even if they don't say it.
reactions: Talent shows early. You either pick things up fast or you don't.

entries.intro: The shade behind the {poi_type} in the afternoon is the coolest spot in the settlement.
details: The building blocks the sun completely from about midday on. A wall of cool shadow.
You'll find people sitting there during breaks, backs against the stone, hats over their eyes.
reactions: Shade is currency in summer. The {poi_type} happens to be the richest bank.

statCheck.pass: The north-facing wall stays cool because the stone never gets direct sun: it radiates the chill it absorbs at night all day. Natural cooling that no amount of design could improve on.
statCheck.fail: It's shady there. That's why it's cool.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s roof leaks when the rain comes from the north. Every time.
details: The crew puts buckets down before the storm even starts. They've got the routine down.
South rain, no problem: north rain, buckets out. It's been like that for years.
reactions: A leak you can predict is almost the same as a leak you've fixed. Almost.

statCheck.pass: The flashing where the roof meets the north wall was installed with the wrong overlap: water gets under the edge and runs down inside. A roofer could fix it in a day.
statCheck.fail: It leaks from one direction. I don't know roofing well enough to say why.

entries.intro: The birdsong near the {poi_type} at dawn is beautiful. A whole chorus before anyone arrives.
details: The trees around the building have become a gathering spot for birds. Dozens of them at sunrise.
Once the work starts and the noise picks up, they scatter. But that pre-dawn concert is something.
reactions: The best music in the settlement happens when nobody's awake to hear it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s supply cart didn't come this week. First missed delivery in a long time.
details: The crew is managing with what they have, stretching supplies thinner than they'd like.
It'll probably show up next week, but the gap throws off the rhythm.
reactions: One missed delivery reminds you how thin the margin actually is.

statCheck.pass: The road from {other_settlement} washed out in the last storm and the cart couldn't get through. They're working on clearing it.
statCheck.fail: The delivery didn't come. I didn't hear why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is warmest in the settlement during winter. The work keeps the building heated.
details: People stop by just to thaw out on cold days, pretending they have business there.
The crew never turns anyone away. They know what the cold does.
reactions: A warm building in winter is worth more than coin to the people nearby.

entries.intro: The footpath that circles the {poi_type} is hardpacked and smooth from years of use.
details: You can walk it in the dark without tripping. Your feet know the ground.
It gets wider every year as more people take the shortcut.
reactions: Paths are made by walking, not by planning. This one proves it.

statCheck.pass: The path predates the building. It was an animal track first, then a foot trail, and the {poi_type} was built next to it because the ground was already packed and level.
statCheck.fail: It's a well-worn path. People use it because it's there.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been producing {resource_type} at a pace I haven't seen before. Impressive, honestly.
details: The output is higher than last season by a noticeable margin. The results speak for themselves.
I overheard the crew saying they found a way to cut one of the slow steps. Efficiency gains.
reactions: When a small improvement compounds over weeks, the difference becomes hard to ignore.

statCheck.pass: They reorganized the staging area so materials flow in one direction instead of doubling back. Saved a quarter hour per cycle, which adds up across a full day.
statCheck.fail: They're producing more. I don't know the technical reasons.

entries.intro: The flag on top of the {poi_type} is so faded it's almost white. Used to have color to it.
details: Sun bleaches cloth faster than you'd think. A few seasons of exposure and the dye is gone.
Nobody's climbed up to replace it. The pole's high and the effort outweighs the vanity.
reactions: A faded flag is still a flag. It just tells time in addition to telling ownership.

statCheck.pass: The flag was the personal standard of the family that founded the {poi_type}. Nobody replaces it because doing so would mean choosing a new design, and nobody has the authority to decide.
statCheck.fail: It's an old flag. I don't know the history.

entries.intro: The delivery of {resource_type} to the {poi_type} came early this month. The crew was caught off guard.
details: No space prepared, no one assigned to receive it. They improvised, but it wasn't smooth.
Early is better than late, but unexpected is unexpected either way.
reactions: Being ahead of schedule only helps if everyone knows about it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s wastewater has been running cleaner than usual. Whatever they changed, the creek downstream is grateful.
details: Used to be murky runoff that stained the stones. Now it's nearly clear.
The crew installed a settling basin, I think. Simple fix, big improvement.
reactions: Taking care of the mess you make is the most basic kind of responsibility.

entries.intro: The mortar on the {poi_type}'s east wall is crumbling. You can pick it out with your fingernail.
details: The joints are open enough that you can feel a draft through the wall from inside.
Repointing isn't glamorous work, but the building needs it before the next freeze.
reactions: A wall is only as strong as what holds the stones together.

statCheck.pass: The original mortar was mixed too lean: not enough bite to bind the sand. It was cheap to lay and it's been cheap in quality ever since.
statCheck.fail: The mortar's crumbling. Old age, I assume.

entries.intro: The light at the {poi_type} in the evening when the crew is finishing up is golden. Warm and low through the windows.
details: The lanterns inside catch the dust in the air and everything glows. It's prettier than the crew would admit.
From outside you just see shapes moving behind warm glass. Peaceful.
reactions: The prettiest moments in a workday are the ones nobody's trying to make pretty.

entries.intro: Someone stacked firewood against the {poi_type}'s wall. A big pile, neatly done.
details: The rows are tight and level. Whoever did it takes stacking seriously.
It'll keep the building warm through the cold months if they're smart about rationing it.
reactions: A well-stacked woodpile is a promise that winter will be manageable.

statCheck.pass: The stacker is the oldest crew member: they learned the technique from their parent, who stacked wood professionally. There's science to the airflow between the rows that makes it season faster.
statCheck.fail: It's firewood. Somebody stacked it neatly.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s work schedule has been unpredictable this month. Open one day, closed the next.
details: Nobody seems to know the pattern. People show up and it's either running or it isn't.
It's annoying for anyone who needs something done on a timeline.
reactions: Inconsistency is worse than being closed. At least you can plan around closed.

statCheck.pass: They're working around a supply shortage, only opening when materials arrive: no materials, no work. It's not disorganization, it's rationing.
statCheck.fail: Their schedule makes no sense to me.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} always has someone sweeping the front step in the morning. Without fail.
details: It's the first thing they do before any real work starts. A clean threshold sets the tone.
I've noticed it because I walk past around the same time every day. Sweep, sweep, done.
reactions: Small rituals like that say more about a place than any grand gesture.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s well-worn threshold has a dip in the center. Generations of boots carved that groove.
details: The wood is lowest right where everyone steps. A shallow valley worn by a million footfalls.
It'll need replacing eventually, but for now it's a record of how long the place has been busy.
reactions: Everything wears away if enough people pass through. Stone, wood, patience.

statCheck.pass: The threshold was carved from a single plank of ironwood: the hardest timber in the region. Anything else would have worn through years ago.
statCheck.fail: The wood is worn down. Old doorways do that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew invited people from {other_settlement} to see their operation. Friendly, open about it.
details: They gave them a full walk-through, answered questions, shared what they've learned.
The visitors were polite and took notes. Seemed genuinely interested.
reactions: Sharing knowledge builds bridges. Better to be generous than guarded.

entries.intro: The lock on the {poi_type}'s main door is temperamental. Takes three tries on a good day.
details: The mechanism is old and the key barely fits. You have to jiggle it just right.
Everyone who uses it has their own technique. It's become a rite of passage.
reactions: Replacing a lock is easy. Getting around to it is the hard part.

statCheck.pass: The lock mechanism was built by the original owner and uses a non-standard keyway. A locksmith could replace it in an hour, but the crew considers it a security feature: nobody else can pick it.
statCheck.fail: It's a tricky lock. That's all I know.

entries.intro: The warmth from the {poi_type} melts the frost on the road out front before anywhere else in the settlement.
details: On cold mornings you can see exactly where the building's heat reaches. Frost line cuts sharp.
The crew uses it as a measure: if the frost is gone by sunrise, the fires are burning right.
reactions: A warm building that warms the road too. The {poi_type} earns its place even when you're just walking past.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} needs a new roof before next winter. Everyone knows it, nobody wants to fund it.
details: The beams are sagging and the tiles shift with every hard wind.
It's the kind of expense that only gets more expensive the longer you wait.
reactions: Deferred maintenance is just a loan you take out against the future. With interest.

statCheck.pass: The central ridge beam has a split that's getting wider under load: once that goes, the whole roof follows. It's not urgent today, but the window is closing.
statCheck.fail: The roof is bad. How bad exactly, I couldn't tell you.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been doing good work this season. I've got no complaints, which is saying something.
details: The quality has been consistent, the timing predictable, the crew cooperative.
When everything works the way it should, there's nothing to talk about. And that's the best outcome.
reactions: No news from the {poi_type} is the best news.

entries.intro: The wind tunnel between the {poi_type} and the building next to it is fierce. Nearly took my hat yesterday.
details: The gap between the two buildings funnels the wind and accelerates it. Basic physics.
People lean into the gust when they walk through. It catches you off guard every time.
reactions: Architecture creates weather that wasn't there before. That gap is a wind factory.

statCheck.pass: The gap is narrower than building codes allow now: it was permitted under older rules. The wind speed through it is roughly double open air.
statCheck.fail: It's windy in the gap. I don't know the physics.

entries.intro: I saw a {wildlife_type} perched on the {poi_type}'s roofline this morning. Just sitting there, watching the settlement.
details: It stayed for a good while before flying off. Unbothered by the noise below.
The crew pointed it out to each other like it was an event. Small joys.
reactions: When a wild creature chooses your roof, it feels like a quiet compliment.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s water barrel is growing algae on the inside. Green film on the surface.
details: The crew's been meaning to scrub it out but keeps putting it off.
Nobody drinks from it, so it's not a health problem. Just looks unpleasant.
reactions: Neglect starts small and green and gets bigger if you let it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is the most painted building in the settlement. Every few years someone adds a new color.
details: Under the current coat there are layers going back decades. A history in pigment.
The latest color was chosen by committee. You can tell: it's inoffensive to the point of invisibility.
reactions: A building that people keep painting is a building people care about. Even if they can't agree on the color.

statCheck.pass: There are at least eight layers of paint under the current one, each from a different decade. If you chipped a cross-section you'd see the color history of the settlement's tastes.
statCheck.fail: It's been painted a lot. That's all I notice.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had to turn away orders this week. Too much demand, not enough hands.
details: It's a good problem to have, I suppose. Better than the other way around.
The crew is stretched thin as it is. Taking on more would mean worse results for everyone.
reactions: Saying no to work takes discipline. Most people just drown instead.

statCheck.pass: They lost two experienced workers last month and haven't replaced them: the demand is the same but the capacity dropped. They're making hard choices about what to prioritize.
statCheck.fail: They're busy. More than that, I couldn't say.

entries.intro: The rainwater running off the {poi_type}'s roof has cut a little channel in the dirt below. A miniature river.
details: It gets deeper every storm. The water follows the same path and carves it further each time.
Somebody tossed a plank across it as a bridge. It's about six inches wide and feels absurd.
reactions: Water is patient. Give it enough time and it'll reshape anything.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew has been training together for years. They work without speaking half the time.
details: Somebody reaches for a tool and someone else has already handed it over. Like a dance.
New people find it eerie until they learn the rhythm. Then they start doing it too.
reactions: A crew that anticipates each other is worth twice the headcount.

statCheck.pass: The senior crew members all trained under the same mentor and learned the same hand signals and workflow from the ground up. That shared foundation is why they anticipate each other.
statCheck.fail: They work well together. I don't know their background.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s chimney cap blew off in the last storm. Now rain gets into the flue.
details: Every time they light a fire, the wet bricks hiss and steam until they dry out.
The cap is sitting on the ground out back, dented but fixable. It just needs to go back up.
reactions: A chimney without a cap is an invitation for weather to come inside.

statCheck.pass: The mortar holding the cap was old and the wind shear on the exposed face is stronger than most buildings get. They need to bolt the replacement down, not just mortar it.
statCheck.fail: It blew off. Wind does that, I'm told.

entries.intro: The sunflowers near the {poi_type} are taller than I am this year. A wall of yellow along the fence.
details: The soil there must be rich. Everything planted near the building seems to do well.
The crew says the sunflowers track the sun across the sky. I've watched, and they're right.
reactions: Beauty that grows on its own is the best kind. No planning required.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has a drip problem after rain. Water collects on the roof and then lets go in big drops for hours.
details: You walk past and one hits the back of your neck. Cold, sudden, unpleasant.
The crew avoids walking under the eaves out of habit. Visitors learn the hard way.
reactions: It's the building's way of making sure you know it's there.

entries.intro: Someone carved a small notch into the {poi_type}'s door frame at about knee height. Nobody knows why.
details: It's deliberate: clean cut, not accidental. Too low to be a height mark for a child.
People make up theories when they're bored. The notch has a dozen explanations and no confirmed one.
reactions: A mystery that small is fun to think about and impossible to solve.

statCheck.pass: I think it's a flood mark: when the water rose years ago, someone notched the frame to record how high it reached. Knee height is about right for what I've heard.
statCheck.fail: It's a notch. Could mean anything.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew pulled together after a rough stretch last month. Morale is back up.
details: They had some bad weeks where nothing went right and tempers ran short.
But they talked it through and sorted out who was doing what. Things smoothed out.
reactions: Every crew hits rough patches. What matters is whether they come out the other side together.

entries.intro: The woodpile at the {poi_type} is running low. Half what it should be for the season.
details: Either they're burning through it faster or the last delivery was short. Maybe both.
The crew's been rationing, keeping the fires lower than usual.
reactions: Running low on fuel is the kind of problem that makes every other problem worse.

statCheck.pass: The wood supplier is behind on deliveries across the whole settlement. The wet spring made seasoning slow, so everything's delayed and damp.
statCheck.fail: Low fuel. I don't know the supply chain details.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks solid. Well-maintained, clean lines, no obvious neglect.
details: It's the kind of building that makes you feel good about the settlement when you look at it.
The crew takes pride in the appearance. You can tell by the swept entry and oiled hinges.
reactions: A place that looks cared for is a place where people care. The two go together.

entries.intro: The echo around the {poi_type} is strange. Sounds bounce off the walls in ways you don't expect.
details: You'll hear a voice and think it's behind you, but it's actually coming from across the yard.
The crew has learned to ignore it, but visitors look confused constantly.
reactions: Every building has its quirks. This one's just louder about them.

statCheck.pass: The curved wall on the east side acts like a parabolic reflector for sound: voices hit the curve and get focused to the opposite side. An accident of architecture, not design.
statCheck.fail: Sound bounces weirdly there. I don't know why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s old hand-lettered timetable is still tacked to the wall. Nobody follows it anymore.
details: The ink is faded and the hours are from a time when the schedule was different.
It's become decoration rather than information. Feels wrong to take it down.
reactions: Some things stay up because removing them feels like admitting something changed.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been pulling in orders from {other_settlement} this season. Word is getting around.
details: People outside the settlement trust the quality, which is a compliment to the crew.
It means more work, but it also means more coin and more reputation.
reactions: When your work speaks far enough that strangers seek you out, that's success.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s shutters are warped and don't close properly. Gaps big enough to see through.
details: The wood expanded with moisture and never shrank back. Common problem with cheap lumber.
They rattle in the wind and let in cold air. Uncomfortable in every season.
reactions: Warped shutters are the kind of thing you live with until you can't anymore.

entries.intro: The ground around the {poi_type} smells like minerals after it rains. Sharp, clean, almost metallic.
details: The soil near the building has a different composition than the rest of the settlement.
I find it pleasant, but some people wrinkle their noses. Taste varies.
reactions: A place that smells distinct has character. Even if the character is geological.

statCheck.pass: There's an iron-rich clay deposit under that part of the settlement: the rain dissolves trace minerals and brings them to the surface. That's why the soil there has a reddish tint too.
statCheck.fail: It smells metallic after rain. I don't know the geology.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew fixed the front step that everyone tripped on. Finally.
details: It was uneven for months and at least a dozen people caught their toe on it.
The fix took about an hour. Makes you wonder why it took months to get to it.
reactions: Better late than never, but 'never late' would have been better.

entries.intro: The stray dog near the {poi_type} has adopted the crew. Follows them around during shift hours.
details: It waits at the entrance in the morning and leaves when they do. Keeps its own schedule.
They've named it, though nobody agrees on what the name actually is.
reactions: A dog that chooses a workplace has good instincts about people.

statCheck.pass: The dog belonged to a worker who moved away last season and couldn't take it along. It stayed and adopted the whole crew instead.
statCheck.fail: It's a stray. It showed up and stayed.

entries.intro: The puddle that forms by the {poi_type}'s front door freezes perfectly flat in winter. Like a tiny mirror.
details: On clear mornings you can see the sky reflected in it, frost crystals at the edges.
Then someone steps on it and it shatters. A short-lived thing.
reactions: The prettiest things are often the most fragile. And the most temporary.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been struggling with mice. They get into the supplies and chew through everything.
details: The crew finds droppings every morning and spends the first hour cleaning up.
Traps catch a few, but more always come. It's a losing battle.
reactions: Mice don't take days off. That's their advantage over the people chasing them.

statCheck.pass: There's a gap where the wall meets the foundation that's their highway in. Fill it with mortar and tin sheeting and the problem shrinks overnight.
statCheck.fail: Mice get in. I don't know how they manage it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s work bell has a beautiful tone. Clear, bright, carries across the whole settlement.
details: It's old bronze, cast before anyone here was born. The sound hasn't changed.
You could set your day by it if you wanted. Most of us do without realizing.
reactions: A good bell is a community clock. Everyone shares it whether they mean to or not.

statCheck.pass: The bell was cast with a higher tin content than most, which gives it the bright tone: the alloy vibrates at a cleaner frequency. A lesser bell would sound dull by comparison.
statCheck.fail: It's a good bell. Sounds nice.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a small accident this week. Nothing serious: someone dropped a heavy load and it dented the floor.
details: The crew stopped for a minute, made sure everyone was fine, and went right back to work.
The dent is still there. A permanent reminder to lift with your legs.
reactions: Small accidents are lessons. The ones you walk away from teach you the most.

entries.intro: The wildflowers growing along the {poi_type}'s south wall are blooming early this year.
details: They're weeks ahead of the ones in the fields. The wall must trap warmth and give them a head start.
The colors are vivid: purple and yellow against the grey stone.
reactions: Nature finds advantages in the smallest differences. A warm wall is enough.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s waste heap out back is attracting crows. A noisy, aggressive bunch.
details: They fight each other over scraps and make a racket from dawn to dusk.
The crew chases them off but they just circle back. Persistent birds.
reactions: Crows go where the food is. You can't blame them for being practical.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been the subject of conversation lately. People have opinions.
details: Some think it's running better than ever. Others think it's coasting on past success.
The truth is probably somewhere in the middle, where truth usually lives.
reactions: Opinions about a place tell you more about the people talking than the place itself.

statCheck.pass: The production numbers are flat: not improving, not declining. Optimists see potential in stability and pessimists see stagnation.
statCheck.fail: People are talking. I don't have the facts to weigh in.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s old storage shed collapsed under the snow. Just folded in on itself.
details: The frame was rotting for years and the snow weight was the last straw.
Nobody was hurt, thankfully. The shed was empty except for a few old crates.
reactions: A building tells you when it's done. Sometimes it tells you by lying down.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s spring cleaning was thorough this year. The place gleams.
details: Windows washed, floors scrubbed, every surface wiped down. You can smell the soap from outside.
The crew spent a whole day on it. They looked tired but satisfied after.
reactions: A deep clean resets a space. Everything feels possible when the cobwebs are gone.

entries.intro: The moss between the {poi_type}'s paving stones is soft and thick. Like walking on a green carpet.
details: It fills every crack and joint and turns the hard stone walkway into something almost comfortable.
When it's wet it's slippery, though. I've seen people catch themselves more than once.
reactions: Moss softens everything it touches. Until it trips you.

statCheck.pass: The moss variety growing there is a moisture indicator: lush and green means the water table is high, yellowing means things are drying out. A natural gauge if you know how to read it.
statCheck.fail: There's moss between the stones. That's the extent of my botanical knowledge.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} gets the best sunset light in the settlement. The whole front face turns golden.
details: It faces west, and at the right time of year the light hits it perfectly.
I've seen people stop on the road just to look at it. Even the crew pauses sometimes.
reactions: A building with good light is lucky. You can't plan for that kind of thing.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s loading area is a bottleneck. One cart at a time, and if there's a queue, everyone waits.
details: The entrance was designed for hand-carrying, not cart traffic. Nobody planned for growth.
The delays add up over a week. Half a day lost to waiting, easy.
reactions: Design decisions from the past become bottlenecks in the present. That's just how it goes.

statCheck.pass: They could widen the entrance by removing one wall section, but it's load-bearing. The fix requires an engineer's plan, not just a hammer.
statCheck.fail: It's a tight space. I don't know what it would take to change it.

entries.intro: The scent of fresh {resource_type} drifts out of the {poi_type} on warm days. You can smell it halfway across the settlement.
details: It's a clean, sharp smell that tells you production is running smooth.
The crew doesn't notice it anymore, but everyone else does.
reactions: When a place smells like its purpose, it's doing its job.

statCheck.pass: The particular smell changes with what they're working on. Experienced traders can tell the product quality by scent alone before they ever see the finished goods.
statCheck.fail: It has a smell. That's about all I notice.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew donated leftover materials to fix the community well. Generous of them.
details: It was offcuts and surplus, nothing they'd miss, but the gesture meant something to people.
The well works again and the crew got a round of thanks they didn't expect.
reactions: Generosity that costs little but means a lot is the best kind there is.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s exterior wall has a dark stain where water runs down from the roof. An ugly streak.
details: It's been growing darker over the years as more minerals deposit on the stone.
It's just cosmetic, but it makes the building look older and more worn than it actually is.
reactions: Water marks are honest: they show you where the building is losing the fight with weather.

statCheck.pass: The stain is mineral deposit from dissolved limestone in the runoff: the water picks up calcium from the roof mortar and leaves it on the wall as it evaporates. Chemical weathering.
statCheck.fail: It's a water stain. Roofs make those.

entries.intro: I used to walk past the {poi_type} without a second thought. Lately I've been paying more attention.
details: There's always something going on if you watch long enough. Small dramas, small victories.
