The routine of the place is more interesting than I gave it credit for.
reactions: You can live next to something for years and only really see it when you slow down.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s floor is uneven. You can feel it shift underfoot in places.
details: The building settled over the years and the floor followed the ground beneath it.
Furniture leans slightly and things roll toward the low corner. The crew doesn't even notice anymore.
reactions: A crooked floor is just a floor that's been here long enough to make itself comfortable.

statCheck.pass: The foundation on the low side wasn't bedded deep enough and has sunk into softer soil. It's stable now: it settled as far as it's going to.
statCheck.fail: The floor tilts. Old buildings do that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s night shift left their lanterns burning after they finished. Wasteful but pretty.
details: The building was glowing softly with nobody inside. Like a house where the guests left but the party stayed.
Someone went back and doused them by morning. Oil isn't cheap.
reactions: An empty building with lights on feels like a memory of the people who were just there.

entries.intro: {npc_name} has been spending a lot of time at the {poi_type} lately. More than their job requires.
details: They show up early and stay late, helping with whatever needs doing.
The crew seems grateful for the extra hands. Nobody's questioned the motivation.
reactions: When someone volunteers their time, you don't ask why. You just appreciate it.

statCheck.pass: {npc_name} is considering asking for a position there. They've been putting in the hours to prove their worth before they make the request.
statCheck.fail: They're there a lot. I don't know why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been doing better than anyone expected. The numbers are up across the board.
details: People who were skeptical are quietly revising their opinions.
The crew hasn't changed anything dramatic. Just steady work done well, day after day.
reactions: Success that comes from consistency is the most trustworthy kind.

entries.intro: The noise from the {poi_type} has been louder this week. Something about the work they're doing.
details: Hammering, dragging, heavy things being moved. More than the usual background hum.
I don't mind it during the day, but it started before dawn twice this week.
reactions: Noise is the sound of productivity. Doesn't make it less annoying at five in the morning.

statCheck.pass: They're reorganizing the workspace: moving heavy equipment to better positions. It's a one-time disruption that should make daily operations quieter in the long run.
statCheck.fail: It's been loud. I don't know what they're up to.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been standing through every storm this year without so much as a loose tile. Built to last.
details: The foundation is solid stone and the timber is old-growth. Heavy, dense, unyielding.
Other buildings in the settlement have needed patching after every big wind. This one shrugs it off.
reactions: The builders who put that up knew what they were doing. Every storm proves them right.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew has a superstition about starting work on certain days. They won't begin a new batch on a rest day eve.
details: Something about bad luck carrying over. I don't follow the logic but they're firm about it.
It means one fewer production day some weeks, but the crew won't budge.
reactions: Superstitions are just habits with a story attached. Harmless, mostly.

statCheck.pass: It started because a bad batch was once begun the night before a rest day and went wrong: one coincidence became tradition. Nobody wants to be the one to break it.
statCheck.fail: They have their reasons. I don't argue with tradition.

entries.intro: The lamplight from the {poi_type} is the last thing I see before I fall asleep. My window faces right at it.
details: When the lights go out, I know the crew has finished for the night. That's my cue.
If the lights are still on late, I know something's running behind schedule.
reactions: My sleep schedule is calibrated to someone else's work schedule. That's settlement life.

statCheck.pass: If the lights stay on past the second bell, it means they're working overtime on a rush order. I've learned to read their schedule better than my own.
statCheck.fail: The lights go on and off. I just watch.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been taking on a trainee from {other_settlement}. Sent over to learn the trade.
details: They're young and nervous but willing. The crew has been patient with them.
It's a good exchange. We teach them something, and the connection between settlements strengthens.
reactions: Training someone from elsewhere builds relationships that outlast the apprenticeship.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s well water has a slight mineral taste this week. Not bad, just different.
details: The crew noticed it first thing in the morning. The water is still clear, just tastes of iron.
It comes and goes with the water table. Nothing to worry about.
reactions: Water tells you about the ground it comes from. This week the ground tastes like iron.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew finished a big order ahead of deadline. They're in good spirits.
details: There's a lightness to the place when pressure lifts. People joke more, move easier.
They earned the breather. The next order will come soon enough.
reactions: The satisfaction of finishing early is sweeter than the relief of finishing on time.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s drains are blocked again. Standing water where it shouldn't be.
details: The same spot clogs every few months. Debris washes down and piles up at the bend.
The crew clears it with a stick and some cursing. Temporary fix, permanent problem.
reactions: Some problems don't get solved. They just get managed.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is positioned perfectly to catch the morning breeze. The air flows right through when the doors are open.
details: On a hot day it's the most comfortable spot in the settlement for the first few hours.
The crew opens both ends and lets the cross-draft do the cooling.
reactions: Good placement is the kind of design that doesn't cost anything but saves everything.

entries.intro: The paint on the {poi_type}'s window frames is chipped and peeling. Flakes on the ground below.
details: The wood underneath is grey and dry. Without paint it'll start to rot.
Someone mentioned repainting weeks ago, but it hasn't happened yet.
reactions: Peeling paint is the first sentence in a long story about neglect.

entries.intro: I leaned against the {poi_type}'s wall last summer and the stone was warm as bread from the oven.
details: The dark stone absorbs heat all day and holds it into the evening.
The crew says the walls stay warm to the touch until well after sunset.
reactions: A warm wall on a cool evening is one of those small pleasures nobody talks about.

statCheck.pass: The dark stone is basalt: volcanic rock that absorbs and retains heat better than any other local building material. The builders chose it for that exact property.
statCheck.fail: Dark stone gets warm. That's common enough.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s new {resource_type} output has people talking. Quality that hasn't been seen in years.
details: Whatever they did differently this season, it's working. The product practically sells itself.
Even people who don't care about {resource_type} are impressed. That's how you know it's good.
reactions: Excellence announces itself. You don't need to advertise when the work is that strong.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s work schedule collides with market day now. People can't be in two places at once.
details: The crew misses the morning market every week. They end up with whatever's left over.
It's a scheduling problem nobody wants to solve because changing either side inconveniences someone.
reactions: Compromise is easy when it's someone else compromising.

entries.intro: The birds nesting under the {poi_type}'s eaves are back this spring. Same spot as last year.
details: They built fresh nests right over the old ones. Efficient, I suppose.
The crew watches them carry twigs in and out. It's become the unofficial entertainment.
reactions: Return visitors are the best kind. They chose the place on purpose.

entries.intro: The stones of the {poi_type}'s foundation are massive. Each one must weigh as much as a person.
details: I've always wondered how they moved them into place. No machinery today could do it easily.
The building sits on those stones like a mountain sits on the earth. Immovable.
reactions: Foundations are the one part of a building you get one chance to do right.

statCheck.pass: The foundation stones were quarried locally from an outcrop that's since been exhausted: each block was shaped on site and rolled into position on log rollers. The technique is older than the settlement.
statCheck.fail: They're big stones. I don't know how they got there.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been running short-handed and it shows. Longer waits, rougher finish on the work.
details: The remaining crew is doing their best, but there's a limit to what fewer hands can produce.
Quality drops when people are rushed. That's not a criticism: it's physics.
reactions: You can have speed, quality, or low headcount. Pick two.

statCheck.pass: Two workers are out with the same illness that's been going around. It should pass in a week, but until then the {poi_type} operates at two-thirds capacity.
statCheck.fail: They're short-staffed. I don't know the details.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} has good drainage. Even after a downpour, the ground around it is dry within the hour.
details: The builders graded the land to slope away from the foundation on all sides.
Other buildings in the settlement sit in puddles. This one sheds water like a duck.
reactions: Smart building starts with how the water moves. Everything else follows from that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been a reliable employer for as long as I've lived here. Steady work, steady pay.
details: People build their lives around the certainty it provides. Rent, food, futures.
When you depend on a place for your livelihood, you want it to do well. And it has.
reactions: Gratitude for a steady workplace is the quiet kind. People feel it more than they say it.

entries.intro: The timbers in the {poi_type} creak differently in summer and winter. A warm groan versus a cold snap.
details: Wood expands and contracts with the temperature. You can hear the seasons in the beams.
In deep winter the pops are sharp enough to make you flinch.
reactions: A building that talks to you through its timbers is just being honest about how it feels.

statCheck.pass: The loudest pops come from the ridge beam: it's a single timber that spans the full width and takes the most thermal stress. If it ever cracked truly, you'd hear it from outside.
statCheck.fail: Wood makes noise. That's the limit of what I know.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} wasted a whole day on a bad batch of {resource_type}. Had to scrap it all.
details: The material looked fine going in, but the result was substandard. Can't sell what doesn't meet the bar.
The crew was frustrated but they caught it before it shipped. Better to lose a day than a reputation.
reactions: Scrapping bad work is the right call every time. Even when it stings.

statCheck.pass: The raw stock had been stored improperly and absorbed moisture. Wet material doesn't process the same way, and by the time you notice, you've wasted the whole cycle.
statCheck.fail: The batch was bad. I don't know the technical reason.

entries.intro: There's a knot in the {poi_type}'s main beam that looks like a face. Once you see it, you can't unsee it.
details: It's got two dark spots for eyes and a curved grain line for a mouth. Smiling, almost.
The crew has opinions about whether it's friendly or unsettling. I land on friendly.
reactions: Wood grows in patterns that have nothing to do with us. We just can't help seeing ourselves in them.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s yard is muddy enough to swallow a boot after the last week of rain.
details: The crew laid planks across the worst spots but they sink too. It's mud all the way down.
Everything that goes through the yard comes out brown. Boots, hems, moods.
reactions: Mud season tests everyone's patience. There's no shortcut: you just wait for it to dry.

entries.intro: The crew at the {poi_type} baked something in their break fire yesterday. The whole area smelled wonderful.
details: I don't know what it was, but the aroma carried across the road. Something with spices.
They were eating together outside, laughing about something. Looked like a good day.
reactions: A crew that eats together works together. That's been true since the beginning of work.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s shutter bolt broke and now one window just hangs open. Day and night.
details: Rain gets in when it blows that direction. They've hung a cloth over it as a temporary fix.
The cloth is soaked half the time and flapping the other half. Not much of a solution.
reactions: Temporary fixes become permanent faster than anyone intends.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s well produces the coldest water in the settlement. People come from all over for it.
details: Even in the heat of summer it comes up icy. Something about how deep the well goes.
The crew keeps a shared cup on a hook beside it. Everyone uses it.
reactions: Cold water on a hot day is the simplest luxury and the most appreciated.

entries.intro: The ivy on the {poi_type} has lost its leaves for winter. Just bare brown vines clinging to the wall.
details: It looked so full and green a few weeks ago. Now it's skeletal, every tendril exposed.
Come spring it'll leaf out again and cover the wall in weeks. But right now it looks dead.
reactions: Dormancy looks like death if you don't know the plant. Patience is the only cure.

statCheck.pass: That ivy variety stores energy in its roots during winter: the bare vines aren't dead, they're feeding the root system below the frost line. It comes back stronger each spring.
statCheck.fail: It lost its leaves. Winter does that.

entries.intro: I heard the {poi_type}'s best worker is thinking about leaving. That would be a real loss.
details: They've been the backbone of the crew for years. Hard to replace that kind of experience.
I hope it's just talk. People grumble about change and then stay.
reactions: You never know what you have until the person holding it together considers walking away.

statCheck.pass: They got an offer from someone in {other_settlement}: better pay, less strain. Hard to argue with that.
statCheck.fail: It's a rumor. I don't have the details.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s front porch is the unofficial meeting spot for the neighborhood. People gather there by habit.
details: Nobody planned it. The porch just happens to be at the right intersection, with the right amount of shade.
You can catch up on everything happening in the settlement by standing there for ten minutes.
reactions: A gathering spot chooses itself. No one decides: people just show up.

statCheck.pass: The porch faces the intersection where three paths meet: anyone walking between the main areas of the settlement passes it. The traffic flow made it the natural gathering point.
statCheck.fail: People meet there. I don't know why that spot specifically.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s recent work has been the best I've seen from them. Something clicked.
details: The output quality jumped noticeably. People are placing repeat orders, which is always a good sign.
The crew seems proud without being smug about it. They know they've earned the praise.
reactions: When skill and effort align, the results speak louder than any boast.

entries.intro: The smoke coming from the {poi_type}'s chimney has been thick and dark lately. Heavier than usual.
details: It lingers low instead of rising clean. The air around the building is hazy.
The crew says the fuel quality dropped. They're burning what they have, not what they'd prefer.
reactions: You work with what you've got. Even if what you've got makes the neighbors cough.

statCheck.pass: They're burning green wood that wasn't seasoned long enough: it smokes heavy because the moisture hasn't cooked out. Drier stock would burn clean.
statCheck.fail: The smoke is heavy. Bad fuel, probably.

entries.intro: The shadows that the {poi_type} casts in the late afternoon make interesting shapes on the road.
details: The chimney shadow stretches long and thin. The roofline makes sharp angles against the dirt.
They shift as the sun moves, like a slow clock drawn in dark lines.
reactions: Shadows are the building's way of reaching further than its walls allow.

statCheck.pass: The shadow pattern shifts noticeably through the seasons because the building has an asymmetric roofline: in midsummer the chimney shadow barely reaches the road, in midwinter it crosses the entire street.
statCheck.fail: Shadows move with the sun. Nothing unusual.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew organized their own rest day rotation. Everyone gets a break without the place shutting down.
details: They worked it out among themselves, no outside pressure. Just practical thinking.
Morale improved overnight. People work better when they know a day off is coming.
reactions: A crew that solves its own problems is a crew that doesn't need managing.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s ventilation is poor. You can see the haze inside on a busy day.
details: The air gets thick and people slow down without realizing why.
Opening the windows helps but lets in dust and cold depending on the season. No easy fix.
reactions: Air quality isn't glamorous, but it affects everything from mood to stamina.

statCheck.pass: The roof vents were sealed during a repair last year and never reopened: the convection cycle that used to pull stale air out is blocked. A few hours with a chisel would restore it.
statCheck.fail: It's stuffy in there. I don't know the building well enough to say why.

entries.intro: I brought {npc_name} past the {poi_type} the other day and they had opinions about the construction.
details: They pointed out details I'd never noticed: how the lintel is slightly off-center, how the window arches aren't quite symmetrical.
Once you see the imperfections, you see them every time you walk past.
reactions: Knowing too much about buildings makes the world less tidy. Ignorance was bliss.

entries.intro: The moonlight on the {poi_type}'s roof tiles at night is a sight. Silver on grey, everything quiet.
details: The building looks completely different without people around. Serene, almost reverent.
I only see it when I'm up late, which makes it feel like a secret the night keeps.
reactions: Buildings have a nighttime life that most people never witness.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s storage keeps getting damp. Anything left in there overnight comes out clammy.
details: The walls sweat when the temperature drops. Condensation on every surface by morning.
The crew lines things with oilcloth, but it's a constant battle against the moisture.
reactions: Dampness is the quiet enemy of everything you own.

statCheck.pass: The floor is below ground level on that side, which means groundwater seeps through the walls. A vapor barrier or raising the floor would solve it, but neither is cheap.
statCheck.fail: It's damp. Old buildings are like that sometimes.

entries.intro: The children use the {poi_type}'s front wall for their ball games. Thump, thump, thump all afternoon.
details: The crew tolerates it with good humor. A few of them join in during breaks.
There's a worn spot on the wall now, lighter than the surrounding stone.
reactions: A wall that children play against is a wall in a good neighborhood.

statCheck.pass: The worn spot on the wall is actually good for the stone: the repeated impact has polished the surface smooth and sealed the pores against moisture. The children are accidentally weatherproofing it.
statCheck.fail: Kids play, walls get marked. That's how it goes.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s cobblestone approach is uneven and ankle-turning. Someone's going to get hurt.
details: The stones have shifted over the years, some up, some down. A tripping hazard in every direction.
People who know the path walk it carefully. Newcomers stumble and swear.
reactions: An uneven path isn't charming: it's a broken ankle waiting to happen.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s lantern bracket has a lovely wrought-iron design. Scrollwork, leaves, the works.
details: It's the fanciest thing on an otherwise plain building. Someone cared about that one detail.
When the lantern is lit, the ironwork throws elaborate shadows on the wall.
reactions: One beautiful detail elevates the whole building. Whoever made that bracket knew it.

entries.intro: The wind carries the smell of the {poi_type}'s fires into the settlement most evenings. Woodsmoke and something sharp underneath.
details: I've grown to associate that smell with the end of the workday. A signal more than a scent.
It's strongest right before dark, when the fires are banked and the smoke thickens.
reactions: You can map a settlement by its smells. The {poi_type} has a whole quadrant claimed.

statCheck.pass: The sharp note under the woodsmoke is from the particular stone they use in the firebox: when it heats, it releases a sulfur compound. Harmless, but distinctive.
statCheck.fail: Smoke smells like smoke. I don't analyze it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s newest addition is a covered workspace out front. Simple but effective.
details: Just poles and a sloped roof, open on three sides. Gives the crew shade and rain protection for outdoor tasks.
It went up in two days and looks like it's been there for years.
reactions: The best improvements are the obvious ones nobody thought to do sooner.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s had trouble with its water supply running slow. Takes forever to fill a bucket.
details: The flow is a trickle compared to what it used to be. Everything takes twice as long.
The crew is patient about it, but patience has limits when you're on a schedule.
reactions: Slow water is the kind of problem that makes every other task take longer by association.

statCheck.pass: The pipe is partially blocked with mineral scale: years of hard water built up a crust inside that narrows the flow. A vinegar soak would clear it.
statCheck.fail: The water's slow. Could be anything in the plumbing.

entries.intro: The apprentice at the {poi_type} finished their first solo project this week. You could see the pride.
details: The crew gathered around to inspect it, nodded, said a few words. That's how they mark achievement.
It wasn't perfect, but it was solid and done independently. That counts for a lot.
reactions: The first thing you make on your own is the one you remember forever.

entries.intro: The tree next to the {poi_type} drops leaves on the roof every autumn. A thick, wet blanket of them.
details: They clog the gutters and pile in the corners. The crew climbs up every few days to clear them.
Some people have suggested cutting the tree. The crew won't hear of it.
reactions: A tree is worth the trouble it causes. Even when the trouble is annual.

statCheck.pass: The tree's roots help stabilize the slope behind the building. Cutting it would save the gutters but risk the foundation during heavy rain.
statCheck.fail: They keep the tree. I assume it's sentimental.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been sending samples to {other_settlement} for comparison. Seeing how they stack up.
details: The results came back favorable. Competitive quality, comparable volume.
The crew was relieved more than celebratory. They expected to be close, not behind.
reactions: Measuring yourself against others keeps complacency away. A healthy habit.

entries.intro: The old hook on the {poi_type}'s wall used to hold something. Nobody remembers what.
details: It's sturdy iron, driven deep into the stone. Whatever it held was heavy.
People hang bags on it now, or coils of rope. It's found a second life.
reactions: A good hook outlasts its purpose and finds a new one without any help.

statCheck.pass: That hook was for a pulley system: they used to hoist heavy loads to the upper floor through a window that's since been bricked shut. The hook is the only trace left of the old workflow.
statCheck.fail: It's an old hook. I don't know its history.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a spider problem a while back. Webs in every corner, catching the light.
details: The crew swept them out but they came back within days. Persistent builders, spiders.
They've mostly given up on the high corners. Not worth the ladder.
reactions: You share a building with whatever else decides to live there. That's the arrangement.

statCheck.pass: The spiders are actually keeping the fly population down: the crew figured that out after the first cleaning made the flies worse. Now they leave the high webs alone on purpose.
statCheck.fail: Spiders build webs. That's what they do.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks different in the fog. You can barely make out the roofline.
details: It's like the building is half-remembered instead of fully there. Just a shape in the grey.
The crew says they can hear each other but not see each other crossing the yard on mornings like that.
reactions: Fog turns every familiar thing into something you have to rediscover.

entries.intro: Someone painted a little mark on the {poi_type}'s doorframe. A circle with a line through it.
details: Nobody's claimed responsibility, and nobody's complained about it either.
It's been there a few weeks now. The paint hasn't faded, so it was done with proper stuff.
reactions: A mark on a doorframe is either meaningful or meaningless, and I can't decide which.

statCheck.pass: It's a surveyor's mark: someone measured the building for tax assessment and left their reference point. Routine, but nobody bothered to explain it.
statCheck.fail: It's a mark. I don't know what it means.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been getting a fresh coat of whitewash. Bright enough to squint at in the sun.
details: It looks brand new against the older buildings nearby. Clean lines, no stains.
Give it a season and the weather will bring it back to the same grey as everything else.
reactions: Fresh whitewash is the closest thing a building gets to a bath.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s well ran dry for a day last week. Everyone had to haul water from elsewhere.
details: It came back on its own by morning, but that one day was a scramble.
The crew didn't panic, but you could see the worry under the calm.
reactions: You don't think about water until there isn't any. Then it's all you think about.

statCheck.pass: The water table dips when it hasn't rained for a stretch: the well is shallow enough that a dry spell can temporarily drop below the intake. A deeper well would solve it permanently.
statCheck.fail: Wells go dry sometimes. I don't know the cause.

entries.intro: I like the color the {poi_type}'s stone turns in the evening light. Warm, almost golden.
details: During the day it's just grey stone, nothing special. But right before sunset it transforms.
I've stood and watched it happen. The color slides across the face of the building like a curtain.
reactions: The same building at different hours is a different building entirely.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s delivery was late this week. Threw the whole schedule off.
details: People downstream of the supply chain were standing around waiting. Idle hands, idle pay.
One delay at the source ripples outward until half the settlement feels it.
reactions: A late delivery is never just late. It's early frustration for everyone who was counting on it.

statCheck.pass: The cart axle snapped on the road in and they had to unload, repair, and reload before they could move. A three-hour job that cost the whole day.
statCheck.fail: It was late. I didn't hear why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s roof catches the rain in a way that makes a waterfall off the east side. Quite the sight in a downpour.
details: The water sheets off in a clean curtain. Children stand near it and dare each other to run through.
It's cut a groove in the ground below over the years. A little channel worn into the dirt.
reactions: Rain finds the same paths every time. Patient enough to carve stone, let alone dirt.

statCheck.pass: The roof slope was steepened during a repair years ago: the original pitch shed water gently, but the new angle concentrates it into that single cascade. A gutter would fix it.
statCheck.fail: Water runs off roofs. That one just does it dramatically.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been producing solid {resource_type} all month. Consistent quality, no complaints.
details: When the output is steady, people stop talking about it. That's the best compliment.
The crew just keeps their heads down and works. No drama, no surprises.
reactions: Consistency doesn't get celebrated, but it's the thing everything else depends on.

entries.intro: There's a stray cat that's claimed the {poi_type} as its territory. Sits on the wall and watches everyone.
details: The crew feeds it scraps, so it's not going anywhere. Fat and content.
It hisses at dogs but ignores people entirely. The undisputed lord of that wall.
reactions: A cat that chooses a building has better judgment than most tenants.

statCheck.pass: The cat showed up the same week the rat problem ended. The crew figured out the connection and has been feeding it ever since: cheapest exterminator they've ever had.
statCheck.fail: It's a cat that sits there. That's all I know.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been drawing {resource_type} buyers from out of town. Word travels when quality is good.
details: They make the trip specifically to buy here instead of sourcing locally. That says something.
The crew is flattered but trying not to let it go to their heads.
reactions: When people come to you instead of the other way around, you've done something right.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s sign is crooked. Has been for weeks.
details: One nail came loose and nobody's climbed up to fix it. So it hangs at an angle.
It's become a landmark of its own. 'Turn left at the crooked sign.'
reactions: A crooked sign is the kind of thing you stop noticing until someone points it out.

statCheck.pass: The crew hasn't fixed it because the crooked sign is actually drawing more attention than a straight one would. People look up at it, and looking up means noticing the business.
statCheck.fail: The sign is crooked. Nobody's gotten to it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} lost a whole batch to spoilage. Weeks of work gone overnight.
details: The mood around the place has been grim since. Nobody's pointing fingers, but everyone's frustrated.
They'll recover, but it's a setback that stings more than it should because it was avoidable.
reactions: Losing work you've already done is worse than never starting. The effort makes the loss heavier.

statCheck.pass: Someone left the storage open and a night of unexpected humidity ruined everything that was curing. A simple mistake with an expensive consequence.
statCheck.fail: Something spoiled. I don't know the specifics.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s chimney has a lean to it that I've never liked. Slight, but visible.
details: I'm probably the only one who notices. But once you see it, it nags at you.
It's been that way for years and hasn't gotten worse. Probably fine.
reactions: A leaning chimney is either a problem or a personality trait. Hard to tell which.

statCheck.pass: The chimney was built on a footing that settled unevenly in the first few years. It shifted and then stopped: the lean is stable now, just not plumb.
statCheck.fail: It leans a bit. I'm not a builder, so I can't say why.

entries.intro: The morning crew at the {poi_type} starts so early you can hear them before dawn. Doors, voices, footsteps.
details: I've learned their routine by sound alone. The heavy door, then the water pump, then the fire.
My mornings start when theirs do, whether I want them to or not.
reactions: Living near a workplace means your schedule isn't entirely your own.

statCheck.pass: The crew tried shifting the start time later by an hour, but the output dropped because the afternoon heat forced an earlier stop. The early start is the lesser of two problems.
statCheck.fail: They start early. I don't know their reasons.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks sturdy enough to outlast us all. Thick walls, solid joints.
details: Everything about the construction says 'built to endure.' No shortcuts, no cheap materials.
Our grandchildren will probably walk past it and not think twice about how well it was made.
reactions: Good construction is invisible. You only notice buildings when they start to fail.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s gutters overflow every time it rains hard. Water sheeting down the walls.
details: The staining from the runoff is visible from the road. Brown streaks down the stonework.
The crew puts buckets under the worst spots, but that's just catching the symptom.
reactions: Water damage is the kind of problem that's easy to ignore and expensive to fix.

statCheck.pass: The gutters are undersized for the roof area: whoever installed them calculated for a smaller building. The roof was extended but the drainage never caught up.
statCheck.fail: The gutters overflow. Could be a lot of things.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s got a reputation for fair dealing. People trust what comes out of there.
details: That kind of trust takes years to build and one bad transaction to lose.
The crew knows it too. They're careful with every order, every promise.
reactions: A good reputation is the most valuable thing a place can own. Can't buy it, can only earn it.

entries.intro: Weeds are coming up between the stones around the {poi_type}. Nobody's pulled them in weeks.
details: Some of them are knee-high already. Another week and they'll be waist-high.
The crew says it's not their job, and whoever's job it is hasn't done it.
reactions: Weeds don't care whose responsibility they are. They just grow.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s front step is worn smooth from years of boots. Polished like marble.
details: There's a dip in the center where the most feet have landed. A shallow bowl ground by traffic.
It's the kind of wear that tells you a place has been important for a long time.
reactions: Worn stone is a record of everyone who passed through. A history written in footsteps.

statCheck.pass: The wear on the step is deepest on the right side because the door hinges left: everyone steps right to pass it. The foot traffic pattern is written into the stone.
statCheck.fail: The step is worn smooth. Lots of people use it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s output dropped this month. Not dramatically, but enough to notice.
details: Orders are backing up by a day or two. Nothing urgent, but the cushion is gone.
The crew is working the same hours. It's the materials that have been inconsistent.
reactions: A small drop today becomes a big problem next month if nobody addresses it.

statCheck.pass: The supplier changed their source for raw stock and the new material processes slower: same effort, less yield. The crew has been asking for the old supply back.
statCheck.fail: Output is down a bit. I don't know the reason.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s weather vane spins freely in the lightest breeze. A copper arrow pointing nowhere in particular.
details: On calm days it drifts lazily, can't commit to a direction. On windy days it points true.
I check it out of habit when I pass, even though I could just look at the clouds.
reactions: A weather vane is a simple thing that gives you one piece of useful information. That's enough.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} always smells like work. Sawdust, metal, oil, something I can't name.
details: The smell is in the walls by now. You could scrub the whole place and it would still carry that scent.
I find it oddly comforting. It smells like productivity.
reactions: A working building smells like what it does. That's honest.

statCheck.pass: The smell is actually sealed into the walls by years of heat and oil: the porous stone absorbed the work like a sponge absorbs water. Even an empty building would carry that scent for decades.
statCheck.fail: It smells like work. I've never thought about why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew took on a big order from {other_settlement}. They've been running double shifts.
details: The place is lit and active well past normal hours. The crew looks tired but motivated.
A big order means good money, but it also means everyone is stretched thin until it's done.
reactions: Prosperity and exhaustion walk hand in hand when the work piles up.

entries.intro: A wildflower patch has taken hold next to the {poi_type}. Purple and yellow blooms all along the wall.
details: Nobody planted them. Seeds blew in, found the right soil, and now there's a garden.
The crew has been careful not to trample them. They seem proud of the accident.
reactions: The best gardens are the ones that plant themselves.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s flagstone cracked during the freeze. A clean split right down the middle.
details: Water got in the cracks, froze, expanded, and the stone gave way. Simple physics.
They'll need to replace it before it crumbles further, but stone isn't cheap.
reactions: Ice is patient and strong. Given enough winters, it'll crack anything.

statCheck.pass: The stone was cut along its natural grain instead of across it: water enters the grain lines and when it freezes, the expansion follows the weakness. A crosscut stone would have held.
statCheck.fail: Ice cracked the stone. That happens.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been running so smoothly lately that people barely mention it. That's the goal.
details: No complaints, no drama, just steady work going out and fair pay coming in.
The crew has found their rhythm and they're keeping it.
reactions: When nobody talks about a workplace, it means everything is working.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s door sticks in damp weather. You have to shoulder it open.
details: The wood swells when it's humid and the frame won't give. Everyone who enters gives it a shove.
In dry weather it swings freely. The building breathes with the seasons.
reactions: A sticky door is a minor annoyance that you adjust to without even thinking about it.

statCheck.pass: The frame is oak but the door is pine: they expand at different rates with moisture, so the fit changes with the weather. Matching the woods would solve it permanently.
statCheck.fail: The door sticks when it's damp. Old doors do that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had its busiest week in memory. The crew could barely keep up.
details: Every surface was in use, every hand occupied. The kind of rush you can't prepare for.
By the end of the week they were exhausted but the backlog was cleared.
reactions: A rush like that tests a crew's limits. Passing the test feels good once it's over.

statCheck.pass: A competing operation in {other_settlement} shut down temporarily, and their orders diverted here. Once they reopen, the volume will normalize.
statCheck.fail: It was busy. I don't know what caused the surge.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s rainwater barrel is overflowing. Nobody's drawn from it in days.
details: The overflow is running down the side and pooling underneath. Mosquitoes already found it.
Someone should either use the water or tip the barrel. Sitting full and ignored helps nobody.
reactions: Unused resources are just problems waiting to happen.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s morning routine is like clockwork. Same order, same sounds, same time every day.
details: First the fire gets stoked, then the shutters open, then the tools come out. Never varies.
I could set my day by their schedule. Probably more accurate than any clock.
reactions: Routine is the backbone of every productive place. Not exciting, but reliable.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s windows catch the morning sun perfectly. The whole front glows.
details: Whoever designed the building oriented it just right. A happy accident or clever planning.
The crew keeps the glass clean, which makes the effect even stronger.
reactions: Good light makes a building feel welcoming before you even step inside.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s inventory records are apparently a mess. Numbers don't match stock.
details: It's not theft, just sloppy bookkeeping that's accumulated over months.
The crew has been putting off the audit because nobody wants to do the count.
reactions: Putting off tedious work doesn't make it smaller. It just makes it more tedious when you finally do it.

statCheck.pass: Three different people have been keeping separate tallies and none of them reconcile. A single ledger and one person responsible would sort it out in a day.
statCheck.fail: The numbers are off. That's all I know.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s stone walls hold the cool in summer. It's the most comfortable place to be on a hot day.
details: Thick walls act like a buffer against the heat. The air inside is always a few degrees cooler.
The crew knows this and works through the hottest hours without complaint.
reactions: A cool building on a hot day is a luxury that costs nothing to maintain.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s loading area is a bottleneck every delivery day. Carts backed up, people waiting.
details: There's only room for one cart at a time, so they queue on the road and block traffic.
Everyone has an opinion about how to fix it, but nobody has the authority to change the layout.
reactions: A bottleneck that everyone recognizes and nobody fixes is the most frustrating kind.

statCheck.pass: The original design included a turnaround loop for carts, but it was filled in when the yard was expanded. Now carts have to back out into traffic to leave.
statCheck.fail: It gets congested on delivery days. That's all I see.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s old bell still rings every day at noon. Clear as the day it was hung.
details: It's more tradition than timekeeping now. Everyone already knows what time it is.
But the sound is part of the settlement's rhythm. It would feel wrong without it.
reactions: Traditions survive because removing them would leave a gap nobody can fill.

statCheck.pass: The bell was cast from copper and tin salvaged from the settlement's first failed enterprise: a smelting operation that went under. The bell is the only thing left from that era.
statCheck.fail: It's an old bell that rings at noon. That's all I know about it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been quiet lately. Less activity than usual.
details: Not closed, just slow. Fewer people around, shorter hours.
The crew seems unbothered. Maybe it's just a seasonal lull.
reactions: Quiet periods are either restful or worrying, depending on how long they last.

statCheck.pass: Their main buyer cut their standing order in half: not because of quality, just belt-tightening on their end. The {poi_type} is waiting it out.
statCheck.fail: It's been quiet. I don't know why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew celebrated a milestone this week. Years of operation, I think.
details: They put out a barrel and shared drinks with whoever stopped by. Low-key but genuine.
The mood was warm: pride without ceremony, just people who've worked together a long time.
reactions: Milestones are best celebrated simply. A drink and a nod between people who earned it.

entries.intro: The rats got into the {poi_type}'s grain store. Chewed through the sacking overnight.
details: The crew found droppings and holes in the morning. Had to sort out what was salvageable.
They've set traps and moved the sacks off the floor, but the damage is done.
reactions: Rats don't respect your plans. They just see opportunity.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} sits at the highest point in the settlement. You can see its roofline from everywhere.
details: It's the first thing you spot coming in from the road. A natural landmark.
The elevation gives it a slight authority over the surrounding buildings, even if that wasn't intended.
reactions: Position matters more than size when it comes to presence.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s new hire is working out well. Fits right in with the existing crew.
details: They picked up the routines quickly and the others have accepted them without fuss.
Good hiring is invisible. When it works, it just looks like the crew was always that size.
reactions: A new person who fits smoothly into a team is worth more than two who don't.

entries.intro: The floorboards at the {poi_type} creak in a specific sequence when you walk across. Like a wooden melody.
details: Each board has its own note. The crew knows which ones to avoid during early shifts.
Visitors always pause when the floor talks back at them. The crew doesn't even hear it anymore.
reactions: You stop hearing the sounds of a place once it becomes yours. Familiarity is selective deafness.

statCheck.pass: The loudest board is directly over an air gap where the joist shrank away from its support: the board flexes into empty space, which amplifies the creak. A shim would silence it.
statCheck.fail: The floor creaks. Most old floors do.

entries.intro: The fence around the {poi_type}'s yard is sagging in the middle. Needs new posts.
details: It's been leaning for months and the lean is getting worse. One strong wind might flatten it.
The crew props it up with spare lumber, which looks terrible but keeps it standing.
reactions: A sagging fence is an honest fence. It tells you exactly how much attention it's been getting.

entries.intro: I walked past the {poi_type} at dusk and the firelight in the windows was beautiful. Warm orange against the blue sky.
details: There were silhouettes of people moving inside, going about the last tasks of the day.
From outside it looked peaceful. Like a painting of someone else's evening.
reactions: A lit window at dusk is one of the most human sights there is.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s tools are getting dull and nobody's sharpened them in a while.
details: Dull tools mean more effort for worse results. The crew is compensating with muscle instead of edge.
Sharpening is one of those maintenance tasks that gets skipped when things are busy.
reactions: A dull tool doesn't cut less. It just makes you work harder for the same result.

entries.intro: The dust that comes off the {poi_type} coats everything nearby. My window sill is always gritty.
details: It settles on clothes hung out to dry, on market stalls, on the road. A fine layer of work.
I've stopped fighting it. Wiping just makes room for the next layer.
reactions: Living near a working place means accepting its byproducts as part of your life.

statCheck.pass: The dust is worst on days when they're processing dry material with the wind blowing this direction. Wet days and calm days are fine, so it's a weather problem as much as a work problem.
statCheck.fail: Everything near the {poi_type} gets dusty. That's all I know.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew put up a noticeboard outside. Orders, schedules, the odd joke.
details: People stop to read it even if they have no business there. It's become casual entertainment.
The jokes change weekly. Someone on the crew has a good sense of humor.
reactions: A noticeboard is a window into a workplace's personality.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s threshold stone is polished dark from years of boots scraping. Like glass in the center.
details: The edges are still rough and pale, but the walking path is worn smooth and shiny.
It tells you the building sees heavy traffic, just from looking at the ground.
reactions: Wear patterns are honest records. They show you how a place is actually used.

entries.intro: The crew at the {poi_type} got into a disagreement about methods this week. Loud, but brief.
details: They argued it out in the open, came to a decision, and moved on. No lingering grudge.
That's the sign of a healthy crew: they fight about the work, not about each other.
reactions: A disagreement that resolves itself in the same afternoon is just collaboration with volume.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s output of {resource_type} has slowed to a trickle. People are starting to talk.
details: What used to take a day now takes three. The crew says the source material is harder to work.
Buyers are patient for now, but patience isn't infinite.
reactions: Slow production makes everyone nervous, even when there's a good explanation.

statCheck.pass: The easy stock has been used up and what's left requires more processing: same raw material, more labor per unit. The cost per batch has gone up noticeably.
statCheck.fail: It's slow. I haven't heard a clear reason.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s roof leaks when the rain comes from the north. A specific, predictable flaw.
details: The crew positions buckets before northerly storms with practiced efficiency.
They've patched it twice but the water finds a new way in every time.
reactions: A leak you can predict is almost tolerable. Almost.

statCheck.pass: The north-facing roofline meets an older section at a bad angle: the joint can't be sealed properly without tearing off both sections and rebuilding the junction. A major job for a minor leak.
statCheck.fail: It leaks in certain weather. I don't know the building details.

entries.intro: There's a patch of moss on the {poi_type}'s north wall that's been growing for years. Deep green and soft.
details: It doesn't harm the stone, or so I'm told. Just sits there, quietly alive.
In winter it goes dark and dormant. In spring it comes back brighter than before.
reactions: Moss on a building is a sign that nature and construction have come to terms.

statCheck.pass: That moss species only grows where the stone stays damp year-round: it tells you that wall never fully dries, which means there's seepage behind the surface that the builders should address.
statCheck.fail: It's moss. It grows on the shady side.

entries.intro: The crew at the {poi_type} has been sharing meals together more often. Eating outside when the weather is good.
details: They've set up a makeshift table from a plank and two barrels. Simple but functional.
You can hear them laughing from the road. Good morale is loud.
reactions: A crew that breaks bread together works better together. Always has.

entries.intro: The smell from the {poi_type} has been stronger than usual this week. Gets in your clothes.
details: I hung laundry out and it came in smelling like the place. Not pleasant.
The crew doesn't notice because they're in it all day. The rest of us notice plenty.
reactions: You go nose-blind to your own workplace. Everyone else has to live with it.

statCheck.pass: They changed to a cheaper processing method that produces more odor as a byproduct. Saves money, costs goodwill with the neighbors.
statCheck.fail: It smells more than usual. I don't know what changed.

entries.intro: I notice the {poi_type} every time I walk to market. It's on the most-traveled route in the settlement.
details: You'd think I'd stop seeing it after this many years, but some buildings hold your eye.
It anchors that stretch of road. Without it, the walk would feel different.
reactions: Some buildings define a route just by being there. Remove them and the path feels empty.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s newest equipment is working well. Faster output, cleaner results.
details: The investment paid for itself within the first month, or so the crew says.
It's satisfying to see a practical improvement that actually improves things.
reactions: Spending wisely on the right tools is the smartest thing a workplace can do.

entries.intro: The ground around the {poi_type} is hard-packed and bare. Nothing grows in the traffic zone.
details: Boots, carts, and daily use have beaten the earth into something like stone.
After rain it's a slick of mud. After dry spells it's a cloud of dust.
reactions: Busy ground is bald ground. That's just the cost of being a useful place.

statCheck.pass: The bare ground extends exactly as far as the carts turn: the turning radius of a loaded cart defines the dead zone. Widen the approach and vegetation would reclaim half of it.
statCheck.fail: Nothing grows where people walk. That's normal.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s fire pit was left smoldering overnight and nobody noticed until morning.
details: Luckily nothing caught. The embers just burned down to white ash.
The crew got a stern reminder about banking fires. Could have been much worse.
reactions: Carelessness with fire is the kind of mistake you only get away with so many times.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s woodpile is stacked neatly as a wall. Someone takes pride in that.
details: Every log is the same length, bark out, ends aligned. You could measure with it.
A tidy woodpile tells you the person who stacked it cares about the small things.
reactions: Order in the small things usually means order in the big things too.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew has been grumbling about their tools. Old, worn, and overdue for replacement.
details: Half the handles are splintered and wrapped in cord. The blades are nicked and unevenly ground.
They make do, but making do is slower and harder than working with proper equipment.
reactions: Good workers deserve good tools. Anything less is a tax on their skill.

entries.intro: The climbing vine on the {poi_type} has reached the roofline. It'll be over the top by next year.
details: Some people think it looks charming. Others say it's eating the mortar between the stones.
The debate has been going on as long as the vine has been climbing.
reactions: Plants and buildings negotiate their boundary over decades. The plant usually wins.

statCheck.pass: The vine's roots have already loosened several stones near the window: you can wiggle them by hand. Another year and the repair bill doubles.
statCheck.fail: The vine grows. Whether it's a problem or not, I couldn't say.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s output has earned praise from a trader who usually complains about everything.
details: When that person says something nice, you know it's genuine. They don't give compliments freely.
The crew was quietly pleased. That kind of validation means more than easy praise.
reactions: A compliment from a critic is worth ten from a flatterer.

entries.intro: A cart wheel broke right outside the {poi_type} yesterday. Spilled the load across the road.
details: The crew came out and helped clean up without being asked. Had it sorted within the hour.
Traffic was diverted around the mess. A minor inconvenience that felt like chaos in the moment.
reactions: A broken wheel is nobody's fault and everybody's problem for the next hour.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} is sheltered from the worst winds by its position. Smart placement.
details: The surrounding terrain breaks the gusts before they reach the building.
On days when the rest of the settlement is battling the wind, the {poi_type} sits calm.
reactions: Location is the one decision you can't undo. Getting it right matters more than anything you build.

statCheck.pass: The builders chose that spot deliberately: the hill behind acts as a windbreak and the slope away prevents flooding. The best-sheltered position in the settlement.
statCheck.fail: It's in a good spot. Lucky, I suppose.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s latrine situation is, frankly, overdue for attention. Everyone complains.
details: The current setup is too close to the working area and the wind carries the smell.
The crew has been promised improvements for months. Nothing has changed.
reactions: Sanitation is unglamorous but essential. Ignoring it doesn't make it less urgent.

entries.intro: I saw {npc_name} fixing something at the {poi_type} after hours. Working by lamplight, alone.
details: They were focused, taking their time. Nobody asked them to do it.
That kind of unpaid care for a workplace says something about a person.
reactions: People who fix things on their own time care about the place, not the credit.

statCheck.pass: {npc_name} noticed a fault that would have caused a bigger problem by morning. They couldn't sleep knowing it was there, so they went and dealt with it.
statCheck.fail: They were working late. I didn't ask why.

entries.intro: The mortar between the {poi_type}'s stones is crumbling in places. You can pick it out with a fingernail.
details: It's mostly cosmetic for now, but water gets in the gaps and the freeze cycle will widen them.
Repointing the joints is a tedious job, but it's cheaper than rebuilding the wall later.
reactions: Crumbling mortar is a building asking quietly for help. Most people don't hear it until it's shouting.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s schedule lines up with the sunset nicely this time of year. The crew leaves as the light fades.
details: There's something right about ending work with the day. Natural rhythm.
In winter the math changes and they finish in the dark. Less pleasant.
reactions: Working by daylight and resting by dark is how things ought to be.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been a good neighbor to the surrounding houses. No major disputes in years.
details: They keep the noise reasonable, the mess contained, and the smoke pointing the right way.
A workplace that respects its neighbors is a rare and valuable thing.
reactions: Being a good neighbor is just doing your work without making it everyone else's problem.

entries.intro: The rain turned the path to the {poi_type} into a stream. Water running right through the ruts.
details: People were stepping on the high edges to keep their feet dry, balancing like cats.
By afternoon the water had subsided, but the ruts are deeper now. Worse for the next rain.
reactions: Every rain digs the path a little deeper. Erosion works on its own schedule.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s {resource_type} is in demand this season. People are buying as fast as it's made.
details: The crew can barely keep stock on hand. Everything goes out the same day it's finished.
It's good for the settlement's trade. Having a desirable product brings visitors.
reactions: Demand that outpaces supply is the best problem a workplace can have.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s canopy is sagging under the weight of last night's snow. Bowing in the middle.
details: The crew is up there with brooms knocking it off before the supports give way.
Wet snow is heavier than it looks. A finger's width can weigh more than a season of rain.
reactions: Snow is beautiful right up until it starts breaking things.

statCheck.pass: The canopy supports were sized for rain load, not snow: rain runs off but snow accumulates. The original builders came from a warmer region and didn't account for heavy snowfall.
statCheck.fail: Snow is heavy. The canopy wasn't built for it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} casts the longest shadow of any building here. You can tell the time by where it falls.
details: At noon it barely reaches the road. By late afternoon it crosses to the other side.
I've caught myself checking the shadow instead of the sun. It's oddly reliable.
reactions: A sundial made of architecture. Someone should be proud of that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been borrowing workers from other trades to keep up with demand.
details: People with different skills are learning new ones on the job. Some adapt faster than others.
It's a temporary arrangement, but the cross-training might benefit the settlement long-term.
reactions: A person with two trades is twice as useful when the need arises.

statCheck.pass: The cross-trained workers are actually being evaluated for permanent reassignment: the {poi_type} is growing and needs dedicated staff, and this is a quiet audition.
statCheck.fail: They're helping out temporarily. I don't know the details.

entries.intro: The lock on the {poi_type}'s supply room jammed. They had to break it off to get in.
details: Now there's no lock at all, just an open door. The crew takes turns keeping watch.
A replacement lock has been ordered, but deliveries take time.
reactions: Security you never think about becomes obvious the moment it fails.

statCheck.pass: The lock didn't jam on its own: the cold warped the iron bracket that holds it, putting pressure on the mechanism until it seized. The bracket needs replacing, not just the lock.
statCheck.fail: The lock broke. These things happen.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks handsome with fresh snow on the roof. Clean white lines on dark timber.
details: The smoke from the chimney rises straight up in the cold air. The whole scene looks composed.
By afternoon the snow will be gone or grey, but for the first hour it's perfect.
reactions: Fresh snow forgives every flaw. Even the plainest building looks dignified under it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s newest worker is still learning the routines. I can tell by how they hesitate.
details: They double-check everything and ask questions the experienced crew answers without looking up.
The gap between knowing and doing closes with repetition. They'll be fine.
reactions: Everyone was new once. The awkward phase passes faster than it feels.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s waste pile is getting too large. It spills onto the path now.
details: Scraps, offcuts, and unusable material piled higher than the fence.
The crew planned a cleanup day but it keeps getting postponed.
reactions: A waste pile is a to-do list in physical form. The bigger it gets, the less likely it shrinks.

statCheck.pass: The waste pile contains usable material that a scrap buyer from {other_settlement} would pay for: someone just needs to sort the valuable offcuts from the true rubbish.
statCheck.fail: It's a pile of waste. I don't look closely.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s water wheel is mesmerizing. I've stood and watched it turn for longer than I'd admit.
details: The rhythm of it is hypnotic: steady, constant, no hesitation.
Water in, motion out. The simplest engine there is.
reactions: There's something calming about watching something work without effort.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s cart horse has been off its feed this week. The crew is worried.
details: A sick horse means no deliveries. Everything depends on one animal being well.
They've been hand-feeding it and walking it gently. Patience and hay.
reactions: A workplace's productivity sometimes depends on the health of one animal. Fragile system.

statCheck.pass: The horse ate something it shouldn't have in the pasture: a patch of bitter weed that upsets the stomach. It'll pass in a few days, but the grazing area needs clearing.
statCheck.fail: The horse is unwell. I don't know animals well enough to guess why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been painted by the same person for years. Each time, a slightly different shade.
details: They mix the paint from local pigments, so no two batches match exactly.
The building has subtle layers of color if you look closely: history in paint.
reactions: Imperfect matching gives a place more character than perfection ever could.

statCheck.pass: The painter mixes iron oxide from local clay with linseed oil: each clay source gives a slightly different tint. The color variations map to where the clay was dug that year.
statCheck.fail: The paint changes shade. Local materials, I assume.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew has been mentoring the younger workers more deliberately this season.
details: Teaching takes time away from production, but the investment pays off over years.
The experienced workers seem to enjoy passing on what they know. Pride in the craft.
reactions: A trade that gets taught well survives. One that doesn't gets forgotten.

entries.intro: Frost crept across the {poi_type}'s windows this morning. Intricate patterns in the ice.
details: Each pane had a different design, like the cold was improvising. Ferns, feathers, crystals.
The crew scraped it off to let the light in. Gone in seconds.
reactions: Frost art is the most temporary beauty there is. Blink and it's water.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had a visitor from {other_settlement} who spent the whole day inspecting the operation.
details: They took notes, asked questions, measured things. Very thorough, very quiet about their purpose.
The crew cooperated but was uneasy. Nobody likes being evaluated without knowing why.
reactions: An inspector who doesn't explain their interest makes everyone imagine the worst.

statCheck.pass: They were a buyer scouting a potential long-term supply contract: they wanted to verify quality and capacity before committing. Good news if it works out.
statCheck.fail: Someone came and looked around. I don't know what for.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s chimney smoke rises straight on calm days. A perfect vertical line.
details: When the wind picks up it bends and stretches like a grey ribbon across the sky.
I've started reading the weather by how the smoke behaves. More accurate than my joints.
reactions: Smoke is the most honest weathervane. It goes exactly where the air tells it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s contribution to the settlement's trade this year has been impressive.
details: More volume, better quality, reliable timing. The trifecta that traders actually care about.
Other settlements have noticed. We're developing a reputation, and it starts there.
reactions: One well-run operation lifts the whole settlement's standing.

entries.intro: A heavy branch came down on the {poi_type}'s shed in the last storm. Punched right through the roof.
details: The shed's a wreck, but the main building is untouched. Lucky positioning.
Clearing the debris and patching the shed will take a few days of work.
reactions: Storm damage is always random. What gets hit and what doesn't is pure chance.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s doorway is framed by two old carved posts. Worn but dignified.
details: The carvings are geometric: circles, lines, interlocking angles. Simple but precise.
Years of weather have softened the edges, but the pattern is still clear.
reactions: Craftsmanship that outlasts the carver's lifetime is craftsmanship done right.

statCheck.pass: The pattern is a traditional builder's mark: each shape represents a material or technique used in the construction. It's a blueprint carved in shorthand, if you know how to read it.
statCheck.fail: They're old carvings. Decorative, I assume.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been a fixture of the settlement for longer than anyone remembers.
details: People talk about it the way you talk about the hills or the river: it was always there.
Newcomers assume it's old. Locals forget it has an age at all.
reactions: The oldest things in a settlement become invisible. They're part of the landscape.

statCheck.pass: The oldest section of the {poi_type} uses a different construction method than the rest: larger stones, no mortar, just friction-fit. That technique predates the current settlement entirely.
statCheck.fail: It's old. I don't know how old.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s workers planted a shade tree out front last spring. It's taken well.
details: It's still young, barely taller than a person, but you can see what it'll become in ten years.
They water it religiously. Someone on the crew has adopted it as their personal project.
reactions: Planting a tree you won't sit under for years is the most optimistic thing a person can do.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s floor has a low spot where water pools when someone spills. A small lake forms.
details: The crew mops it up, but it always collects in the same spot. The floor won't forget its shape.
Eventually the boards in that spot will rot from repeated moisture. Predictable and slow.
reactions: Small problems with fixed locations are easy to understand and hard to fix.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s afternoon light is beautiful. Dusty gold beams slanting through the high windows.
details: The dust in the air catches the light and makes it visible: solid bars of gold from window to floor.
The crew works in those beams without a thought. I'd sit in that light all day if I could.
reactions: Good light is wasted on people too busy to notice it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s order from {other_settlement} fell through at the last minute. All that preparation for nothing.
details: The crew had already allocated materials and time. Now they have excess stock and an empty schedule.
It's not the first time and it won't be the last. Trade depends on trust, and trust breaks.
reactions: A canceled order is worse than no order. You've spent the effort already.

statCheck.pass: The buyer found a cheaper source and backed out without notice. No contract, no obligation: just a handshake that turned out to be worth nothing.
statCheck.fail: The deal fell apart. I don't know the details.

entries.intro: The echo inside the {poi_type} is remarkable. Clap your hands and you hear it bounce twice.
details: The stone walls and high ceiling make every sound ring. Conversations carry further than you'd expect.
The crew has learned to talk quietly when they don't want to be overheard.
reactions: A room with an echo makes secrets difficult and announcements easy.

statCheck.pass: The echo comes from the ceiling height combined with parallel stone walls: the sound bounces between the flat surfaces without anything to absorb it. Hanging cloth or tapestry would deaden it.
statCheck.fail: It echoes. Stone rooms do that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s well-kept appearance says a lot about the crew. They take pride in the place.
details: The windows are clean, the path is swept, and everything is where it should be.
It's not fancy, just maintained. That's enough to set it apart.
reactions: Maintenance is the most underrated form of craftsmanship.

entries.intro: There's a puddle that forms near the {poi_type} every time it rains. Ankle-deep and unavoidable.
details: People take running starts to jump over it or resign themselves to wet boots.
It dries within a day, but you'd think someone would fill in the low spot eventually.
reactions: That puddle has probably ruined more pairs of boots than anything else in the settlement.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s earliest shift starts with the roosters. I know because I hear both.
details: The rooster crows, then the door creaks, then the first tools clank. The same order every morning.
I don't need to be awake, but the sound travels and here I am.
reactions: Roosters and workplaces are natural allies. Both exist to end your sleep.

statCheck.pass: The rooster belongs to the crew's senior member, who keeps it specifically to mark the shift start. It's not an accident: it's a living alarm clock with feathers.
statCheck.fail: Roosters crow at dawn, and the crew starts at dawn. Coincidence, probably.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been a training ground for half the tradespeople in the settlement. Good tradition.
details: People come through, learn the basics, and move on to their own work.
The skills they carry with them started in that building. It's a quiet legacy.
reactions: A place that teaches people well is worth more than a place that just produces goods.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s supply costs went up this month. Same materials, higher price.
details: The supplier blamed transport costs, but the crew suspects opportunism.
Either way, the margins are tighter now. Less room for error.
reactions: When costs rise but prices stay the same, someone at the end of the chain absorbs the hit.

statCheck.pass: The supply route changed due to road conditions and the extra distance adds genuine cost. It's not gouging, just geography being inconvenient.
statCheck.fail: Prices went up. I don't know the supply chain well enough to say why.

entries.intro: I counted the windows on the {poi_type} once out of boredom. More than I expected.
details: Fourteen, if you count the ones on the side you can't see from the road.
Most buildings here have four or five. That many windows means good ventilation and light.
reactions: Boredom makes you notice things you'd never think to look at.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew swapped shift patterns and everyone's happier for it.
details: The early shift now ends earlier, the late shift starts later. Simpler rhythm.
It cost nothing to change and improved morale overnight. Should have done it sooner.
reactions: The easiest improvements are often the ones sitting right in front of you.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s foundation stones are sweating moisture in the heat. Dark patches on the base.
details: The stone pulls water from deep underground and it surfaces when the air is hot enough.
It looks like the building is perspiring. Unnerving if you don't know it's normal.
reactions: Stone that sweats is stone that breathes. Better that than sealed and cracking.

statCheck.pass: The building sits on a clay layer that holds groundwater near the surface: the stone wicks it up through capillary action. In dry seasons the patches disappear entirely.
statCheck.fail: The stones get damp in the heat. I don't know why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s awning provides the only shade on that stretch of road. Everyone stops under it.
details: On hot days there's a cluster of people there who have no business with the place. Just seeking shade.
The crew doesn't mind. It gives them an audience.
reactions: Shade is a public good that costs nothing to share.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s waste goes into a pit that's getting full. It'll need digging out soon.
details: The crew has been putting it off because it's the worst job on the roster.
Another month and it overflows. Unpleasant now, disgusting then.
reactions: The jobs nobody wants to do are always the ones that can't be skipped.

entries.intro: The wind whistles through the {poi_type}'s eaves at night. A low, constant note.
details: It changes pitch with the wind speed: higher in gusts, lower in steady breeze.
The crew is used to it but visitors find it eerie the first time.
reactions: A building that sings in the wind has more personality than most people I know.

statCheck.pass: The gap between the roofline and the wall has an uneven spacing that creates the whistle: one section acts like a pipe, funneling air through a narrow slot. Sealing it would stop the noise, but the crew likes it.
statCheck.fail: It whistles when the wind blows. Old buildings do that.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s stone steps hold heat well into the evening. Warm to sit on after sunset.
details: The crew sits there some evenings, winding down after the day's work.
I've joined them once or twice. Good conversation comes easily on warm stone.
reactions: A warm step and a quiet evening is all the luxury most people need.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s drainage ditch is clogged with leaves and silt. The water has nowhere to go.
details: It's been backing up and softening the ground near the foundation. Not good for the building.
Cleaning a ditch is half an hour of work that prevents weeks of damage.
reactions: Prevention is cheap and repair is expensive. Everyone knows this and nobody acts on it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s lamp is the first thing you see coming into the settlement at night. A beacon.
details: It's visible from the main road long before the other buildings. A warm point in the dark.
Travelers have told me they use it to navigate. It's a landmark without trying to be one.
reactions: One light in the right spot can make an entire settlement feel welcoming.

entries.intro: The noise from the {poi_type} has been bothering {npc_name}. They've mentioned it more than once.
details: It's the hammering mostly. Steady and rhythmic, but relentless when you're trying to rest.
{npc_name} hasn't complained formally, just grumbles about it to whoever will listen.
reactions: Noise tolerance is personal. What one person ignores drives another to distraction.

statCheck.pass: {npc_name}'s house is directly downwind of the {poi_type} and the sound carries along that corridor without losing strength. It's a placement problem, not a volume problem.
statCheck.fail: It's noisy. Some people mind, some don't.

entries.intro: The pigeons have chosen the {poi_type}'s roof ledge as their favorite perch. A whole row of them.
details: They sit there cooing and leaving droppings on the wall below. The crew shoos them, but they return.
It's a small war of persistence that the pigeons are winning.
reactions: Pigeons and people have been sharing buildings since buildings existed. The pigeons don't ask permission.

statCheck.pass: The roof ledge has a slight overhang that creates a sheltered pocket: warm in the sun, dry in the rain. The pigeons chose the most thermally comfortable perch in the settlement.
statCheck.fail: Pigeons like rooftops. This one is no different.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} runs cleaner since they reorganized the workflow. Less wasted motion.
details: Tools are where they're needed, supplies flow in order, nothing gets handled twice.
The crew says it feels easier even though the output is higher. That's the mark of good design.
reactions: Efficiency looks like laziness to people who don't understand the effort behind it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s well rope is fraying. You can see individual strands peeling off.
details: It still holds, but every bucket feels like a gamble. The crew pulls gently.
Replacing a rope is trivial, but the old one hasn't technically failed yet, so it stays.
reactions: People wait for things to break instead of replacing them when they're weak. Human nature.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} looks best in autumn, I think. The colors of the trees frame it perfectly.
details: The stone walls against red and gold leaves. It looks like something from a story.
By winter the trees are bare and the building looks exposed. But autumn is generous to it.
reactions: Every building has a season that flatters it most. The {poi_type}'s is autumn.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s hinges squeak loud enough to announce every arrival. No sneaking in there.
details: The crew knows who's coming by the sound the door makes. A long squeak means the main door, a short one means the side.
They've been oiled but the squeak comes back within days. The hinges want to talk.
reactions: Squeaky hinges are the cheapest alarm system there is.

statCheck.pass: The hinges are iron set in stone: they can't be shimmed or adjusted without drilling new holes. The squeak comes from slight rust in the pin, and oiling the pin requires lifting the entire door off.
statCheck.fail: The hinges squeak. That's all I know about them.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been operating without interruption through the whole rainy season. Reliable.
details: Other places shut down for flooding or road washouts. This one kept going.
The crew takes it as a point of pride, and rightly so.
reactions: Reliability during bad weather is the real test of a well-run operation.

entries.intro: The path to the {poi_type} washes out every spring. Same section, same ruts.
details: They fill it with gravel and it holds until the next heavy rain. Then back to mud.
The cycle repeats yearly. A permanent fix would be cobblestones, but that's real money.
reactions: Temporary fixes and seasonal problems deserve each other.

statCheck.pass: The path follows a low seam where two soil types meet: clay underneath holds the water while the topsoil washes away. Cobblestones over a gravel bed would drain properly.
statCheck.fail: The path washes out. It's in a low spot.
