entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s stone has a lichen pattern on the north side. Green and gold, like a map.
details: It's been growing so slowly that I only notice the change year to year.
The shapes are irregular and beautiful, if you pause long enough to look.
reactions: Lichen treats stone like canvas. The painting takes decades, but the result is worth it.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew threw together an end-of-week gathering. Invited the whole street.
details: Nothing formal: just drinks and conversation in the yard after the shift ended.
It brought people together who don't normally talk. The kind of thing that builds community.
reactions: Open invitations are generous because they include people who might not think to come.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s scrap pile has been picked over by people looking for usable bits.
details: What's waste to the crew is material to someone else. Offcuts, bent nails, odd lengths of timber.
The crew doesn't mind. One person's scrap is another's project.
reactions: Waste is just potential that hasn't found its next purpose yet.

statCheck.pass: The crew leaves the best offcuts in a separate pile near the road on purpose: it's their quiet way of giving back to people who can't afford new materials.
statCheck.fail: People take scraps. The crew doesn't seem to care.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s roof caught every leaf this autumn. A thick layer piled up in the valleys.
details: The weight of wet leaves is more than you'd think. Water pools underneath them and sits.
The crew is dreading the cleanup, but putting it off makes it worse.
reactions: Autumn is beautiful from the ground. From the roof, it's a maintenance nightmare.

entries.intro: I helped unload a delivery at the {poi_type} yesterday. Heavy work, but good company.
details: The crew works with an easy coordination: everyone knows where things go without asking.
They offered me a drink afterward. I'll take that over payment.
reactions: Shared labor creates a bond that conversation alone can't match.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s second floor has a visible sway when people walk across it. Flexible, not dangerous.
details: New visitors freeze when they feel it move. The crew just walks right through without a thought.
The timbers flex because they're long-span and unsupported in the middle. Built to give, not break.
reactions: Flexible construction lasts longer than rigid construction. The building knows what it's doing.

statCheck.pass: The floor joists are green timber that was never fully seasoned: they retained enough elasticity to flex instead of crack. A happy accident of the original construction.
statCheck.fail: The floor moves. I'm told it's fine.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s morning deliveries arrive by cart, and the horse knows the route by heart.
details: The driver barely holds the reins. The horse stops at the right spot and waits.
Regularity taught the animal the job better than any training could.
reactions: A horse that knows the route is smarter about it than the person holding the reins.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew fixed a problem that had been lingering for months. Finally got around to it.
details: The repair took less than a day. Everyone's asking why they waited so long.
The answer is always the same: other things seemed more urgent. Until they weren't.
reactions: The longest part of most repairs is deciding to start them.

entries.intro: The mud-brick section of the {poi_type} is eroding faster than the stone parts. The difference is visible.
details: The stone walls are clean and sharp. The mud-brick is rounding at the corners and crumbling at the base.
Different materials age at different rates. The building is showing its layers.
reactions: Mixed construction tells you a building was built in stages, each with what was available.

statCheck.pass: The mud-brick was mixed with too much straw and not enough clay: it's weaker than a proper mix and absorbs more water. The builder cut costs on that section and it shows.
statCheck.fail: Mud-brick doesn't last like stone. That's common knowledge.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s water supply is the sweetest in the settlement. People come from across town for it.
details: There's no mineral taste, no grit, just clean water. The crew considers it their best feature.
I fill a jug there whenever I pass by. It makes better tea than what comes from my own well.
reactions: Good water is the foundation of everything. Cooking, drinking, cleaning: it all starts there.

entries.intro: The last big windstorm rearranged everything that wasn't nailed down outside the {poi_type}.
details: Barrels, planks, a handcart: all scattered across the yard and into the road.
The crew spent the first hour of the next morning gathering instead of working.
reactions: Wind doesn't care about your organization. It has its own ideas about where things belong.

entries.intro: I heard the {poi_type}'s annual yield was their best yet. The crew's earned a good rest.
details: Consistent effort over the whole year adds up. No single big moment, just steady progress.
The settlement benefits when the {poi_type} does well. The ripple goes outward.
reactions: A good year is the reward for hundreds of ordinary days done well.

entries.intro: The wood on the {poi_type}'s windowsills has turned silver from years of sun and rain.
details: It's not rot, just weathering. The grain stands out in sharp relief, hard and dry.
New wood would look wrong next to it. The silver is part of the character now.
reactions: Weathered wood has a dignity that fresh lumber can't match. It's earned its look.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew has been taking fewer breaks this week. Pushing to meet a deadline.
details: You can see the pace is quicker, the chatter is less. Focused, not frantic.
Once the deadline passes, I expect they'll slow down and catch their breath.
reactions: Occasional intensity is fine. Constant intensity burns people out.

statCheck.pass: The deadline is for a standing order that renews annually: if they miss it, the contract goes to a competitor in {other_settlement}. The pressure is justified.
statCheck.fail: They're busy. I don't know what's driving it.

entries.intro: The gate to the {poi_type}'s yard doesn't latch properly. It swings open in the wind.
details: I've seen the crew close it three times in a single afternoon, only for the wind to undo their work.
A new latch would solve it, but the old one is 'good enough' by the crew's standard.
reactions: A gate that won't latch is an invitation the wind accepts every time.

statCheck.pass: The latch post has rotted at the base where it meets the ground: the whole post needs replacing, not just the hardware. A new latch on a rotten post will fail the same way.
statCheck.fail: The gate doesn't close properly. Could be a lot of things.

entries.intro: The sunset hits the {poi_type}'s west wall and turns the stone red. For about ten minutes it glows.
details: The effect is brief but striking. Then the shadow climbs the wall and it's grey again.
I've stopped to watch it more than once. Ten minutes of beauty for free.
reactions: Some things are only beautiful because they're brief.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s fuel storage got soaked in the last rain. Wet kindling means slow fires for days.
details: The crew is drying what they can and cursing the tarp that was supposed to cover it.
Wet fuel burns smoky and slow. Everything takes longer until the supply dries out.
reactions: A tarp that fails its one job causes more frustration than no tarp at all.

statCheck.pass: The tarp was an old sail that had already lost its waterproofing: the wax coating had worn through, and nobody tested it before trusting it with the fuel supply.
statCheck.fail: The tarp leaked. Bad luck.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been standing long enough that the settlement was built around it, not the other way around.
details: The road curves to avoid it. The neighboring buildings angle toward it like sunflowers.
Remove it and the layout of this whole area would stop making sense.
reactions: The oldest building in a settlement defines the shape of everything that came after.

statCheck.pass: The building predates the current settlement layout by at least a generation: the road was originally a footpath that detoured around it, and the detour became the permanent route.
statCheck.fail: It's old. Older than the road, probably.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew pulled together after a rough week. Good to see the solidarity.
details: When things go wrong, you learn who your crew really is. This crew showed up.
They covered for each other without being asked. That kind of teamwork can't be faked.
reactions: Adversity reveals character. A good crew shows their best when things are worst.

entries.intro: The stain on the {poi_type}'s wall has been there so long it's become a feature. Nobody remembers what caused it.
details: It's a dark splash, waist-high, shaped like nothing in particular.
New arrivals ask about it, but the crew just shrugs. It was there before any of them started.
reactions: Some things become permanent simply because nobody made the effort to change them.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s {resource_type} quality has been spotty this month. Some batches good, some not.
details: The inconsistency is worse than steady mediocrity. Buyers don't know what to expect.
The crew is troubleshooting, but they haven't pinned down the variable yet.
reactions: Inconsistency erodes trust faster than a single failure ever could.

statCheck.pass: The raw material is coming from two different sources this month and they process differently: when source A and source B get mixed in a batch, the result is unpredictable.
statCheck.fail: Quality has been uneven. I don't know the details.

entries.intro: The rainwater dripping from the {poi_type}'s eaves sounds musical. A steady percussion in the quiet.
details: Each drip hits a different surface: stone, wood, a bucket. Three notes in rotation.
I stood under the overhang and listened for longer than was sensible.
reactions: Rain makes musicians of buildings, whether they intend it or not.

statCheck.pass: Each drip point is a spot where the roofline has a low dip or a nail head that channels water to a single drop: the spacing creates the rhythm because the dips are evenly spaced along the eave.
statCheck.fail: Rain drips off eaves. It sounds nice, I suppose.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} had all its lamps lit at once last night. Every window glowing.
details: It doesn't happen often: usually just one or two lights after dark.
The whole building was alive with light. Like a festival with no festival.
reactions: A fully lit building at night is a generous sight. Wasteful, but generous.

statCheck.pass: They were doing a full inventory count that runs late: every corner needs light for accuracy. It happens once a season and always looks dramatic from outside.
statCheck.fail: All the lights were on. I don't know what they were doing.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s yield of {resource_type} has been generous this season. More than expected.
details: The crew is in high spirits because surplus means security. A buffer against lean months.
Some of the extra has been traded to {other_settlement} at good rates.
reactions: A season of surplus is the memory that carries you through a season of scarcity.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s roof beam is visible from outside where the wall meets the roof. Massive timber.
details: It's been darkened by smoke and age to almost black. Hard as iron now.
That one beam carries most of the roof's weight. Everything above depends on it.
reactions: The things that hold everything together are usually the ones you can't easily see.

statCheck.pass: That beam is old-growth hardwood: a single trunk that was probably twice the age of the settlement when they felled it. They don't grow timber like that anymore.
statCheck.fail: It's a big beam. Looks solid.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s crew keeps a tidy workspace. Tools clean, surfaces swept, everything in its place.
details: You can tell the difference between a crew that cleans at the end of the day and one that doesn't.
This crew cleans. The place opens fresh every morning because it closed clean the night before.
reactions: Cleanliness in a workspace isn't about appearance: it's about respect for the work.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s pest problem is back. Something chewing through stores at night.
details: The crew finds evidence every morning: gnawed sacks, scattered grain, droppings.
They've tried traps and poison and still the damage continues. Persistent creatures.
reactions: Pests treat your livelihood as a buffet. You can't negotiate with hunger.

statCheck.pass: There's a gap in the foundation stones on the north side where something gets in: the crew hasn't found it because it's hidden behind stored supplies. Move the stock and the entry point is obvious.
statCheck.fail: Something's getting in. Nobody knows how.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s courtyard collects all the warmth on a sunny day. It's a natural sun trap.
details: The walls block the wind and the stone reflects the heat. Noticeably warmer than the street outside.
The crew eats out there in good weather. You'd want to as well.
reactions: A sheltered courtyard on a cold day is worth more than a fireplace.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s iron fittings are rusting. Orange streaks running down the stone below each bracket.
details: The rust is cosmetic for now, but it weakens the metal over time.
Replacing ironwork is expensive and oiling it is cheap. Guess which gets done less often.
reactions: Rust is slow and inevitable. You can delay it, but you can't stop it.

statCheck.pass: The brackets were installed without protective coating: a layer of wax or tar on the iron before mounting would have prevented the rust entirely. A simple step that was skipped during construction.
statCheck.fail: Iron rusts. That's just what it does.

entries.intro: The swallows that nest at the {poi_type} every spring arrived early this year.
details: They're already building, swooping in and out with beaks full of mud.
The crew considers them good luck. A building the swallows choose is a building that's sound.
reactions: Animals make better judges of a building's health than any inspector.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s original builder left their mark on the cornerstone. Initials and a date, deeply carved.
details: It's easy to miss unless you know where to look: low on the south corner, half-covered by dirt.
The date puts the building older than I thought. It's aged well.
reactions: A builder who signs their work expects it to last long enough for someone to read it.

statCheck.pass: The initials match a family name that's still known in the settlement: descendants of the original builder still live here, though they've moved on to other trades.
statCheck.fail: There are initials and a date. I don't know whose they are.

entries.intro: The compost heap behind the {poi_type} is steaming in the cold air. Decomposition keeps its own heat.
details: You can see the vapor rising on cold mornings like the pile is breathing.
The crew uses the finished compost for the garden plot. Waste feeding growth.
reactions: A steaming compost pile is proof that nothing is truly wasted if you're patient.

statCheck.pass: The crew turns the pile on a schedule to keep oxygen flowing through the center: that's what maintains the heat and speeds decomposition. An unturned pile would go cold and sour.
statCheck.fail: The compost is hot. I don't know why.

entries.intro: The {poi_type} just completed a trade with {other_settlement} that benefits everyone here.
details: Fair terms, good product, reliable partner. The kind of deal you build a relationship on.
The crew is already talking about the next exchange. Momentum matters in trade.
reactions: One good deal creates the trust that makes the next one easier.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s drains froze solid overnight. Water backed up and spilled into the workspace.
details: The crew spent the first hour of the morning chipping ice and mopping. Not ideal.
Frozen drains are a winter problem that comes back every year, always at the worst time.
reactions: Winter takes every weakness in a building and makes it obvious.

statCheck.pass: The drain runs along the north wall where the sun never reaches in winter: it's the coldest spot on the building and always the first thing to freeze. Rerouting to the south side would buy weeks of frost protection.
statCheck.fail: Drains freeze in winter. That's just the cold.

entries.intro: The {poi_type}'s been standing through worse than anything we've seen in our lifetimes. That's reassuring.
details: The walls have weathered storms, freezes, and dry spells without complaint.
A building that's survived decades of weather has proven itself in ways newer structures haven't.
reactions: Endurance is the most honest form of quality. Time tests everything.
